prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 68-year-old male presents to ED via ambulance with sudden onset of right-sided facial drooping, right arm weakness, and slurred speech that began 90 minutes ago while watching television.
prompts.reasoning: Sudden-onset focal neurological deficits (facial droop, arm weakness, dysarthria) following a vascular distribution demand urgent evaluation for stroke. The FAST criteria are positive. Both ischaemic and haemorrhagic stroke, as well as stroke mimics (hypoglycaemia, Todd's paresis post-seizure, tumour), must be rapidly excluded.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The deficit has remained static since onset — no fluctuation, no progression. No headache, no vomiting, no loss of consciousness, and no preceding aura or sensory symptoms. His wife confirms he was well 2 hours prior.
prompts.reasoning: Static onset without headache or vomiting argues strongly against haemorrhagic stroke. Absence of fluctuation makes TIA less likely. Non-cortical involvement pattern (motor and speech without visual field defect) could suggest MCA territory or deeper structures.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: Known hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and hypercholesterolaemia for 10 years. Smokes 20 cigarettes/day. No history of atrial fibrillation or prior cardiac events. No anticoagulation. Father had a stroke at 65.
prompts.reasoning: Multiple vascular risk factors (hypertension, DM, hypercholesterolaemia, smoking) strongly predispose to large-vessel atherosclerosis and small-vessel arteriolosclerosis. Absence of AF makes cardioembolism less likely, favouring a thrombotic aetiology. Family history suggests heritable vascular risk.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: BP 178/96 mmHg, HR 84 regular, T 36.8°C, BM 5.6 mmol/L. GCS 14/15. Right lower facial droop. Right arm power 2/5, right leg 3/5. Dysarthria. Right plantar upgoing. No neck stiffness. NIHSS score 14.
prompts.reasoning: Regular rhythm makes AF-related embolism less likely. Normal BM excludes hypoglycaemia as a stroke mimic. Right-sided UMN pattern deficit with dysarthria localises to the left hemisphere, likely left MCA territory. NIHSS 14 indicates a moderate-severe stroke — thrombolysis eligibility must be assessed urgently.

prompts.label: CT Head (Non-Contrast)
prompts.text: CT head (non-contrast): hyperdense MCA sign on the left, subtle loss of grey-white differentiation in the left putamen and insular ribbon. No haemorrhage. ASPECTS score 7/10. CT angiography: occlusion of the left M1 MCA segment.
prompts.reasoning: Hyperdense MCA sign indicates thrombus in the vessel. Loss of grey-white differentiation reflects early cytotoxic oedema from ischaemia. No haemorrhage allows thrombolysis. CT angiography identifies the M1 occlusion. ASPECTS ≥6 suggests potentially salvageable penumbra — mechanical thrombectomy should be considered.

prompts.label: Carotid Imaging & Cardiac Workup
prompts.text: CT angiography of the carotid arteries: severe (85%) stenosis at the left internal carotid artery bifurcation due to calcified atherosclerotic plaque. 12-lead ECG: sinus rhythm. 24-hour Holter monitor: no AF detected. Lipid profile: LDL 4.2 mmol/L.
prompts.reasoning: Severe carotid stenosis with in-situ plaque confirms a thrombotic mechanism (atherosclerosis → plaque rupture → local thrombosis at the carotid bifurcation, classically affected). Absence of AF on Holter excludes paroxysmal cardioembolic aetiology. Elevated LDL confirms atherosclerotic risk. Pathologically, the infarcted brain shows pale (bland) infarction — distinguishing it from haemorrhagic embolic infarcts.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 74-year-old female is brought to ED with sudden onset of left-sided weakness affecting her arm and face, and difficulty understanding speech (receptive dysphasia), lasting 2 hours. Onset was at rest.
prompts.reasoning: Sudden focal neurological deficits localising to the right hemisphere demand urgent stroke assessment. Haemorrhagic stroke, mimics, and both thrombotic and embolic ischaemic subtypes must all be considered. Onset at rest is more typical of embolism than thrombosis.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: No headache, no vomiting. She reports two prior brief episodes over the last month — transient left arm tingling lasting 20 minutes each, both resolved completely. No preceding trauma. No fever.
prompts.reasoning: Prior TIA-like episodes in the same vascular territory suggest repeated embolisation from a proximal source. Emboli from cardiac sources (AF, valvular disease, thrombi) can cause multiple, discrete, cortical and subcortical infarcts — explaining the recurrent symptoms.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: Known atrial fibrillation — not on anticoagulation (patient declined). Hypertension, mild mitral regurgitation. No diabetes. Non-smoker. Taking aspirin 100mg/day and amlodipine.
prompts.reasoning: Atrial fibrillation is the most common source of cardiogenic emboli. AF causes stasis of blood in the left atrial appendage, predisposing to thrombus formation — emboli can then dislodge and occlude cerebral vessels. Aspirin alone provides insufficient protection against cardioembolic stroke in AF (anticoagulation is indicated).

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: BP 162/88 mmHg, HR 92 bpm (irregular). GCS 13. Right upper motor neuron facial palsy. Left arm power 1/5, leg 3/5. Receptive dysphasia. Right plantar response equivocal. No papilloedema.
prompts.reasoning: Irregular pulse confirms atrial fibrillation — the likely embolic source. Cortical deficits (receptive dysphasia) localise to the right posterior superior temporal cortex (Wernicke's area), suggesting right MCA territory involvement. UMN pattern weakness confirms a supratentorial lesion.

prompts.label: CT Head & MRI DWI
prompts.text: CT head (non-contrast): no haemorrhage. Loss of grey-white differentiation in the right posterior temporal cortex. MRI DWI (performed within 3 hours): restricted diffusion in a wedge-shaped cortical distribution across the right posterior MCA territory. No haemorrhagic transformation visible.
prompts.reasoning: Wedge-shaped cortical infarction in a vascular territory distribution on MRI DWI is characteristic of embolic stroke — emboli lodge distally at arterial branch points. Haemorrhagic transformation (red/haemorrhagic infarction) can occur in embolic stroke when the embolus fragments and flow is restored, distinguishing embolic from thrombotic infarction pathologically.

prompts.label: Echocardiography & Anticoagulation
prompts.text: Transoesophageal echocardiogram (TOE): spontaneous echo contrast ('smoke') in the left atrial appendage with a 1.2cm mobile thrombus confirmed. No valvular vegetations. CHA₂DS₂-VASc score: 5 — very high thromboembolic risk. IV alteplase administered within thrombolysis window; anticoagulation initiated after 24-hour post-thrombolysis CT.
prompts.reasoning: TOE confirms a left atrial appendage thrombus — the embolic source in AF. Pathologically, embolic infarcts are haemorrhagic (red/haemorrhagic infarct) due to reperfusion of the infarcted territory after embolus fragmentation — distinguishing them from pale thrombotic infarcts. Anticoagulation is the definitive secondary prevention strategy in AF-related cardioembolic stroke.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 65-year-old male presents to ED reporting that 3 hours ago he developed sudden inability to move his right arm and difficulty speaking for approximately 15 minutes. He is now completely back to normal.
prompts.reasoning: Fully resolved focal neurological deficit demands urgent evaluation for TIA — a high-risk harbinger of completed stroke. TIA must be distinguished from stroke (deficit resolved vs persisting), seizure with Todd's paresis, hemiplegic migraine, and hypoglycaemia. This is a medical emergency requiring same-day assessment.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: Symptoms peaked within 1 minute and resolved completely by 15 minutes. No loss of consciousness, no tongue biting, no post-ictal confusion. No preceding headache or visual aura. No similar episodes previously. Neighbour witnessed the episode.
prompts.reasoning: Abrupt onset of focal symptoms with complete resolution within 24 hours, witnessed by another person, is the hallmark of TIA (modern definition: no infarct on imaging). Absence of post-ictal confusion, tongue biting, or loss of consciousness makes focal seizure less likely. Absence of headache makes migraine less likely.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: Hypertension, type 2 diabetes, hypercholesterolaemia, and previous MI 3 years ago. Smokes 10 cigarettes/day. Takes aspirin, metformin, atorvastatin, and ramipril. No AF documented previously.
prompts.reasoning: Heavy vascular risk factor burden (HTN, DM, hyperlipidaemia, prior MI, smoking) confirms high atherosclerotic risk. Prior MI raises the possibility of intracardiac thrombus as an embolic source. The ABCD² score is likely high (age ≥60, BP elevated, clinical features, duration, diabetes) — indicating high 2-day stroke risk requiring urgent inpatient or same-day clinic assessment.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Neurological examination is entirely normal. BP 168/94 mmHg. HR 80 bpm regular. No carotid bruits. No focal neurological deficits. MMSE 30/30. Fundoscopy normal.
prompts.reasoning: Normal neurological examination with complete symptom resolution supports TIA rather than completed stroke. Elevated BP without AF makes large-vessel atherosclerotic disease most likely. Absence of carotid bruit does not exclude significant carotid stenosis — imaging is required.

prompts.label: MRI DWI Brain
prompts.text: MRI brain with DWI sequence: a small (8mm) area of restricted diffusion in the left corona radiata — consistent with a small acute ischaemic lesion not yet visible on CT. Carotid ultrasound: 60% stenosis at the left internal carotid artery bifurcation with mixed echogenicity plaque.
prompts.reasoning: DWI MRI is significantly more sensitive than CT for acute small infarcts — a small DWI-positive lesion confirms ischaemia even when clinically transient. Carotid stenosis identifies a high-risk source for plaque rupture and thromboembolism. Urgent carotid endarterectomy should be considered within 2 weeks for symptomatic >50% stenosis.

prompts.label: Cardiac Workup & Secondary Prevention
prompts.text: 12-lead ECG: sinus rhythm. 48-hour cardiac monitoring: no AF detected. Echocardiogram: mild LV hypertrophy, no intracardiac thrombus or patent foramen ovale. Lipid profile: LDL 3.8 mmol/L. Antiplatelet dual therapy (aspirin + clopidogrel) initiated for 21 days followed by clopidogrel monotherapy. Urgent lipid management intensified.
prompts.reasoning: Cardiogenic embolism excluded (no AF, no thrombus, no PFO). The final mechanism is large-vessel thromboembolism from carotid atherosclerosis. Annual risk of ischaemic stroke after TIA is 3-4%, up to 11% in the first 7 days and 24-29% over 5 years without intervention — emphasising urgency of secondary prevention.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 55-year-old male is admitted to ICU following successful resuscitation from an out-of-hospital cardiac arrest. Bystander CPR was initiated within 3 minutes. Return of spontaneous circulation (ROSC) was achieved after 18 minutes of downtime.
prompts.reasoning: Prolonged cardiac arrest causes global cerebral ischaemia — the entire brain is deprived of oxygen. Neurological outcome depends critically on downtime and time to ROSC. Selective vulnerability means certain regions (watershed zones, hippocampus, Purkinje cells) are most susceptible. Targeted temperature management (TTM) is indicated.

prompts.label: Collateral History
prompts.text: A colleague witnessed him clutch his chest before collapsing. He had been complaining of central chest pain for 1 hour prior to collapse. No prior history of epilepsy, drug use, or structural heart disease known to family.
prompts.reasoning: Chest pain preceding collapse suggests a cardiac cause (likely VF secondary to MI). Global cerebral ischaemia is the anticipated neurological consequence. The prolonged downtime (18 minutes before ROSC) significantly increases the risk of irreversible cortical damage — neurons are irreversibly injured after 5–8 minutes of global ischaemia.

prompts.label: Past Medical History
prompts.text: Hypertension, type 2 diabetes, and dyslipidaemia. Smokes 30 pack-years. No prior neurological history. Posterior spinal fusion surgery 5 years ago — no known coagulopathy.
prompts.reasoning: Heavy vascular risk factor burden raises pre-test probability of underlying coronary artery disease as the cardiac arrest substrate. The combination of cardiac arrest with prolonged downtime essentially confirms global cerebral ischaemia as the mechanism of brain injury.

prompts.label: Physical Examination (Post-ROSC, Day 1)
prompts.text: GCS 6 (E1V1M4). Pupils 4mm bilaterally, sluggishly reactive. Absent oculocephalic reflex. No spontaneous eye movements. Posturing (decorticate) to painful stimuli. Generalised myoclonic jerks. Core temperature 36.2°C maintained on TTM protocol at 34°C.
prompts.reasoning: Depressed consciousness with myoclonic jerks and absent brainstem reflexes post-cardiac arrest is consistent with severe hypoxic-ischaemic encephalopathy. Myoclonus (particularly status myoclonus) is a poor prognostic sign. Neuroprognostication should be deferred until at least 72 hours post-ROSC and after full rewarming.

prompts.label: Biomarkers & EEG
prompts.text: NSE (neuron-specific enolase) at 48 hours: 89 µg/L (significantly elevated; >60 µg/L associated with poor outcome). S100B elevated. Continuous EEG: burst-suppression pattern with generalised myoclonic status epilepticus. Coronary angiogram: proximal LAD occlusion (treated with primary PCI).
prompts.reasoning: Markedly elevated NSE confirms extensive neuronal death. EEG burst-suppression with myoclonic status epilepticus is associated with poor neurological outcome. The underlying cardiac cause (STEMI with LAD occlusion) has been treated — the brain injury is now the focus. Neuroprognostication uses a multimodal approach (clinical, EEG, biomarkers, imaging).

prompts.label: MRI Brain (Day 3)
prompts.text: MRI DWI (Day 3): bilateral symmetric restricted diffusion in the arterial watershed zones (anterior watershed: parasagittal cortex between ACA/MCA; posterior watershed: parieto-occipital cortex between MCA/PCA). Diffuse cortical ribboning — loss of grey-white differentiation indicating laminar necrosis. Hippocampal and cerebellar cortical restricted diffusion.
prompts.reasoning: Bilateral watershed infarcts between the territories of the major cerebral arteries are the hallmark of global ischaemia — these boundary zones are most vulnerable to perfusion pressure drops. Laminar (cortical) necrosis reflects selective neuronal vulnerability (layers III, V, VI). Hippocampal involvement reflects selective vulnerability of CA1 neurons (Sommer sector). Pathologically: laminar necrosis on histology.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 61-year-old male presents with sudden onset of weakness of his right arm and right leg, without any facial involvement, speech disturbance, or sensory loss. The symptoms began 6 hours ago and have remained stable.
prompts.reasoning: Pure motor hemiplegia (arm and leg without face, without cortical signs) is one of the classic lacunar syndromes. However, all stroke types must be excluded. The capsular or pontine location of the lesion must be determined with imaging.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: No headache, no vomiting, no change in consciousness, no speech difficulty, no visual symptoms. The weakness involves the arm and leg equally on the right side. No sensory change. No prior similar episodes.
prompts.reasoning: Pure motor hemiplegia without cortical signs (no aphasia, no visual field defect, no neglect, no higher cognitive deficit) is characteristic of a lacunar infarct in the posterior limb of the internal capsule or pons. Cortical signs would suggest a large-vessel stroke affecting the cortex.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: Longstanding hypertension (treated but often non-compliant, BP often runs >160/90). Type 2 diabetes for 8 years (HbA1c 8.9%). Mild CKD stage 3. No AF. Takes amlodipine and metformin; not on a statin.
prompts.reasoning: Hypertension and diabetes are the principal risk factors for cerebral small vessel disease — the mechanism underlying lacunar infarcts. Poorly controlled BP causes arteriolosclerosis and lipohyalinosis of deep perforating arteries (lenticulostriate, thalamoperforating, basilar perforators), predisposing to lacunar infarction.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: BP 172/100 mmHg, HR 76 bpm regular. Pure motor deficit: right arm power 3/5, right leg power 3/5. Reflexes brisk right > left. Right plantar upgoing. No facial asymmetry. No aphasia. No sensory loss. No neglect. NIHSS 6.
prompts.reasoning: Hemisensory loss absent and face spared — pure motor hemiparesis of arm and leg localises to the posterior limb of the internal capsule (corticospinal fibres) or corona radiata. Absence of all cortical signs confirms the lacunar syndrome. Small perforating vessel disease is the underlying pathology.

prompts.label: CT Head & MRI Brain
prompts.text: CT head: no haemorrhage. Old white matter changes. No obvious acute lesion. MRI DWI: 12mm area of restricted diffusion in the posterior limb of the left internal capsule. FLAIR: multiple chronic lacunae (3–15mm) in the basal ganglia and centrum semiovale — evidence of widespread small vessel disease.
prompts.reasoning: DWI detects acute lacunar infarcts not visible on CT. The posterior limb of the internal capsule contains the corticospinal tract, explaining the pure motor deficit. Multiple chronic lacunae on FLAIR confirm longstanding cerebral small vessel disease. Large DWI lesions in cortical distribution would indicate large-vessel stroke.

prompts.label: Vascular Workup & Risk Factor Management
prompts.text: Carotid Doppler ultrasound: no significant stenosis. 24-hour Holter: no AF. Echocardiogram: normal. Bloods: LDL 3.4 mmol/L, HbA1c 9.1%, eGFR 52 mL/min. Diagnosis: lacunar infarct due to cerebral small vessel disease from hypertension and diabetes. Antiplatelet therapy, statin, and intensive BP and glucose control initiated.
prompts.reasoning: No large-vessel disease or cardiac source confirms the diagnosis of lacunar infarct from small vessel disease. Pathologically, affected perforating arteries show lipohyalinosis (replacement of smooth muscle by fibrous tissue with lipid deposition) and, in severe hypertension, fibrinoid necrosis — leading to occlusion or microaneurysm formation (Charcot-Bouchard). The small infarct cavitates to form a lacune (Latin: lake).

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 42-year-old female is brought to ED with a severe headache, confusion, and two witnessed tonic-clonic seizures over the last 4 hours. Her husband reports she has been having blurred vision since yesterday.
prompts.reasoning: Headache, confusion, seizures, and visual disturbance in an acutely ill patient require urgent investigation. All causes of acute encephalopathy — including SAH, ICH, meningitis, encephalitis, and hypertensive crisis — must be urgently excluded. In a woman of childbearing age, eclampsia must be considered.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: No fever. No neck stiffness. Headache is diffuse and occipital — not sudden-onset thunderclap. She is not pregnant and not on the OCP. No history of drug use. Blurred vision is bilateral and worsening. She has been increasingly drowsy over the past 2 hours.
prompts.reasoning: Diffuse progressive headache (not thunderclap) with bilateral visual disturbance and encephalopathy points away from SAH and meningitis. The bilateral visual involvement suggests a posterior cerebral process (PRES). Progressive encephalopathy with seizures in the context of extreme hypertension fits hypertensive encephalopathy.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: Longstanding hypertension — poorly adherent to medications. Stopped her three antihypertensive medications 3 weeks ago because 'they made her dizzy.' No renal disease known. No diabetes. Family history of hypertension.
prompts.reasoning: Abrupt withdrawal of antihypertensive medications is a common precipitant of hypertensive crisis. Uncontrolled severe hypertension (likely secondary or essential) exceeding the brain's autoregulatory capacity leads to forced cerebral vasodilation, breakdown of the blood-brain barrier, vasogenic oedema, and the syndrome of hypertensive encephalopathy.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: BP 238/148 mmHg (bilateral arms similar). HR 96. T 37.0°C. GCS 12/15. Bilateral papilloedema (Grade III). Flame-shaped retinal haemorrhages on fundoscopy. No meningism. No focal neurological deficits. Urine dipstick: 3+ proteinuria.
prompts.reasoning: BP of 238/148 mmHg exceeds the brain's autoregulatory capacity (~160–180 mmHg MAP). Papilloedema and retinal haemorrhages confirm hypertensive end-organ damage. Proteinuria suggests hypertensive nephropathy. The absence of focal deficits and thunderclap onset argues against haemorrhagic stroke. This is a hypertensive emergency.

prompts.label: Blood Tests & Urinalysis
prompts.text: Creatinine 158 µmol/L (elevated), eGFR 42. LFTs normal. FBC normal. LDH mildly elevated. No thrombocytopaenia (excludes TTP/HUS and HELLP). Urine: proteinuria (2g/24h). BM normal. Pregnancy test negative.
prompts.reasoning: Renal impairment confirms hypertensive end-organ damage. Normal platelet count and LDH level exclude TTP, HUS, and HELLP syndrome (which can also cause hypertensive encephalopathy-like presentation). Negative pregnancy test excludes eclampsia. The combination of findings confirms hypertensive emergency with end-organ damage.

prompts.label: MRI Brain (FLAIR)
prompts.text: MRI brain (FLAIR): bilateral symmetrical T2/FLAIR hyperintensities in the posterior parieto-occipital cortex and subcortical white matter — the hallmark distribution of posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome (PRES). No haemorrhage. No restricted diffusion (confirming vasogenic rather than cytotoxic oedema). Findings reversed on repeat MRI 5 days after BP control.
prompts.reasoning: PRES (posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome) is the radiological manifestation of hypertensive encephalopathy — vasogenic oedema predominantly in the posterior regions due to relative lack of sympathetic innervation. Complete reversal with BP control confirms the vasogenic mechanism and distinguishes from irreversible ischaemic or haemorrhagic injury. Histology shows petechial haemorrhages and fibrinoid necrosis in small vessels.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 72-year-old male is brought to the memory clinic by his wife, who reports that over the past 18 months he has had two distinct episodes of sudden cognitive decline, each following what appeared to be 'mini-strokes'. Between episodes, there has been some partial improvement.
prompts.reasoning: Step-wise cognitive decline following cerebrovascular events is the hallmark of vascular dementia, specifically multi-infarct dementia. However, AD, LBD, and mixed dementia must be considered. The step-wise (not gradual) deterioration pattern with partial inter-episode recovery is a key distinguishing feature.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: After the first episode 18 months ago, he lost the ability to manage finances and developed mild gait difficulties. After the second episode 6 months ago, he developed urinary incontinence and became emotionally labile. He retained relatively intact long-term memory. No personality change or visual hallucinations.
prompts.reasoning: Executive dysfunction (loss of financial management), gait disturbance, and emotional lability are characteristic features of vascular dementia, reflecting frontal-subcortical circuit disruption from white matter small vessel disease. Relative preservation of memory is more consistent with vascular than Alzheimer dementia. Urinary incontinence and gait issues could also suggest NPH.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: Longstanding hypertension, type 2 diabetes, hypercholesterolaemia, and current smoker. Prior ischaemic stroke affecting the right hemisphere 18 months ago. AF diagnosed 2 years ago — on warfarin but INR often subtherapeutic.
prompts.reasoning: Multiple vascular risk factors and prior documented stroke strongly support a vascular aetiology for the dementia. Poorly controlled AF with subtherapeutic anticoagulation (INR) explains ongoing cardioembolic events. The history directly links cerebrovascular events to cognitive deterioration — satisfying the temporal relationship required for vascular dementia diagnosis.

prompts.label: Physical Examination & Cognitive Testing
prompts.text: MMSE 21/30. MoCA 17/30 — deficits predominantly in executive function, attention, and processing speed. Relative preservation of orientation and memory. Broad-based gait with small shuffling steps ('marche à petit pas'). Bilateral brisk reflexes and bilateral extensor plantar responses. Pseudobulbar affect (emotional lability).
prompts.reasoning: The neuropsychological profile of executive dysfunction and attention deficits with relatively preserved memory distinguishes vascular from Alzheimer dementia. Bilateral UMN signs indicate bilateral cortical or subcortical hemisphere damage. Pseudobulbar affect reflects bilateral corticobulbar tract involvement from multiple infarcts.

prompts.label: Blood Tests & Vascular Risk
prompts.text: HbA1c 8.8%, LDL 3.6 mmol/L, INR 1.6 (subtherapeutic). FBC, TFTs, B12, folate: normal (excluding reversible causes). Carotid ultrasound: bilateral plaque, 50% stenosis on the right. ECG: AF confirmed.
prompts.reasoning: Poorly controlled vascular risk factors confirm ongoing cerebrovascular disease risk. Normal metabolic screen excludes reversible dementia causes. The carotid stenosis and AF confirm ongoing thromboembolic risk factors. Addressing these is the principal therapeutic strategy in vascular dementia.

prompts.label: MRI Brain
prompts.text: MRI brain: multiple cortical and subcortical infarcts in both hemispheres, including an old right MCA territory infarct and multiple small lacunar infarcts in the basal ganglia. Extensive white matter hyperintensities (Fazekas grade 3). Moderate generalised cortical atrophy. Pathologically: bilateral infarcts in varying stages with gliosis and cystic cavitation.
prompts.reasoning: Multiple cortical and lacunar infarcts on MRI confirm multi-infarct dementia — cumulative small and large vessel disease. Extensive periventricular and deep white matter changes represent diffuse small vessel ischaemic injury (leukoaraiosis). Pathologically: infarcts in varying stages, gliosis, myelin loss in white matter, and vessel wall changes (lipohyalinosis, atherosclerosis) — the morphological substrate of vascular dementia.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 58-year-old male is brought to ED by paramedics with sudden onset severe headache, left-sided weakness, and progressive decline in consciousness over 30 minutes. He collapsed at work.
prompts.reasoning: Sudden-onset focal deficit with rapidly progressive headache and declining consciousness strongly suggests haemorrhagic stroke. The combination of headache, focal deficits, and deteriorating GCS is more common in ICH than ischaemic stroke. SAH (thunderclap headache) and ischaemic stroke must also be excluded.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The headache was severe and sudden but not thunderclap — it peaked over 2 minutes. Left hemiplegia developed before the headache. He has vomited twice. GCS dropped from 14 to 10 over 30 minutes. No anticoagulant use, no trauma, no preceding aura.
prompts.reasoning: Headache accompanying focal deficits, vomiting, and rapid deterioration indicates raised ICP from a space-occupying lesion — most likely haematoma with surrounding oedema. The non-thunderclap headache (peaking over minutes, not seconds) and focal deficits make ICH more likely than SAH. Rapid deterioration from expanding haematoma is a neurosurgical emergency.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: Severe hypertension for 15 years — poorly controlled (BP often >180/110 despite medications). No anticoagulant use. No prior stroke or TIA. No family history of bleeding disorders or vascular malformations. Heavy alcohol use. Takes amlodipine and lisinopril; often non-compliant.
prompts.reasoning: Longstanding severe hypertension is the single most important risk factor for hypertensive ICH. Chronically elevated BP causes Charcot-Bouchard microaneurysms (miliary aneurysms) on small perforating arteries — predominantly in the basal ganglia, thalamus, pons, and cerebellum. Rupture of these microaneurysms causes intraparenchymal haemorrhage.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: BP 212/124 mmHg, HR 88 irregular. GCS 10 (E2V3M5). Left hemiplegia (arm and leg 0/5). Left hemisensory loss. Eyes deviated to the right (towards the lesion). Bilateral papilloedema. Right pupil 5mm sluggishly reactive — early uncal herniation.
prompts.reasoning: Contralateral hemiplegia with ipsilateral gaze deviation ('eyes looking at the lesion') localises to the right hemisphere. Papilloedema indicates raised ICP. Unilateral pupillary dilatation suggests early uncal herniation from a large right hemispheric haematoma compressing the ipsilateral CN III — a neurosurgical emergency.

prompts.label: CT Head (Non-Contrast) — Urgent
prompts.text: CT head (non-contrast): large (45mL) hyperdense (white) intraparenchymal haematoma in the right basal ganglia (putamen/internal capsule) with surrounding hypodense oedema. 8mm midline shift. Intraventricular extension into the right lateral ventricle. No subarachnoid blood. No underlying structural lesion.
prompts.reasoning: Hyperdense intraparenchymal haematoma in the basal ganglia/putamen is the classic location for hypertensive ICH (supplied by lenticulostriate arteries with Charcot-Bouchard microaneurysms). CT is the first-line investigation — immediately distinguishes haemorrhagic from ischaemic stroke, precludes thrombolysis, and guides management. Intraventricular extension worsens prognosis.

prompts.label: Coagulation, Vascular Imaging & Management
prompts.text: Coagulation screen: normal (PT, APTT, INR, platelets). CT angiography: no AVM, no aneurysm, no underlying structural abnormality — confirming primary hypertensive aetiology. Haematoma volume predicts 30-day mortality (ICH score 3 = ~70% mortality). BP target: <140 mmHg systolic maintained with IV labetalol.
prompts.reasoning: Normal coagulation excludes anticoagulation-related or coagulopathic haemorrhage. No vascular malformation on CTA confirms hypertensive aetiology — the classic histological finding is Charcot-Bouchard microaneurysms on lenticulostriate and other perforating arteries. The basal ganglia/putamen location is pathognomonic of hypertensive ICH. Early intensive BP reduction (<140 mmHg) aims to limit haematoma expansion.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 45-year-old female presents to ED with a sudden, severe headache she describes as 'the worst headache of her life', reaching maximal intensity within seconds of onset.
prompts.reasoning: A 'thunderclap' headache — reaching peak intensity within seconds — is a neurological red flag that must be investigated urgently for SAH. At this early stage, migraine, hypertensive crisis, CVST, and meningitis remain on the differential.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: She developed neck stiffness and photophobia shortly after onset. There is no fever, no preceding aura, and no history of similar headaches. Her husband found her slumped in the bathroom.
prompts.reasoning: Meningism (neck stiffness + photophobia) with thunderclap onset and no fever narrows the differential significantly toward SAH. Meningitis becomes less likely without fever. CVST can also mimic this presentation.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: She has poorly controlled hypertension and smokes 15 cigarettes/day. Her father died of a 'brain bleed' in his 50s. She takes no anticoagulants.
prompts.reasoning: Hypertension and smoking are risk factors for both SAH and hypertensive ICH. A family history of intracranial haemorrhage raises suspicion for an inherited vascular abnormality (e.g. berry aneurysm, ADPCKD). CVST is less likely without prothrombotic risk factors.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: BP 188/102 mmHg, HR 92, T 37.2°C, GCS 14/15. Marked neck stiffness. Kernig's sign positive. No papilloedema. No focal neurological deficits.
prompts.reasoning: Positive Kernig's sign and neck stiffness confirm meningeal irritation. Absence of fever and the thunderclap onset favour SAH over meningitis. No papilloedema means LP can safely proceed after CT.

prompts.label: CT Head (Non-Contrast)
prompts.text: Hyperdense (white) blood in the basal cisterns, Sylvian fissures, and interhemispheric fissure. No hydrocephalus. CT sensitivity for SAH is ~98% within the first 6 hours.
prompts.reasoning: Hyperdense blood in the subarachnoid space on non-contrast CT confirms SAH. CT is highly sensitive in the first 6 hours; sensitivity drops significantly beyond 24 hours. Identification of the underlying cause with angiography is the next step.

prompts.label: CT Angiography & Lumbar Puncture
prompts.text: CT angiography: 7mm saccular aneurysm at the left posterior communicating artery origin. LP (performed as CT was done >6h post-ictus): xanthochromia on spectrophotometry, confirming blood breakdown products in the CSF.
prompts.reasoning: Xanthochromia on LP (CSF yellowing from haemoglobin breakdown) confirms SAH when CT is negative or equivocal — it persists for up to 2 weeks. CT angiography identifies the underlying berry aneurysm at the Circle of Willis, the most common cause of non-traumatic SAH.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 38-year-old female is referred to neurology after an MRI performed for headaches incidentally identified a vascular abnormality at the Circle of Willis. She reports occasional unilateral headaches and no neurological symptoms.
prompts.reasoning: Incidental intracranial vascular abnormality requires characterisation. The differential includes aneurysm, AVM, and other vascular lesions. Asymptomatic aneurysms are found in 2–3% of the population. Management depends on size, location, morphology, and patient factors.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: She has never had a sudden severe ('thunderclap') headache. No history of diplopia or visual disturbance. The current headaches are mild, bilateral, and pressure-like — likely tension headaches. No neurological deficits at any time.
prompts.reasoning: Absence of thunderclap headache indicates no prior rupture (SAH). No diplopia means no current CN III compression from an enlarging posterior communicating artery aneurysm. The aneurysm appears to be an incidental finding — prevalence-based management decisions are required.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Family History
prompts.text: Hypertension diagnosed 2 years ago (BP 148/90 mmHg on medication). Non-smoker. Her brother was diagnosed with autosomal dominant polycystic kidney disease (ADPCKD) last year. She has not yet been screened for ADPCKD.
prompts.reasoning: ADPCKD is associated with a 5–10× increased risk of saccular (berry) aneurysms. Family history of ADPCKD should prompt screening for intracranial aneurysms. Other associations include NF1, Marfan syndrome, Ehlers-Danlos syndrome, and aortic coarctation. Hypertension and smoking further increase rupture risk.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: BP 152/94 mmHg. Neurological examination entirely normal. No palpable renal masses. Fundoscopy normal — no papilloedema. No cranial nerve palsy.
prompts.reasoning: Normal neurological examination confirms an unruptured, asymptomatic aneurysm. Renal examination is important given the family history of ADPCKD. BP control is a key modifiable risk factor for aneurysm growth and rupture.

prompts.label: CT Angiography & MRI Characterisation
prompts.text: CT angiography and MRI time-of-flight MRA: 6mm saccular aneurysm at the right internal carotid artery-posterior communicating artery (ICA-PCoA) junction. Smooth dome, narrow neck (neck:dome ratio favourable). No surrounding enhancement. Renal ultrasound ordered for ADPCKD screening.
prompts.reasoning: ICA-PCoA is a classic location for berry aneurysms at the Circle of Willis (other common sites: ACoA, MCA bifurcation, basilar apex). The saccular morphology reflects a focal defect in the media and internal elastic lamina at arterial bifurcation points — the pathological basis of berry aneurysm formation. Size (6mm) and location inform rupture risk.

prompts.label: Histopathology & Management Decision
prompts.text: Renal ultrasound: multiple bilateral renal cysts — consistent with ADPCKD. Genetics referral arranged. Neurovascular MDT: PHASES score indicates 2.5% 5-year rupture risk. Recommendation: endovascular coiling versus watchful waiting with 6-monthly surveillance MRA. Histologically, the aneurysm wall shows absence of the internal elastic lamina and muscular media, with only a thin fibrous adventitia.
prompts.reasoning: Berry aneurysms arise at sites of arterial branching where haemodynamic stress causes focal degeneration of the internal elastic lamina and media — the developmental defect in the vessel wall. The dome is composed of fibrous adventitia alone, lacking normal vascular wall components. ADPCKD confirms the predisposing connective tissue abnormality. The PHASES score guides intervention vs surveillance decisions.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 28-year-old male presents with a first-ever generalised tonic-clonic seizure lasting 3 minutes. He has no prior history of epilepsy. He reports he has occasionally noticed pulsatile tinnitus over the past year.
prompts.reasoning: New-onset seizure in a young adult without a clear provoking factor or family history requires neuroimaging to exclude a structural cause. Pulsatile tinnitus (heard by the patient — subjective) may indicate abnormal vascular flow. AVMs, tumours, and cavernomas must be excluded.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The seizure was witnessed by his flatmate who confirms a generalised tonic-clonic pattern with post-ictal drowsiness. No prodrome. No history of alcohol or drug use. No fever. No head trauma. Occasional mild headaches — different in character from his usual tension headaches.
prompts.reasoning: In a young adult, a focal structural lesion precipitating seizures must be urgently excluded. The pulsatile tinnitus and progressive headaches suggest an abnormal vascular structure with high-flow arteriovenous shunting. AVMs can present with seizures (25%), haemorrhage (50%), or incidentally.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Family History
prompts.text: No significant medical history. No anticoagulants. No family history of vascular malformations, hereditary haemorrhagic telangiectasia (HHT/Osler-Weber-Rendu), or intracranial abnormalities. Non-smoker, minimal alcohol.
prompts.reasoning: Most AVMs are sporadic congenital malformations — not inherited. However, familial AVMs occur in HHT (autosomal dominant). Absence of family history does not exclude AVM. No anticoagulation means any haemorrhage would not be drug-related.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: GCS 15. BP 128/76 mmHg. Neurological examination normal — no focal deficits. No cranial bruits on auscultation of the skull (present in <25% of AVMs). Fundoscopy normal. MoCA 28/30 (mild attention deficit, likely post-ictal).
prompts.reasoning: Normal neurological examination is compatible with an unruptured AVM. Cranial bruits (auscultated over orbits or skull) are not reliably present. The absence of focal deficits does not exclude an AVM — symptoms depend on location, size, and whether haemorrhage has occurred.

prompts.label: MRI Brain
prompts.text: MRI brain (T2 and FLAIR): compact nidus of serpiginous flow voids in the left temporal lobe, 3cm diameter, with associated hemosiderin staining indicating prior microhaemorrhages. No mass effect, no surrounding oedema. DWI: no acute ischaemia. Surrounding gliosis.
prompts.reasoning: Flow voids on MRI represent the high-velocity arteriovenous shunting through the AVM nidus (tangle of abnormal vessels without an intervening capillary bed). Hemosiderin staining indicates prior microhaemorrhage. The temporal location explains the seizure. MRA/angiography is required for treatment planning.

prompts.label: Catheter Angiography
prompts.text: Digital subtraction angiography (DSA — gold standard): nidus fed by branches of the left MCA with early venous drainage into the superficial Sylvian vein. Spetzler-Martin grade II (small, eloquent cortex, superficial drainage). Annual haemorrhage risk ~2–4%. Multidisciplinary decision: stereotactic radiosurgery (Gamma Knife) planned.
prompts.reasoning: DSA is the gold standard for AVM characterisation — defining the nidus, feeding arteries, and draining veins. AVMs are congenital abnormal connections between arteries and veins without a capillary bed — the nidus is a tangle of dysplastic vessels with thin walls prone to rupture. Histologically: abnormal vessels with variable wall thickness lacking normal smooth muscle layers. The Spetzler-Martin grade guides treatment risk.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 79-year-old female is admitted with sudden onset right arm weakness and headache lasting 20 minutes, which has now resolved. She has had three similar brief episodes over the past 6 months.
prompts.reasoning: Recurrent transient focal neurological episodes (TFNEs) in an elderly patient can represent TIAs, cortical spreading depression from amyloid angiopathy, or 'amyloid spells' (cortical transient focal neurological episodes from cortical microhaemorrhages). The recurrent nature without clear vascular territory specificity raises suspicion for cerebral amyloid angiopathy.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The episodes are stereotyped — always affecting the right arm with positive sensory symptoms (tingling spreading proximally to distally) rather than pure weakness. Duration 10–20 minutes each. No vascular risk factors. She has noticed increasing memory difficulties over the past 2 years.
prompts.reasoning: Stereotyped positive sensory symptoms spreading in a 'march' pattern are more consistent with focal cortical seizure activity or cortical spreading depression than TIA (which typically causes negative symptoms — loss of function). In an elderly patient with cognitive decline, these features raise strong suspicion for cerebral amyloid angiopathy with cortical superficial siderosis.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: No history of hypertension or diabetes. Mild cognitive decline over 2 years — previously diagnosed as 'probable early AD'. ApoE genotype testing not previously performed. On aspirin 100mg/day for cardiovascular prophylaxis.
prompts.reasoning: CAA predominantly affects normotensive elderly individuals — distinguishing it from hypertensive ICH. ApoE ε4 allele (chromosome 19) increases risk of CAA as well as Alzheimer disease — explaining the co-existing cognitive decline. The two conditions frequently co-exist. Aspirin use may increase haemorrhagic risk in CAA.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: BP 128/78 mmHg. Neurological examination currently normal — between episodes. MMSE 24/30 (short-term memory deficit). No focal deficits. Normal fundoscopy. No papilloedema.
prompts.reasoning: Normal BP is an important finding — this is not hypertensive small vessel disease. MMSE reduction reflects underlying cognitive decline, consistent with co-existing AD pathology. The transient nature of deficits, now resolved, suggests cortical dysfunction related to microhaemorrhage rather than completed infarction.

prompts.label: MRI Brain — Standard Sequences
prompts.text: MRI brain (T2/FLAIR): multiple cortical/subcortical chronic microhaemorrhages and superficial siderosis in the posterior parietal and occipital regions bilaterally. Moderate white matter change. Lobar (not basal ganglia) distribution is noted.
prompts.reasoning: Lobar distribution of microhaemorrhages (posterior > anterior; cortical and subcortical) is characteristic of CAA — amyloid deposits predominantly in cortical and leptomeningeal vessels. Contrast with hypertensive ICH, which is deep (basal ganglia, thalamus, pons). The Boston Criteria require the lobar distribution pattern on MRI.

prompts.label: MRI — Gradient Echo (GRE/SWI) Sequence
prompts.text: MRI GRE/SWI (susceptibility-weighted imaging): multiple cortical microhaemorrhages (blooming artifacts) predominantly in posterior cortical regions — count exceeds 10. Linear superficial siderosis along cortical sulci. ApoE genotyping: ApoE ε4/ε4 homozygote. Boston Criteria: 'probable CAA' met.
prompts.reasoning: SWI/GRE is exquisitely sensitive to haemosiderin — making microhaemorrhages appear as dark 'blooms'. Multiple cortical microhaemorrhages in lobar distribution plus superficial siderosis meets the Boston Criteria for probable CAA without histological confirmation. Pathologically: β-amyloid deposits in the walls of cortical and leptomeningeal arterioles, causing vessel wall weakening and predisposing to lobar haemorrhages.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: An 82-year-old female is brought to ED by ambulance after a witnessed fall from standing height, striking her head on a tiled floor. She lost consciousness briefly at the scene and her GCS on arrival is 10/15.
prompts.reasoning: Head trauma with loss of consciousness and altered GCS demands urgent CT imaging to exclude intracranial haemorrhage. In the elderly, cerebral atrophy stretches bridging veins, increasing SDH risk even from minor trauma. EDH is less common in elderly patients (requires arterial injury). Stroke causing a fall must also be excluded.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: She was initially speaking after the fall (GCS 13) but has deteriorated progressively over 40 minutes to GCS 10. No lucid interval in the classic sense. She is on warfarin for AF. Her warfarin INR yesterday was 3.2.
prompts.reasoning: Progressive neurological deterioration after head trauma, without a classic lucid interval, is more consistent with acute SDH than EDH (which classically has a lucid interval). Therapeutic warfarin (INR 3.2) dramatically increases haematoma expansion risk and worsens outcomes. Urgent INR reversal is critical alongside imaging.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: Atrial fibrillation (on warfarin), hypertension, and mild vascular dementia. Lives alone in sheltered housing. Several prior falls noted in her medical records. No known coagulopathy beyond warfarin anticoagulation.
prompts.reasoning: Warfarin anticoagulation for AF is a major risk factor for traumatic intracranial haemorrhage. Vascular dementia causes cerebral atrophy — stretching the bridging veins and increasing SDH risk from relatively minor head impacts. Elderly age with multiple fall risk factors makes head injury with SDH the most likely diagnosis.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: GCS 10/15 (E2V3M5). BP 166/92 mmHg, HR 88 irregular, T 36.9°C. Right-sided hemiparesis (arm 2/5, leg 3/5). Left pupil 5mm, sluggishly reactive; right pupil 3mm reactive — contralateral pupil dilatation suggests left-sided mass effect (CN III compression from left uncal herniation).
prompts.reasoning: Left-sided haematoma causing right hemiplegia (crossed signs: lesion contralateral to weakness) plus left pupil dilatation (ipsilateral CN III compression from uncal herniation) localises to a left hemisphere subdural collection. Progressive deterioration with Cushing's triad risk — this is a neurosurgical emergency requiring immediate CT and surgical consideration.

prompts.label: CT Head (Non-Contrast)
prompts.text: CT head: hyperdense (white) crescent-shaped collection over the left cerebral convexity, crossing suture lines, with underlying brain compression. 12mm maximal thickness, 8mm midline shift. No underlying contusion. Concurrent small SAH in the left sylvian fissure. INR reversal with IV Vitamin K and 4-factor PCC (prothrombin complex concentrate) initiated immediately.
prompts.reasoning: A crescent-shaped hyperdense collection that follows the contour of the brain and crosses suture lines is the hallmark of acute SDH — distinguishing it from EDH (biconvex lens-shaped, bounded by sutures). SDH results from tearing of bridging veins (draining cortex to dural sinuses). In the elderly, cerebral atrophy stretches these veins — even minor trauma can rupture them. Urgent INR reversal minimises haematoma expansion.

prompts.label: Neurosurgical Management
prompts.text: After INR reversal (target <1.5 with PCC), emergency burr hole craniotomy performed for haematoma evacuation. Intraoperative findings: dark venous blood clot beneath the dura with torn bridging vein identified. Post-operative CT: haematoma evacuated, midline shift resolved. GCS improved to 13 post-operatively.
prompts.reasoning: Acute SDH with midline shift >5mm and neurological deterioration is a surgical emergency. The dark venous blood (vs bright arterial blood in EDH) and torn bridging vein confirm the SDH mechanism. Unlike chronic SDH, which forms gradually over weeks, acute SDH from bridging vein rupture requires urgent evacuation. High mortality (30–60%) even with surgery, especially in the elderly with anticoagulation.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 74-year-old male is brought to the GP by his daughter, who has noticed increasing confusion, slowed thinking, and an unsteady gait developing over the past 4 weeks. He has also had mild headaches.
prompts.reasoning: Slowly progressive cognitive decline with gait disturbance in an elderly male has a broad differential including dementia subtypes, NPH, and space-occupying lesions including chronic SDH. A crucially important and treatable diagnosis, chronic SDH can mimic dementia and should always be excluded with neuroimaging.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: On direct questioning, he recalls a minor fall 6 weeks ago — he bumped his head on a car door but did not lose consciousness. He dismissed it as 'just a knock'. He is on aspirin for secondary prevention of an MI 3 years ago. No fever, no meningism, no visual symptoms.
prompts.reasoning: A 'trivial' head injury 6 weeks prior that was dismissed by the patient is a classic precipitating event for chronic SDH. The insidious onset 4–8 weeks after minor trauma, with progressive cognitive decline and gait disturbance, is characteristic. Aspirin use may promote haematoma expansion by impairing platelet function.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: Lives alone since wife died. Prior MI (on aspirin). Mild hypertension. Moderate alcohol use (2–3 units/day). Cerebral atrophy noted on a CT scan performed 5 years ago. No anticoagulants. No history of bleeding disorders.
prompts.reasoning: Cerebral atrophy (from age, alcohol, prior cerebrovascular disease) stretches the bridging veins — a key risk factor for chronic SDH from minor trauma. Alcohol also impairs haemostasis. Living alone means the insidious cognitive changes may not be noticed until significantly advanced. The combination of atrophy, aspirin, and minor trauma is the classic chronic SDH risk profile.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: GCS 14/15. MMSE 23/30 (orientation and attention deficits). Mild right upper limb drift. Brisk reflexes right side, right plantar equivocal. Broad-based gait. BP 158/88 mmHg. No papilloedema. No meningism.
prompts.reasoning: Mild right UMN signs with gait disturbance and cognitive decline localises to a left hemisphere space-occupying lesion. The absence of papilloedema does not exclude a subdural collection — raised ICP may not be severe. The gradual onset distinguishes this from acute SDH. This pattern of confusion + focal signs + gait disturbance in an elderly person requires urgent CT.

prompts.label: CT Head (Non-Contrast)
prompts.text: CT head: bilateral iso-to-hypodense crescent-shaped collections over both cerebral convexities, right > left. Right-sided collection measures 18mm with 7mm midline shift. Isodensity of collection indicates blood products 3–4 weeks old (haemoglobin partially broken down). Underlying cortical sulci not visible — compressed.
prompts.reasoning: Chronic SDH appears isodense or hypodense on CT (compared to acute SDH which is hyperdense) as haemoglobin degrades over 2–4 weeks. The crescent-shaped collection crossing suture lines confirms the subdural space. Bilateral collections are common in chronic SDH. The isodense appearance can be mistaken for normal brain — a common diagnostic pitfall. MRI is more sensitive.

prompts.label: MRI Brain & Management
prompts.text: MRI brain (T1 and T2): bilateral subdural collections with mixed signal intensity (heterogeneous content reflecting re-bleeds within a chronic collection). Burr-hole drainage performed under local anaesthesia. Intraoperative fluid: motor oil-coloured, xanthochromic fluid confirming chronic SDH. Post-operative cognitive improvement over 48 hours.
prompts.reasoning: Chronic SDH fluid is typically dark brown/'motor oil'-coloured — old blood products form a liquefied collection surrounded by a membrane (outer and inner membranes formed from dura and arachnoid). The pathological process involves initial bridging vein rupture → small haematoma → organisation and membrane formation → re-bleeds from fragile membrane vessels → expansion. The excellent response to burr-hole drainage confirms the diagnosis and illustrates the importance of not missing this treatable condition.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 22-year-old male is brought to ED after being struck on the right side of his head by a cricket bat. He was briefly unconscious for approximately 2 minutes, then regained full consciousness and was talking normally.
prompts.reasoning: Brief LOC followed by apparent full recovery (lucid interval) after head trauma is the classic presentation of EDH. However, concussion, cerebral contusion, and SDH must also be considered at this stage.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: Over the 45 minutes since injury, he has developed a rapidly worsening headache, vomited twice, and is becoming increasingly confused and agitated. His GCS has dropped from 15 to 12.
prompts.reasoning: Lucid interval followed by rapid neurological deterioration is the hallmark of EDH — an expanding arterial bleed under pressure. SDH is also possible but the classic lucid interval pattern is more characteristic of EDH. This is a neurosurgical emergency.

prompts.label: Past Medical History
prompts.text: He is otherwise healthy with no bleeding disorders and takes no medications including anticoagulants. He was wearing a helmet that came off on impact. No alcohol or drugs.
prompts.reasoning: No anticoagulants or coagulopathy — the bleed is purely traumatic. The mechanism (direct blow to the temporoparietal region) is consistent with middle meningeal artery territory injury.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: GCS 11/15. BP 154/78 mmHg, HR 52 bpm (bradycardia) — Cushing's response. Pupils: right 6mm dilated and unresponsive — ipsilateral CN III compression from uncal herniation. Left-sided hemiparesis (arm and leg 3/5). Right-sided scalp haematoma.
prompts.reasoning: Cushing's triad (hypertension, bradycardia, irregular respirations) indicates critically raised ICP from the expanding haematoma. Ipsilateral pupillary dilatation (right CN III compressed by right uncus herniating over tentorium) and contralateral hemiparesis confirms a large right-sided mass lesion. This is a life-threatening emergency requiring immediate CT and neurosurgery.

prompts.label: CT Head (Non-Contrast)
prompts.text: CT head (non-contrast): biconvex (lens-shaped) hyperdense collection at the right temporoparietal region, with a swirl sign indicating active bleeding. 3cm thickness, 12mm midline shift. Temporal bone fracture visible crossing the middle meningeal groove. Does not cross the coronal suture.
prompts.reasoning: Biconvex lens-shaped hyperdense collection bounded by sutures (not crossing coronal suture) is the hallmark of EDH — distinguishing it from SDH (crescent-shaped, crosses suture lines). The temporal bone fracture crossing the middle meningeal artery groove confirms arterial origin. EDH collects in the potential space between the skull and dura — stripped by the expanding arterial bleed.

prompts.label: Emergency Craniotomy & Outcome
prompts.text: Emergency right temporal craniotomy within 90 minutes: middle meningeal artery lacerated at the fracture site confirmed as the bleeding source. 60mL of arterial haematoma evacuated. Dura tacked to bone. Post-operative GCS 14/15 within 24 hours — excellent outcome due to early surgery.
prompts.reasoning: EDH is caused by middle meningeal artery (95%) or dural sinus (5%) rupture, typically from temporal bone fracture. The dura is stripped from the inner table by arterial pressure — forming the biconvex haematoma. Mortality is <5% with timely surgery (<4 hours from deterioration) but approaches 100% untreated. Young patients have tight dura–skull adherence, making the lucid interval possible before critical ICP is reached.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 58-year-old male presents with a 3-month history of progressive morning headache, worst on bending forward or coughing, associated with intermittent nausea. He initially attributed it to stress.
prompts.reasoning: Morning headache worsened by Valsalva manoeuvres is a classic feature of raised intracranial pressure from a space-occupying lesion. All SOLs must be considered — primary and secondary brain tumours, abscess, and subdural collections. Migraine is less likely given the progressive, positional pattern.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: Three weeks ago he had a witnessed generalised tonic-clonic seizure. Over 6 weeks, his family noticed progressive left arm weakness and difficulty finding words (expressive dysphasia).
prompts.reasoning: New-onset seizure with progressive focal deficits localises to the right frontal/parietal cortex. This pattern strongly suggests a progressive SOL. Primary or secondary brain tumour and cerebral abscess must be urgently excluded.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: Ex-smoker (20 pack-years, quit 5 years ago). No known prior malignancy, no immunosuppression, no recent febrile illness, no travel history. Takes amlodipine for hypertension.
prompts.reasoning: No known primary malignancy makes cerebral metastases less likely, but still possible (occult lung). Absence of fever and immunosuppression makes abscess less likely. A primary brain tumour is now the leading differential.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: GCS 14/15. Bilateral papilloedema on fundoscopy. Left arm power 3/5 (pronator drift). Expressive dysphasia. Brisk deep tendon reflexes left side. Left plantar upgoing (Babinski positive).
prompts.reasoning: Papilloedema confirms raised ICP. Progressive UMN pattern weakness with dysphasia localises to the right hemisphere. Abscess becomes less likely without fever. The picture strongly suggests a large right-sided intracranial mass lesion.

prompts.label: Bloods & CT Staging
prompts.text: Bloods: normal FBC, CRP, LFTs. CT chest/abdomen/pelvis: no primary malignancy identified. CT head: large heterogeneous mass in the right frontal lobe with surrounding oedema, mass effect, and midline shift.
prompts.reasoning: No systemic primary malignancy on staging CT makes metastasis less likely. The heterogeneous frontal mass with oedema and midline shift is most consistent with a high-grade primary brain tumour. MRI with contrast is needed for characterisation.

prompts.label: MRI Brain & Stereotactic Biopsy
prompts.text: MRI brain with gadolinium: irregular ring-enhancing mass (6cm) in the right frontal lobe with central necrosis, vasogenic oedema, and 8mm midline shift. Biopsy: high cellular density, nuclear pleomorphism, microvascular proliferation, and palisading necrosis. IDH-wildtype. MGMT unmethylated.
prompts.reasoning: Ring-enhancing lesion with central necrosis on MRI is the hallmark of glioblastoma (WHO Grade IV). Palisading necrosis and microvascular proliferation on histology are the diagnostic hallmarks. IDH-wildtype with unmethylated MGMT indicates poor prognosis (median survival <15 months). Tumour cells infiltrate widely beyond the visible enhancing margin.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: An 8-year-old boy is brought to paediatric ED with a 6-week history of progressive early morning vomiting, unsteadiness, and clumsiness. His parents report he seems to prefer sitting rather than walking.
prompts.reasoning: Progressive cerebellar and raised ICP symptoms in a child must prompt urgent evaluation for a posterior fossa space-occupying lesion. The most common childhood posterior fossa tumours are pilocytic astrocytoma, medulloblastoma, and ependymoma — each with distinct clinical features.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: Morning vomiting (worse in the morning, before getting out of bed) for 6 weeks. Ataxia affecting both limbs but worse on the right. No diplopia. No headache at rest — only positional discomfort. No weight loss, no fever, no developmental regression.
prompts.reasoning: Morning vomiting worse before getting up is a classic feature of raised ICP (from posterior fossa mass compressing the 4th ventricle and causing hydrocephalus). Limb ataxia (cerebellar) without brainstem signs makes brainstem glioma less likely. The indolent 6-week course is more consistent with pilocytic astrocytoma than the more aggressive medulloblastoma.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Developmental History
prompts.text: Normal birth history and development. No family history of NF1 or other phakomatoses. No prior radiotherapy or chemotherapy. No history of immunosuppression. Fully vaccinated.
prompts.reasoning: Pilocytic astrocytoma is the most common brain tumour in childhood and frequently occurs in the cerebellum. It is associated with NF1 (particularly when in the optic pathway). Absence of NF1 features does not exclude it. Medulloblastoma tends to present more acutely and arises from the vermis (causing truncal ataxia).

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: GCS 15. Bilateral papilloedema. Nystagmus on right lateral gaze. Right limb past-pointing on finger-nose test. Dysdiadochokinesia right > left. Gait ataxia — tandem walking impossible. No cranial nerve palsies. Head circumference normal.
prompts.reasoning: Cerebellar signs (ipsilateral limb ataxia, nystagmus, dysmetria, dysdiadochokinesia) with raised ICP (papilloedema) confirm a right cerebellar hemisphere lesion causing hydrocephalus. The ipsilateral (not truncal/axial) cerebellar signs suggest a cerebellar hemisphere tumour — characteristic of pilocytic astrocytoma — rather than vermis involvement (medulloblastoma).

prompts.label: MRI Brain
prompts.text: MRI brain with gadolinium: well-circumscribed cystic lesion in the right cerebellar hemisphere with an avidly enhancing mural nodule — the classic 'cyst with mural nodule' appearance. Obstructive hydrocephalus (dilated lateral and 3rd ventricles). No dissemination along CSF pathways.
prompts.reasoning: The 'cyst with mural nodule' pattern on MRI with an enhancing nodule is characteristic of pilocytic astrocytoma — distinguishing it from medulloblastoma (solid vermian mass with CSF dissemination) and ependymoma (heterogeneous, often with calcium, arising from 4th ventricle floor). The absence of CSF spread differentiates it from medulloblastoma.

prompts.label: Surgical Resection & Histopathology
prompts.text: Gross total resection performed. Histopathology: biphasic pattern — compact fibrillary astrocytes with bipolar 'hair-like' (pilocytic) processes alternating with loose microcystic areas. Rosenthal fibres (eosinophilic hyaline structures) and eosinophilic granular bodies present. KIAA1549-BRAF fusion confirmed. WHO Grade I — excellent prognosis (90% 10-year survival).
prompts.reasoning: The biphasic histology with bipolar pilocytic cells, Rosenthal fibres, and eosinophilic granular bodies is diagnostic of pilocytic astrocytoma. BRAF-KIAA1549 fusion (due to chromosome 7q34 tandem duplication) is present in ~70% of cerebellar pilocytic astrocytomas — a key molecular marker. WHO Grade I confirms it is the most benign primary CNS astrocytic tumour, with excellent prognosis after complete resection.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 34-year-old male presents following a first unprovoked seizure — a focal motor seizure affecting his right arm with secondary generalisation. He is a previously healthy software engineer. No prior neurological history.
prompts.reasoning: New-onset focal seizure in a young adult without a prior history of epilepsy mandates MRI to exclude a structural cause. Low-grade gliomas (LGA) classically present with seizures in young adults as the first symptom. Cavernomas and focal cortical dysplasia must also be considered.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: On direct questioning, he recalls 2 episodes over the past year of involuntary right arm jerking lasting 1–2 minutes that he attributed to 'overwork stress'. No focal deficits between episodes. No headaches, no cognitive change.
prompts.reasoning: The history of recurrent brief focal motor seizures over months before presentation is characteristic of low-grade glioma — these tumours grow slowly and epileptogenicity is often the first and only manifestation. The absence of progressive deficits between seizures supports a low-grade rather than high-grade lesion.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: No prior medical history. No family history of brain tumours or NF1. Non-smoker. Occasional alcohol. No radiation exposure. No immunosuppression. Works long hours with significant occupational stress.
prompts.reasoning: The patient's attribution of symptoms to stress illustrates the diagnostic delay common in low-grade glioma — patients adapt to very slowly progressing symptoms. Ionising radiation (prior cranial RT) is the principal known environmental risk factor for gliomas; absence does not exclude the diagnosis. Young adult age is typical for IDH-mutant LGAs.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: GCS 15. Full neurological examination normal — no focal motor, sensory, or cognitive deficits. No papilloedema. MMSE 30/30. BP and general examination unremarkable.
prompts.reasoning: Normal neurological examination between seizures is characteristic of low-grade glioma — the slowly growing tumour causes seizures without acute focal deficits until advanced. This contrasts with high-grade gliomas and metastases, which typically produce rapid progressive deficits.

prompts.label: MRI Brain
prompts.text: MRI brain: ill-defined T2/FLAIR hyperintense lesion (3cm) in the left frontal lobe with cortical and subcortical involvement. No gadolinium enhancement. No surrounding oedema. No haemorrhage or calcification. No mass effect. The signal abnormality infiltrates across the grey-white junction.
prompts.reasoning: Non-enhancing T2/FLAIR hyperintensity without surrounding oedema or mass effect is characteristic of a low-grade glioma. The absence of enhancement is a key feature — enhancement implies breakdown of the blood-brain barrier, suggesting high-grade dedifferentiation. The cortical/subcortical location in the frontal lobe is typical. No calcification makes oligodendroglioma less likely (but MRI calcification sensitivity is low).

prompts.label: Stereotactic Biopsy & Molecular Profiling
prompts.text: Stereotactic biopsy: mildly hypercellular astrocytic proliferation with nuclear atypia but no mitosis, necrosis, or microvascular proliferation. IDH1 mutation (R132H) positive by immunohistochemistry. ATRX loss (confirms astrocytic lineage). 1p/19q codeletion: absent — confirming astrocytoma (not oligodendroglioma). WHO Grade II IDH-mutant astrocytoma. Planned: surveillance MRI ± early surgery.
prompts.reasoning: IDH mutation (IDH1 R132H is most common) and ATRX loss confirm IDH-mutant astrocytoma. The 2021 WHO CNS classification places IDH-mutant astrocytomas without 1p/19q codeletion as 'Astrocytoma, IDH-mutant' (Grade 2–4). Absence of 1p/19q codeletion distinguishes astrocytoma from oligodendroglioma. IDH mutation confers a significantly better prognosis compared to IDH-wildtype GBM. All low-grade gliomas eventually undergo malignant transformation.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 40-year-old female presents with a 3-year history of recurrent focal seizures affecting her right hand and arm, previously attributed to 'idiopathic epilepsy' and managed with antiepileptics with partial control. Recent MRI ordered by her neurologist has revealed an abnormality.
prompts.reasoning: Long-standing drug-resistant focal epilepsy in a young adult requires structural brain lesion evaluation. Oligodendrogliomas are notorious for causing longstanding epilepsy (often years before diagnosis) due to cortical infiltration. A cortical lesion in the frontal lobe is the most likely cause.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The seizures are stereotyped — right hand tingling followed by clonic jerking spreading to the arm (Jacksonian march). The frequency has increased from monthly to weekly over 6 months. No tonic-clonic generalisation. Mild but progressive cognitive slowing reported by her husband over the past year.
prompts.reasoning: The Jacksonian march (somatotopic cortical spread) localises to the left motor/sensory cortex. Increasing seizure frequency without structural investigation over 3 years represents a diagnostic delay, which is common in slowly growing oligodendrogliomas. The subtle cognitive slowing suggests cortical infiltration beyond the seizure focus.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: No other medical history. No family history of brain tumours or NF1. Previously worked as a nurse but has reduced hours due to seizures. Non-smoker. No prior cranial irradiation. Takes levetiracetam and lamotrigine with partial seizure control.
prompts.reasoning: No known risk factors for glioma. Drug-resistant epilepsy with a cortical lesion in a young adult is a classic oligodendroglioma scenario. Oligodendrogliomas are predominantly cortical, accounting for their high epileptogenicity. The 3-year history of focal epilepsy before structural workup illustrates the importance of neuroimaging in drug-resistant epilepsy.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Neurological examination between seizures: mild right hand fine motor deficit detected on rapid alternating movements. No major focal deficits. MMSE 28/30 (attention mildly reduced). No papilloedema. Interictal EEG: focal spikes over left centroparietal region.
prompts.reasoning: Subtle right hand motor deficit and EEG localisation to the left centroparietal region is consistent with a left cortical lesion. The minimal deficit despite a 3-year epilepsy history confirms the slow growth characteristic of oligodendroglioma. EEG localises the epileptic focus to the tumour location.

prompts.label: CT Head & MRI Brain
prompts.text: CT head: gyral expansion in the left frontal lobe with peripheral calcification ('calcium deposits') — visible as dense foci. MRI T2/FLAIR: cortical/subcortical hyperintense lesion (4cm) without gadolinium enhancement. Involvement of the cortical ribbon. No surrounding oedema.
prompts.reasoning: Peripheral calcification within a cortical glioma on CT is characteristically seen in oligodendroglioma — calcium deposition occurs in up to 90% (cf. lower rates in astrocytomas). The cortical/subcortical location and gyral expansion pattern are typical. Non-enhancing appearance indicates lower grade. MRI better characterises extent of cortical infiltration.

prompts.label: Surgical Resection & Molecular Profiling
prompts.text: Awake craniotomy with cortical mapping performed (to preserve motor function). Histopathology: uniform round tumour cells with clear cytoplasm and distinct membranes — 'fried egg' appearance. Chicken-wire vascular pattern. IDH1 R132H mutation confirmed. 1p/19q codeletion confirmed by FISH — diagnostic of oligodendroglioma. WHO Grade 2. Good chemosensitivity to PCV (procarbazine, CCNU, vincristine).
prompts.reasoning: The 'fried egg' artefact in histology (perinuclear halo from fixation artefact) and chicken-wire vasculature are classic oligodendroglioma histological features. The 2021 WHO classification defines oligodendroglioma molecularly by IDH mutation AND 1p/19q codeletion — both are required. 1p/19q codeletion predicts chemosensitivity (PCV/temozolomide) and is the most important prognostic marker — conferring the best survival among gliomas.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 52-year-old female is referred to neurology following a routine health screen MRI that revealed an incidental intracranial lesion. She initially presents with a mild chronic left frontal headache that she has had for over 2 years.
prompts.reasoning: An incidental extra-axial dural-based intracranial lesion in a middle-aged female demands systematic evaluation. The differential for a dural-based mass includes meningioma (most common), dural metastasis, lymphoma, haemangiopericytoma, and abscess. Meningioma is the most common intracranial meningeal tumour.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The headache is mild, non-positional, and intermittent. No vomiting, no visual disturbance. No focal neurological deficits. No seizures. No prior head trauma. Her husband has noticed she has been more forgetful over the past 6 months.
prompts.reasoning: The mild, non-specific headache and subtle cognitive changes are consistent with a slowly growing space-occupying lesion causing frontal lobe compression. Absence of seizures, papilloedema, and acute focal deficits suggests a benign slow-growing process. The 2-year history of unchanged mild symptoms favours a low-grade or benign lesion.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: No history of malignancy or prior cranial irradiation. Takes the combined oral contraceptive pill (OCP). No history of NF2 or family history of meningioma. Perimenopausal. Takes hormone replacement therapy (HRT). No significant alcohol or smoking history.
prompts.reasoning: Female sex (2:1 female:male ratio), exogenous oestrogen exposure (OCP, HRT), and perimenopausal status are established risk factors for meningioma — meningiomas express progesterone receptors in 70% and oestrogen receptors in 30%, explaining the female predominance and growth stimulation by exogenous hormones. Prior cranial irradiation is the strongest known risk factor.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Neurological examination: subtle expressive word-finding difficulty (mild anomic aphasia). MMSE 28/30. No papilloedema. No focal motor or sensory deficits. No pyramidal signs. BP and general examination normal.
prompts.reasoning: Mild anomia consistent with left frontal/temporal involvement from an adjacent dural mass compressing underlying cortex. The absence of papilloedema and acute deficits confirms a slow-growing lesion. Mild word-finding difficulty suggests the mass is adjacent to Broca's area or its frontal connections.

prompts.label: MRI Brain with Gadolinium
prompts.text: MRI brain with gadolinium: 3.5cm uniformly enhancing extra-axial dural-based mass at the left sphenoid wing, with a characteristic 'dural tail' (linear dural enhancement adjacent to the mass). Buckling of adjacent cortex. No oedema. T2: heterogeneous with internal flow voids. Overlying calvarium: focal hyperostosis (bone thickening).
prompts.reasoning: Uniformly enhancing extra-axial mass with dural tail and adjacent hyperostosis are pathognomonic for meningioma. The dural tail represents reactive dural thickening or tumour infiltration. Extra-axial location (mass external to the brain, with buckling of cortex inward) distinguishes it from primary brain parenchymal tumours. Flow voids indicate vascularity.

prompts.label: Surgical Resection & Histopathology
prompts.text: Elective craniotomy for resection. Histopathology: whorls of uniform oval meningothelial cells with intranuclear inclusions. Psammoma bodies (laminated calcific concentric rings) present. NF2 gene mutation identified on molecular testing. Mitotic rate <4/10 HPF. WHO Grade I meningioma. OCP discontinued. Long-term surveillance MRI planned.
prompts.reasoning: Whorls of meningothelial cells and psammoma bodies are the classic histological features of meningioma — arising from the arachnoid cap (meningothelial) cells. NF2 gene mutations (chromosome 22) are the most common molecular alteration in meningioma. WHO Grade I (benign) has a low recurrence rate after complete resection. Psammoma bodies (from calcification within whorls) are pathognomonic. OCP/HRT cessation reduces hormonal growth stimulation.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 7-year-old girl is brought to paediatric clinic with a 2-month history of progressively worsening morning headaches, nausea, and vomiting — predominantly in the mornings before school. Her teacher reports declining school performance.
prompts.reasoning: Morning headaches with vomiting in a child are the classic presentation of raised intracranial pressure — most commonly from a posterior fossa mass. The differential includes ependymoma, medulloblastoma, and pilocytic astrocytoma. Each has a distinct location and clinical profile.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: Vomiting occurs before getting out of bed — typical for raised ICP (recumbency increases ICP). She has developed a head tilt (torticollis) to the right. No limb weakness but she is increasingly unsteady, preferring to hold furniture when walking. No cranial nerve symptoms.
prompts.reasoning: Head tilt (ocular torticollis from acquired CN IV palsy or cerebellar mass) and progressive gait unsteadiness with raised ICP point to a posterior fossa lesion. Ependymoma classically arises from the floor of the 4th ventricle, producing obstructive hydrocephalus and cerebellar compression. Unlike medulloblastoma, ependymoma tends to produce more localised cerebellar signs.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Developmental History
