prompts.text: Normal birth history and developmental milestones. No family history of NF2 or other phakomatoses. No prior radiation exposure. No prior malignancy. Fully vaccinated. Previously a high-performing student.
prompts.reasoning: Ependymoma has no known strong genetic predispositions in most sporadic paediatric cases (NF2 association is primarily with spinal cord ependymoma in adults). Prior radiotherapy increases glioma risk generally. The school performance decline reflects frontal lobe dysfunction from hydrocephalus — raised ICP impairs attention and memory before obvious motor deficits.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: GCS 15. Bilateral papilloedema (Grade II). Head tilt to the right. Right-sided dysmetria on finger-nose test. Nystagmus on right lateral gaze. Gait ataxia. CN VI palsy right eye (false localising sign from raised ICP). No hemiplegia.
prompts.reasoning: Papilloedema confirms raised ICP. Right CN VI palsy (false localising sign — from ICP causing stretch of the long CN VI course) is common in posterior fossa lesions. Ipsilateral cerebellar signs (right hemisphere) suggest a right-sided mass. Ependymoma typically extends from the 4th ventricle floor, compressing adjacent cerebellar tissue. The absence of brainstem signs differentiates it from brainstem glioma.

prompts.label: MRI Brain
prompts.text: MRI brain with gadolinium: heterogeneous T2-hyperintense mass filling and expanding the 4th ventricle with extension through the foramina of Luschka into the left cerebellopontine angle — the classic 'plastic' ependymoma extrusion. Areas of calcification and cystic change. Moderate enhancement. Obstructive hydrocephalus (dilated lateral and 3rd ventricles).
prompts.reasoning: Ependymoma's characteristic 'plastic' extension through the foramina of Luschka (lateral) and Magendie (posterior) is pathognomonic — the tumour extrudes through these narrow openings. This distinguishes it from medulloblastoma (solid vermian mass, does not extrude) and pilocytic astrocytoma (cyst-nodule, hemisphere). Calcification and heterogeneity are features of ependymoma.

prompts.label: Surgical Resection & Histopathology
prompts.text: Gross total resection performed (key prognostic factor). Histopathology: uniform cells with round nuclei arranged in perivascular pseudorosettes (tumour cells arranged around blood vessels with an anuclear zone). True ependymal rosettes (cells lining a central lumen). RELA fusion (C11orf95-RELA) identified — confirms supratentorial ependymoma subtype in adults; posterior fossa group A (PFA) in this child. CSF staging: no leptomeningeal spread.
prompts.reasoning: Perivascular pseudorosettes are the histological hallmark of ependymoma — tumour cells with fibrillary processes extending to vessel walls, creating a characteristic anuclear zone. True ependymal rosettes (rarer, diagnostic) form around a central canal-like lumen. Molecular classification (PFA/PFB) guides prognosis — PFA has worse outcome. Gross total resection significantly improves survival.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 6-year-old male is brought to ED with 3 weeks of daily morning vomiting, severe headache, and an increasingly unsteady gait. He has become increasingly irritable and has stopped playing with other children.
prompts.reasoning: Rapidly progressive raised ICP symptoms with cerebellar signs in a young child is a classic presentation of medulloblastoma — the most common malignant brain tumour in children. The aggressive pace (3 weeks) is more consistent with medulloblastoma than the more indolent pilocytic astrocytoma.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: Vomiting occurs on waking, before eating. Gait disturbance is predominantly truncal — he falls toward the midline (backwards and forwards) rather than to one side. No arm clumsiness. No diplopia or facial weakness. He has lost 1.5kg in 3 weeks.
prompts.reasoning: Truncal ataxia (difficulty with balance in the midline axis, due to vermian involvement) is the hallmark of medulloblastoma, which classically arises from the cerebellar vermis. This contrasts with pilocytic astrocytoma and ependymoma which typically affect the hemispheres and produce ipsilateral limb ataxia. Weight loss and the aggressive onset suggest a more malignant tumour.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Family History
prompts.text: Normal birth and development. Family history of multiple colonic polyps in the father — Turcot syndrome (Gardner variant) suspected but not confirmed. No prior radiation. Not on medications.
prompts.reasoning: Turcot syndrome (APC gene mutations causing familial adenomatous polyposis plus CNS tumours) is associated with medulloblastoma. Gorlin syndrome (PTCH1 mutation) is also associated with desmoplastic medulloblastoma. These syndromic associations underlie the molecular subgrouping of medulloblastoma (WNT, SHH, Group 3, Group 4). However, most medulloblastomas are sporadic.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: GCS 14. Bilateral papilloedema (Grade III). Bilateral CN VI palsies (bilateral false localising signs from raised ICP). Severely positive Romberg test. Wide-based truncal ataxia — unable to stand without support. No significant limb ataxia. Bilateral upgoing plantars — involvement of adjacent corticospinal tracts.
prompts.reasoning: Severe truncal ataxia with raised ICP and bilateral CN VI palsies confirms a posterior fossa mass causing hydrocephalus. Bilateral pyramidal signs suggest corticospinal tract involvement, possibly from compression of adjacent brainstem structures or transependymal spread. Urgent neuroimaging and neurosurgical referral are required.

prompts.label: MRI Brain
prompts.text: MRI brain: hyperdense, avidly enhancing solid mass in the cerebellar vermis, filling the 4th ventricle. DWI: restricted diffusion (high cellularity). Severe obstructive hydrocephalus. No 'plastic extrusion' into lateral apertures (distinguishes from ependymoma). Gadolinium-enhanced spinal MRI and CSF cytology ordered (staging).
prompts.reasoning: Vermian solid avidly enhancing mass with restricted diffusion (reflecting high cellularity) is characteristic of medulloblastoma. Restricted diffusion on DWI is important — it reflects the dense small blue cell histology. Medulloblastoma's propensity for CSF dissemination (leptomeningeal spread) is the most important adverse prognostic feature — requiring spinal MRI and CSF cytology staging.

prompts.label: Surgical Resection, CSF Staging & Histopathology
prompts.text: Gross total resection performed. CSF cytology: malignant cells present — M1 stage disease. Histopathology: highly cellular small blue cells with scant cytoplasm and carrot-shaped (anaplastic) nuclei, abundant mitoses, Homer Wright rosettes (tumour cells arranged around central fibrillary neuropil). CTNNB1 mutation detected — WNT subgroup (best prognosis). Adjuvant craniospinal irradiation + chemotherapy planned.
prompts.reasoning: Small blue cells with Homer Wright rosettes (central neuropil core, no lumen — differs from ependymal rosettes) are the histological hallmark of classic medulloblastoma. The WNT molecular subgroup (CTNNB1/β-catenin mutation) has the best prognosis (~90% 5-year survival). CSF dissemination (M1 disease) requires craniospinal irradiation. Medulloblastoma accounts for 20% of paediatric brain tumours.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 35-year-old female presents to the ophthalmology clinic with gradually progressive difficulty seeing objects in the peripheral visual fields, particularly on the outer side of both eyes, developing over 6 months. She also reports worsening headaches.
prompts.reasoning: Bitemporal hemianopia localises to a lesion compressing the optic chiasm — specifically the crossing nasal fibres (which carry temporal visual field information). The optic chiasm sits just superior to the pituitary gland, making pituitary adenoma the most important differential. Craniopharyngioma and suprasellar meningioma must also be considered.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: She has had amenorrhoea for 8 months and galactorrhoea (milk discharge from both breasts) despite not being pregnant. She has gained 8kg over the past year. She initially attributed the visual changes to 'eye strain at work'.
prompts.reasoning: Amenorrhoea and galactorrhoea in a non-pregnant woman indicate hyperprolactinaemia — typically from a prolactin-secreting pituitary adenoma (prolactinoma). Elevated prolactin inhibits GnRH → suppressing LH/FSH → amenorrhoea. Galactorrhoea is direct prolactin effect. This strongly narrows the differential to a prolactin-secreting pituitary adenoma.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Drug History
prompts.text: No antipsychotics, antidepressants, antiemetics, or other dopamine-blocking drugs that could cause drug-induced hyperprolactinaemia. No history of hypothyroidism. No family history of MEN1 (multiple endocrine neoplasia type 1). Previously regular menstrual cycles.
prompts.reasoning: Drug-induced hyperprolactinaemia (dopamine antagonists such as antipsychotics, metoclopramide, domperidone) is an important and common cause — excluding this is essential. Hypothyroidism can also raise prolactin. Absence of both confirms a pituitary source. MEN1 syndrome (pituitary + parathyroid + pancreatic tumours) must be considered in young patients with pituitary adenoma.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Visual field testing (confrontation and Humphrey perimetry): bitemporal hemianopia — denser superiorly. Galactorrhoea on breast compression. BMI 29. No features of acromegaly or Cushing's syndrome. Blood pressure normal. Optic disc margins slightly blurred bilaterally.
prompts.reasoning: Bitemporal hemianopia (denser superiorly) reflects preferential compression of the inferior crossing nasal fibres (carrying superior temporal field) from a pituitary mass expanding superiorly. This is the classic visual field defect of pituitary macroadenoma. Blurred disc margins suggest early papilloedema from raised ICP.

prompts.label: Serum Prolactin & Endocrine Workup
prompts.text: Serum prolactin: 4,200 mU/L (markedly elevated; normal <600 mU/L in females). TFTs: normal. IGF-1: normal (excludes GH-secreting adenoma). Morning cortisol and ACTH: normal. Testosterone (male marker — not applicable here). FSH/LH: suppressed. Serum calcium: normal.
prompts.reasoning: Markedly elevated prolactin (>2000 mU/L) in the context of a pituitary mass is diagnostic of prolactin-secreting adenoma (prolactinoma) — drug causes and hypothyroidism typically cause only modest elevations (<2000 mU/L). Normal pituitary function tests (GH, ACTH, TSH axis) confirm it is a pure prolactinoma without panhypopituitarism.

prompts.label: MRI Pituitary (Sella Protocol)
prompts.text: MRI pituitary with gadolinium (sella protocol — thin coronal cuts): 2.8cm macroadenoma with suprasellar extension compressing the optic chiasm from below. The mass elevates and compresses the chiasm. No cavernous sinus invasion. Normal pituitary tissue compressed ('horseshoe' pattern). Treatment: dopamine agonist (cabergoline) — prolactinoma responds dramatically to medical therapy.
prompts.reasoning: MRI sella protocol confirms a pituitary macroadenoma (>1cm) with suprasellar extension compressing the optic chiasm — explaining the bitemporal hemianopia. Prolactinomas are preferentially treated medically with dopamine agonists (cabergoline/bromocriptine) — tumour shrinkage restores vision and prolactin normalises. Surgery is reserved for drug resistance or intolerance. Pituitary adenomas arise from adenohypophysis; they may secrete hormones (functional) or be non-secreting.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 10-year-old boy is referred by his paediatrician with a 6-month history of headaches, declining school performance, and unexplained short stature for his age. His height is below the 3rd centile for his age.
prompts.reasoning: Headaches, cognitive decline, and short stature in a child must raise concern for a suprasellar mass affecting the pituitary-hypothalamic axis. Craniopharyngioma and optic pathway gliomas are the most important suprasellar lesions in children. Growth hormone deficiency is a common consequence of suprasellar lesions.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: He has difficulty seeing the sides of the blackboard at school — consistent with visual field restriction. Polydipsia and polyuria have been noted by his parents (3L urine/day). He has been tired and increasingly somnolent during school hours. No nausea or vomiting.
prompts.reasoning: Bitemporal visual field defect (blackboard sides), diabetes insipidus (DI — polyuria and polydipsia from ADH deficiency due to hypothalamic compression), and excessive somnolence from hypothalamic dysfunction strongly suggest a suprasellar mass compressing the optic chiasm and hypothalamus. Craniopharyngioma characteristically causes DI and panhypopituitarism in children.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Developmental History
prompts.text: Normal birth and early development until age 8. Growth velocity declined from age 8. Previous GP visits for short stature attributed to constitutional delay. No family history of brain tumours or endocrine disorders. No prior radiotherapy. Not on any medications.
prompts.reasoning: Craniopharyngioma has a bimodal age distribution — childhood peak (5–15 years) and adult peak (50–70 years). The gradually declining growth velocity from age 8 represents the onset of GH deficiency from pituitary stalk compression. Constitutional delay is a diagnosis of exclusion — a suprasellar mass must be excluded in a child with short stature and neurological symptoms.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Height -2.5 SDS (severely short for age). Weight appropriate. Bitemporal hemianopia on confrontation. Pale optic discs bilaterally (optic atrophy from chronic chiasmal compression). No pubertal development (Tanner stage 1). Urine osmolality 80 mOsmol/kg (inappropriately dilute).
prompts.reasoning: Optic atrophy from chronic chiasmal compression indicates long-standing visual pathway damage. Absent pubertal development confirms hypogonadotrophic hypogonadism from hypothalamic/pituitary dysfunction. Dilute urine confirms diabetes insipidus (ADH deficiency). Panhypopituitarism (GH, gonadotrophins, TSH, ACTH deficiency) from hypothalamic-pituitary disruption is the defining endocrine consequence of craniopharyngioma.

prompts.label: CT and MRI Skull/Brain
prompts.text: CT skull: suprasellar calcification — 'eggshell calcification' in the suprasellar region. MRI with gadolinium: lobulated suprasellar cystic and solid mass with rim enhancement. Cyst contents: T1-bright (from protein/cholesterol — 'machine oil' fluid). The mass compresses the optic chiasm from above and extends into the 3rd ventricle, causing obstructive hydrocephalus.
prompts.reasoning: Suprasellar calcification on CT combined with a cystic enhancing mass is essentially pathognomonic for craniopharyngioma (adamantinomatous type in children). The T1-bright cyst contents correspond to the 'machine oil' or 'crankcase oil' fluid — dark brown fluid containing cholesterol crystals and haemosiderin — seen at surgery. This distinguishes craniopharyngioma from other suprasellar cysts.

prompts.label: Surgical Resection & Histopathology
prompts.text: Transsphenoidal and transcranial surgery performed. Intraoperative: thick, dark machine-oil-like cyst fluid. Histopathology (adamantinomatous type): squamous epithelium with peripheral palisading, 'wet keratin' (stellate reticulum with keratinising foci), and cholesterol clefts. CTNNB1 (β-catenin) nuclear mutation characteristic of adamantinomatous type. Hypopituitarism treated with hormone replacement.
prompts.reasoning: Adamantinomatous craniopharyngioma (childhood type) has distinctive histology: peripheral palisading squamous epithelium, stellate reticulum, 'wet keratin' nodules, and calcification — arising from remnants of Rathke's pouch (craniopharyngeal duct). The CTNNB1 mutation (β-catenin/WNT pathway) is the molecular hallmark. The papillary type (adult) has BRAF V600E mutation and different histology. Complete resection risks hypothalamic damage — quality of life vs cure must be balanced.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 58-year-old male, known to be HIV-positive (CD4 count 180 cells/µL, not on HAART), presents with progressive confusion, personality change, and mild right arm weakness over 3 weeks. He has lost 5kg in 2 months.
prompts.reasoning: In an immunocompromised (HIV, CD4 180) patient with progressive focal neurological deficits, the main differentials are the common CNS opportunistic infections and malignancies. Primary CNS lymphoma and cerebral toxoplasmosis are the most important to distinguish — they can appear identical on imaging but have different treatments.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: Symptoms have developed over 3 weeks with cognitive decline and personality change (disinhibition, aggression). The right arm weakness is progressive. No fever, no headache, no neck stiffness. No prior toxoplasma prophylaxis was taken. He lives in a cat-owning household.
prompts.reasoning: Progressive subacute focal deficits without fever in a severely immunocompromised patient narrows the differential to PCNSL and toxoplasmosis (which can be focal and sometimes without fever). No toxoplasma prophylaxis (co-trimoxazole) and cat exposure increases toxoplasmosis risk. Absence of fever slightly favours PCNSL. The two cannot be reliably distinguished clinically.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: HIV diagnosed 5 years ago — poor HAART adherence (CD4 nadir 80 cells/µL). No prior neurological history. Heterosexual, no IV drug use. Toxoplasma IgG serology: positive (prior exposure). EBV serology: positive (prior infection).
prompts.reasoning: CD4 <200 cells/µL is the threshold for significant opportunistic infection risk. PCNSL (EBV-associated in HIV patients) almost exclusively occurs at CD4 <50 cells/µL in the HIV setting — CD4 180 is higher, making toxoplasmosis somewhat more likely. However, PCNSL can occur at CD4 <200. Positive Toxoplasma IgG confirms prior infection and reactivation risk. EBV is the oncogenic driver of AIDS-related PCNSL.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: GCS 13. Right hemiparesis (arm 3/5, leg 4/5). Mild expressive aphasia. No meningism. No fever. Fundoscopy: no retinal changes. Lymph nodes: no peripheral lymphadenopathy. Oral candidiasis present — confirms immunosuppression.
prompts.reasoning: Right UMN hemiparesis with aphasia localises to the left hemisphere. Absent lymphadenopathy argues against systemic lymphoma with CNS involvement. Oral candidiasis confirms severe immunosuppression. No fundal lesions makes CMV retinitis less likely. The two diagnoses (PCNSL vs toxoplasmosis) cannot be distinguished by examination.

prompts.label: MRI Brain with Gadolinium
prompts.text: MRI brain: periventricular ring-enhancing lesion (4cm) in the left deep white matter/basal ganglia with central hypointensity on DWI. Unlike toxoplasmosis, the lesion contacts the ependyma (periventricular location). SPECT: increased thallium-201 uptake (high metabolic activity indicates malignancy). Toxoplasma empirical therapy trial: no clinical or radiological improvement after 14 days.
prompts.reasoning: Periventricular ring-enhancing lesion with ependymal contact is more characteristic of PCNSL than toxoplasmosis (which is typically in the basal ganglia and thalamus, often multiple). Thallium SPECT showing high uptake (metabolically active malignancy) differentiates PCNSL from toxoplasmosis (which shows low uptake). Failure to respond to empirical anti-toxoplasmosis therapy supports PCNSL.

prompts.label: CSF Analysis & Stereotactic Biopsy
prompts.text: Stereotactic biopsy (after failed toxoplasma trial): diffuse large B-cell lymphoma (DLBCL) on histology — large B-cells with prominent nucleoli, perivascular cuffing, angiocentric infiltration. EBV in situ hybridisation: EBER-positive (EBV-driven lymphomagenesis). Dexamethasone discontinued before biopsy (steroids cause 'vanishing tumour' — temporary regression masking diagnosis). CSF: EBV DNA PCR positive.
prompts.reasoning: PCNSL is almost exclusively DLBCL (B-cell lineage) in immunocompetent patients; in AIDS, it is EBV-driven DLBCL. Angiocentric infiltration (tumour cells cuffing blood vessels and destroying surrounding brain) is the histological hallmark. Steroids must be withheld before biopsy — they cause tumour lysis and a 'vanishing tumour' appearance that can delay diagnosis. EBV EBER positivity in situ confirms viral oncogenesis.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 62-year-old female with a known history of breast cancer (treated with mastectomy and chemotherapy 3 years ago, currently thought to be in remission) presents with a 4-week history of progressive right-sided headache and new-onset focal seizures affecting the left hand.
prompts.reasoning: New neurological symptoms in a patient with prior malignancy must be assumed to be brain metastases until proven otherwise. Breast cancer is one of the most common primary tumours to metastasise to the brain (along with lung, melanoma, renal, GI). However, GBM, meningioma, and radiation necrosis must also be considered.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: Headaches are progressive, worse in the morning, and exacerbated by coughing. Left hand focal seizures have occurred 4 times, each lasting 1–2 minutes with post-ictal weakness. She has also noted diplopia on left lateral gaze for the past week.
prompts.reasoning: Morning headache worsened by Valsalva indicates raised ICP from a space-occupying lesion. The focal left hand motor seizures localise to the right motor cortex. New CN VI palsy (diplopia on lateral gaze) indicates raised ICP causing false localising sign, or posterior fossa involvement. Multiple neurological localisations in a cancer patient strongly suggest metastatic disease.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Oncological History
prompts.text: Hormone receptor-positive (ER+/PR+), HER2-negative breast cancer — pT2N1M0 (Stage IIA). Treated with surgery, adjuvant chemotherapy (AC-T), and radiotherapy. Now on tamoxifen. Last oncology review 8 months ago showed no evidence of systemic disease on CT chest/abdomen.
prompts.reasoning: Breast cancer can metastasise to the brain even years after apparently successful primary treatment — HR+ HER2- breast cancer tends to have late relapses. CT chest/abdomen may show no systemic disease while intracranial metastases develop (the brain is a 'sanctuary site' protected by the blood-brain barrier from systemic chemotherapy). Brain MRI is far more sensitive than CT for detecting brain metastases.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: GCS 14. Bilateral papilloedema (Grade II). Left-sided facial droop (UMN). Left arm power 3/5, leg 4/5. Right lateral gaze: intact. CN VI palsy left. Right plantar upgoing. Seizure activity witnessed during examination — focal left arm tonic-clonic, self-terminating.
prompts.reasoning: Multiple neurological findings (papilloedema, UMN left hemiparesis, CN VI palsy) from a right hemisphere lesion in a cancer patient. Witnessed focal seizure confirms cortical irritation. The combination of symptoms suggests possible multiple lesions (explaining CN VI palsy AND hemiparesis from different sites), which would be highly characteristic of metastatic disease.

prompts.label: MRI Brain with Gadolinium
prompts.text: MRI brain with gadolinium: multiple (5) ring-enhancing lesions at the grey-white junction in both hemispheres, largest 3cm in the right parietal lobe. Surrounding vasogenic oedema. No leptomeningeal enhancement. Lesions are spherical with sharp margins — characteristic of haematogenous metastasis. CT chest/abdomen: no new systemic disease. Bone scan: new vertebral metastases at L3.
prompts.reasoning: Multiple ring-enhancing lesions at the grey-white junction — the site where tumour emboli lodge as vessels narrow — is characteristic of haematogenous brain metastases. The spherical morphology with surrounding oedema reflects the tumour growing as a mass within the brain parenchyma. Vertebral metastases confirm systemic recurrence. Treatment options: whole-brain radiotherapy, stereotactic radiosurgery (for ≤3 lesions), and targeted therapy (trastuzumab if HER2-positive).

prompts.label: Biopsy, Receptor Testing & Management
prompts.text: Stereotactic biopsy of the right parietal lesion: metastatic adenocarcinoma, morphology consistent with breast primary. ER+/PR+/HER2-negative confirmed. Ki-67 40% (high proliferation). Dexamethasone commenced (reduces vasogenic oedema). Stereotactic radiosurgery to right parietal lesion planned. Systemic CDK4/6 inhibitor added to tamoxifen for metastatic HR+ disease.
prompts.reasoning: Histological confirmation identifies the primary and guides treatment. Receptor testing on brain metastases is critical as phenotypic switching from primary to metastasis can occur. Dexamethasone rapidly reduces vasogenic oedema surrounding metastases. Brain metastases are the most common intracranial neoplasm overall — approximately 10 times more common than primary brain tumours in adults. The grey-white junction location reflects the narrowing of vascular calibre at this site, causing emboli to lodge.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 45-year-old male presents with a 2-year history of progressive hearing loss in the right ear, high-pitched tinnitus on the right, and occasional unsteadiness. He initially attributed these symptoms to earwax.
prompts.reasoning: Unilateral sensorineural hearing loss, tinnitus, and disequilibrium is the classic triad of vestibular schwannoma (acoustic neuroma). The insidious onset and progressive nature over 2 years points away from acute conditions. Ménière's disease and Labyrinthitis can mimic early presentation but typically have different audiological profiles.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The hearing loss is unilateral, affecting only the right ear — he struggles to hear speech in his right ear and on telephone. He has never had sudden complete hearing loss or rotary vertigo. No otalgia or ear discharge. No facial weakness.
prompts.reasoning: Progressive unilateral sensorineural hearing loss (not episodic, not sudden) with ipsilateral tinnitus and disequilibrium (not vertigo) is characteristic of vestibular schwannoma. Ménière's disease typically causes episodic rotary vertigo with fluctuating hearing loss. Absence of facial weakness (CN VII) is typical at this stage — facial nerve involvement occurs late as the tumour enlarges.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Family History
prompts.text: No prior noise exposure. No family history of bilateral vestibular schwannomas or NF2. No hearing problems in childhood. Previously worked as a teacher (no industrial noise exposure). No ototoxic drug use (aminoglycosides, cisplatin).
prompts.reasoning: Bilateral vestibular schwannomas are pathognomonic of NF2 (NF2 gene, Merlin protein — chromosome 22). Absence of family history and bilateral disease does not exclude NF2 as germline mutations can be de novo. Unilateral vestibular schwannoma in a middle-aged adult without NF2 features is sporadic. Absence of ototoxic drugs or noise exposure excludes other causes of SNHL.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Rinne test: Rinne positive right (AC>BC but reduced) — sensorineural pattern. Weber: lateralises to left (away from the lesion). Corneal reflex right: diminished (CN V involvement). Facial nerve (CN VII): intact. No cerebellar signs. No papilloedema. Audiogram: right sensorineural hearing loss of 45 dB, predominantly high frequency.
prompts.reasoning: Sensorineural pattern (Rinne positive but reduced, Weber to opposite ear) on the right confirms right-sided sensorineural hearing loss. Diminished corneal reflex right (CN V compression in the internal auditory canal/CPA angle) is a common associated sign. Intact facial nerve despite significant cochlear nerve involvement is typical of vestibular schwannoma's slow growth. High-frequency SNHL is characteristic.

prompts.label: MRI Internal Auditory Meati (IAM) with Gadolinium
prompts.text: MRI IAM with gadolinium: 2.5cm avidly enhancing mass at the right cerebellopontine angle (CPA), with extension into the right internal auditory canal ('ice cream cone' appearance). The mass does not cross the internal auditory meatus. No bilateral lesions. No other schwannomas or meningiomas. Brainstem not compressed at this size.
prompts.reasoning: An enhancing mass at the CPA angle extending into the IAC with the 'ice cream cone' morphology (rounded CPA component + cylindrical IAC component) is characteristic of vestibular schwannoma. The CPA is the most common location for CPA tumours: 80% vestibular schwannoma, 10% meningioma, 5% epidermoid. Extension into IAC distinguishes schwannoma from meningioma (which does not extend into IAC).

prompts.label: Surgical Resection & Histopathology
prompts.text: Retrosigmoid craniotomy performed. Histopathology: biphasic pattern — Antoni A areas (compact, elongated bipolar cells with nuclear palisading forming Verocay bodies) and Antoni B areas (loose myxoid stroma with scattered cells). S-100 positive. NF2 gene mutation on tumour sequencing. WHO Grade I. Post-operative facial nerve monitoring — intact. Sensorineural hearing lost.
prompts.reasoning: Antoni A (compact, palisaded — Verocay bodies) and Antoni B (loose, myxoid) patterns are the defining histological features of schwannoma. S-100 positivity confirms Schwann cell origin. NF2 gene (encoding Merlin, a tumour suppressor) mutations underlie both sporadic schwannomas and NF2-associated bilateral vestibular schwannomas. WHO Grade I — benign but requires surgery, radiosurgery, or surveillance depending on size and symptoms.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 28-year-old male presents with a 3-month history of progressively worsening headaches, worst in the morning. He has had two episodes of blurred vision, each resolving within minutes. His girlfriend reports he seems 'out of it' at times.
prompts.reasoning: Progressive morning headache with transient visual obscurations (from papilloedema) in a young adult suggests a ventricular or periventricular space-occupying lesion causing obstructive hydrocephalus. Central neurocytoma, ependymoma, and choroid plexus papilloma must be considered for an intraventricular mass in this age group.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: Transient vision blurring lasts seconds and occurs on position change ('positional visual obscurations') — consistent with raised ICP causing transient ischaemia of the optic nerves. Occasional nausea. Episodes of brief confusion may represent obstructive hydrocephalus causing transient reduced consciousness. No seizures, no focal weakness.
prompts.reasoning: Positional visual obscurations are the classic symptom of raised ICP — transient elevation with position change briefly compromises optic nerve perfusion. The episodic confusion may represent acute hydrocephalic decompensation. A third ventricular lesion (colloid cyst) can cause positional hydrocephalus with sudden-onset symptoms, which can be fatal if unrecognised.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: No significant past medical history. No family history of brain tumours or tuberous sclerosis. No prior radiation. Non-smoker, occasional alcohol. No medications. A university student — the headaches have significantly impaired his studies.
prompts.reasoning: Central neurocytoma is a tumour of young adults (peak 20–40 years), typically arising near the foramen of Monro in the lateral ventricle. No genetic predisposition is established for sporadic central neurocytoma. The absence of NF2 features or tuberous sclerosis (which can cause subependymal giant cell astrocytoma — SEGA) narrows the differential.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: GCS 14. Bilateral papilloedema (Grade II). Bilateral CN VI palsies (false localising signs from raised ICP). No focal motor or sensory deficits. MMSE 27/30 (attention deficit from raised ICP). Fundoscopy confirms papilloedema with venous pulsation absent.
prompts.reasoning: Bilateral papilloedema and bilateral CN VI palsies (from raised ICP) without focal hemisphere deficits points to an intraventricular mass causing hydrocephalus rather than a hemisphere parenchymal lesion. Absent venous pulsation confirms raised ICP. No hemisphere deficits suggests the mass is in the ventricular system rather than the parenchyma.

prompts.label: CT Head & MRI Brain
prompts.text: CT head: intraventricular mass at the foramen of Monro with calcification and obstructive hydrocephalus. MRI with gadolinium: heterogeneous mass (3.5cm) at the septum pellucidum/foramen of Monro, with a 'soap bubble' appearance due to prominent vascularity and cystic changes. Moderate gadolinium enhancement. No extraventricular extension.
prompts.reasoning: The 'soap bubble' MRI appearance (multiple cystic spaces within a solid tumour) at the septum pellucidum/foramen of Monro in a young adult is characteristic of central neurocytoma. This location (foramen of Monro in the lateral ventricle) is essentially diagnostic when combined with the demographic. Calcification on CT is common. The differential of a lateral ventricular mass at the foramen of Monro in a young adult is dominated by central neurocytoma.

prompts.label: Surgical Resection & Histopathology
prompts.text: Endoscopic-assisted surgical resection. Histopathology: uniform small round cells with stippled ('salt and pepper') chromatin pattern, neuropil islands (fibrillary matrix between tumour cells), and perivascular arrangement — resembling oligodendroglioma but without 1p/19q codeletion. Synaptophysin strongly positive (neuronal marker). Electron microscopy: dense core granules and microtubule-containing processes. WHO Grade II. Excellent prognosis (5-year survival >90%).
prompts.reasoning: The histological appearance mimics oligodendroglioma ('fried egg' cells) but synaptophysin positivity confirms neuronal (not glial) differentiation — hence 'neurocytoma'. Neuropil islands (fibrillary islands of neuronal processes between tumour cells) are characteristic. EM reveals neuronal ultrastructure (dense core granules, synaptic vesicles). Synaptophysin positivity + foramen of Monro location in a young adult = central neurocytoma. WHO Grade II with good prognosis after resection.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 19-year-old male university student is brought to ED by his flatmates with a 12-hour history of severe headache, high fever, and neck stiffness. He appears unwell and photophobic.
prompts.reasoning: The classic triad of headache, fever, and neck stiffness should immediately prompt consideration of meningitis. SAH must also be urgently excluded (thunderclap headache). The age, close-living environment (university dormitory), and acute rapid progression favour bacterial over viral or fungal meningitis.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: Headache came on over several hours (not thunderclap). He vomited twice. He noticed a non-blanching petechial rash developing on his lower limbs over the last 2 hours. He cannot tolerate light and has been increasingly drowsy. No recent travel. Had an URTI 3 days ago.
prompts.reasoning: A non-blanching petechial/purpuric rash in the context of meningism is meningococcal septicaemia until proven otherwise — a life-threatening emergency requiring immediate IV antibiotics without waiting for LP. The non-blanching nature indicates purpura from meningococcal endotoxin-mediated vasculitis and DIC. The preceding URTI is consistent with nasopharyngeal colonisation by Neisseria meningitidis.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: Lives in a university dormitory — close contact with many peers. Not vaccinated against meningococcal serogroup B (received MenACWY at age 14). No immunodeficiency. Not on antibiotics. No recent foreign travel. Non-smoker, drinks alcohol socially. No drug use.
prompts.reasoning: University dormitory living is a well-established risk factor for meningococcal meningitis due to close contact and droplet spread. Neisseria meningitidis (Group B common in young adults in the UK/Singapore) and Streptococcus pneumoniae are the most common pathogens in this age group. Incomplete vaccination increases risk. Close contacts require ciprofloxacin prophylaxis.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Temperature 39.8°C, HR 128 bpm, BP 88/56 mmHg, RR 24/min. GCS 12 (E3V4M5). Severe neck stiffness — Kernig's and Brudzinski's signs positive. Non-blanching purpuric rash on lower limbs and trunk. Bilateral papilloedema absent. No focal neurological deficits.
prompts.reasoning: Meningococcal meningitis with septicaemia is confirmed clinically. Signs of shock (tachycardia, hypotension) indicate meningococcal septicaemia — a life-threatening emergency. IV ceftriaxone must be given IMMEDIATELY without waiting for CT or LP. Positive Kernig's (inability to extend knee with hip flexed) and Brudzinski's signs confirm meningeal irritation. No papilloedema — CT before LP is still required per local guidelines.

prompts.label: CT Head
prompts.text: CT head (non-contrast): mild generalised cerebral oedema. No hydrocephalus. No midline shift. No mass lesions. Safe to perform lumbar puncture. CSF urgently requested.
prompts.reasoning: CT is necessary before LP to exclude raised ICP with mass effect (risk of tonsillar herniation with LP). Absence of mass lesions and minimal oedema permits LP. Generalised cerebral oedema is consistent with bacterial meningitis. CT is not diagnostic of bacterial meningitis — CSF analysis is definitive. Empirical antibiotics (ceftriaxone ± dexamethasone) should not be delayed awaiting CT if patient is deteriorating.

prompts.label: CSF Analysis & Blood Cultures
prompts.text: CSF appearance: turbid (purulent). Opening pressure 280 mmH₂O (elevated). CSF WBC 4,200 cells/µL (>90% polymorphonuclear neutrophils). CSF protein: 2.8 g/L (elevated). CSF glucose: 1.1 mmol/L; blood glucose 6.2 mmol/L (CSF:blood ratio <0.4). Gram stain: Gram-negative diplococci. Culture: Neisseria meningitidis serogroup B. Blood cultures: positive for N. meningitidis. CT confirms purulent exudate in subarachnoid space on pathological correlation.
prompts.reasoning: The CSF profile is classical for bacterial meningitis: turbid, markedly elevated neutrophils (PMN pleocytosis), high protein, very low glucose (bacteria consume glucose + blood-brain barrier disruption), and low CSF:blood glucose ratio (<0.4). Gram-negative diplococci confirm Neisseria meningitidis. Pathologically, the subarachnoid space is filled with a purulent exudate rich in neutrophils — 'cap of pus'. Bacterial meningitis risk of mortality is 10-30%; survivors risk deafness, cognitive impairment, and limb amputation (due to meningococcal septicaemia).

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 34-year-old male from Indonesia presents with a 3-week history of progressive headache, low-grade fever, and gradually worsening neck stiffness. He appears lethargic and has lost 5 kg over the past 2 months.
prompts.reasoning: Subacute meningitis (weeks, not hours/days) with constitutional symptoms (weight loss, low-grade fever) in a patient from a TB-endemic region should prompt strong consideration of tuberculous meningitis. The insidious onset distinguishes TBM from acute bacterial meningitis. Cryptococcal meningitis and CNS lymphoma must also be considered, particularly in the context of possible immunosuppression.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: He reports his headache began insidiously and has been progressively worsening. He had a productive cough for 6 weeks prior. He has developed double vision over the past week. Night sweats and anorexia are present. No rash, no sudden onset, no focal limb weakness.
prompts.reasoning: Cranial nerve palsies (diplopia — CN VI or III) in the context of subacute meningitis point to TBM, which characteristically causes cranial nerve deficits through inflammation at the base of the brain (basal exudate). CN VI palsy is most common. The productive cough suggests pulmonary TB, which is the source of haematogenous CNS seeding. Night sweats and weight loss complete the constitutional TB syndrome.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: Born and raised in Indonesia (high TB-endemic country). Worked in a crowded urban environment. Not vaccinated with BCG (records unclear). No known HIV status — HIV test never performed. Not on any medications. Non-smoker. No history of prior TB treatment.
prompts.reasoning: TBM is most common in TB-endemic countries. BCG vaccination reduces the risk of miliary TB and TBM in children. HIV co-infection is a major risk factor — HIV testing is mandatory in any patient with TBM, as immunosuppression dramatically worsens prognosis and affects treatment decisions. CNS involvement occurs via haematogenous seeding from a primary focus (lung most common) — Rich foci in the cortex rupture into the subarachnoid space.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Temperature 38.1°C, HR 96 bpm, BP 118/76 mmHg. GCS 13. Neck stiffness present. Left CN VI palsy (left lateral gaze palsy). Fundoscopy: early papilloedema bilaterally. No focal limb deficits. Kernig's sign positive. Lymphadenopathy: 2 cm right cervical lymph node.
prompts.reasoning: Cranial nerve palsy (CN VI) in the context of subacute meningitis localises to the base of the brain — characteristic of TBM. The basal exudate in TBM is thick and fibrinous, trapping cranial nerves as they traverse the basal cisterns. Papilloedema indicates communicating hydrocephalus from CSF outflow obstruction by the basal exudate. Cervical lymphadenopathy may represent concurrent TB lymphadenitis (scrofula).

prompts.label: MRI Brain with Gadolinium
prompts.text: MRI brain (gadolinium): thick enhancement of the basal meninges (basal cisterns, suprasellar cistern, Sylvian fissures). Multiple small hypointense foci in basal ganglia and thalamus consistent with tuberculomas. Moderate communicating hydrocephalus. Chest X-ray: upper lobe consolidation with apical nodules and mild hilar lymphadenopathy.
prompts.reasoning: Basal meningeal enhancement is the hallmark of TBM on MRI — the thick granulomatous exudate collects at the base of the brain, enhancing intensely with gadolinium. Tuberculomas (small caseating granulomas) appear as hypointense foci in the brain parenchyma. Communicating hydrocephalus results from obstruction of CSF reabsorption by basal exudate. CXR confirms active pulmonary TB — the likely source of CNS seeding.

prompts.label: CSF Analysis & Microbiological Workup
prompts.text: CSF: clear/slightly turbid, opening pressure 260 mmH₂O. WBC 380 cells/µL (predominantly lymphocytes — 85%). Protein: 1.9 g/L (elevated). Glucose: 1.8 mmol/L; blood glucose 5.4 mmol/L (CSF:blood ratio 0.33). Ziehl-Neelsen stain: acid-fast bacilli seen (low sensitivity). CSF GeneXpert MTB/RIF: positive for M. tuberculosis, no rifampicin resistance. ADA elevated. HIV test: reactive (CD4 count 142 cells/µL). Histology of tuberculoma: central caseating necrosis surrounded by epithelioid macrophages, Langhans giant cells, and peripheral lymphocytes — a classic caseating granuloma.
prompts.reasoning: TBM CSF profile: lymphocytic pleocytosis, elevated protein, very low glucose — differentiating from bacterial (neutrophilic) and viral (normal glucose) meningitis. ZN stain has poor sensitivity (~10-40%) but GeneXpert improves diagnostic yield. ADA (adenosine deaminase) is elevated in TBM. Pathologically, TBM causes a granulomatous basal exudate with caseating granulomas containing Langhans giant cells. HIV co-infection (CD4 142) markedly worsens prognosis. Treatment: 4-drug RIPE therapy × 9-12 months + dexamethasone (reduces mortality by 30%).

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 22-year-old female university student presents to the ED with a 24-hour history of severe headache, mild neck stiffness, and fever. She has been photophobic and has vomited twice.
prompts.reasoning: Headache, fever, and neck stiffness constitute the classic meningitic triad — bacterial meningitis must be urgently excluded as it is rapidly fatal. The 24-hour course is more consistent with viral than bacterial meningitis (bacterial usually fulminant over hours). SAH must be excluded as a cause of sudden severe headache with meningism.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: Headache built up over several hours — not thunderclap. She had a diarrhoeal illness with abdominal cramps 5 days ago that has since resolved. She has not had a rash. She is oriented and able to hold a conversation despite being uncomfortable. No ear pain, no sinus pain.
prompts.reasoning: A preceding gastroenteritis illness points to enteroviral infection — the most common cause of viral meningitis (enteroviruses account for ~80% of cases). The absence of a petechial rash reduces the likelihood of meningococcal disease. Preserved orientation with moderate symptoms is more consistent with viral than bacterial meningitis, though clinical distinction is unreliable — LP is required. Herpes simplex must be considered given its capacity to cause life-threatening encephalitis.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: No significant past medical history. Not immunocompromised. No prior meningitis. No contact with anyone with meningitis. No recent travel abroad. Not on any medications. Sexually active — not on the oral contraceptive pill. Had all childhood vaccinations including MenACWY.
prompts.reasoning: Viral meningitis is most common in young adults and immunocompetent patients. Enterovirus (Echovirus, Coxsackievirus) is the most common cause, followed by HSV-2 (in sexually active young adults — HSV-2 primary infection can cause aseptic meningitis). MenACWY vaccination makes meningococcal meningitis less likely. Normal immune status argues against cryptococcal or TBM.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Temperature 38.6°C, HR 102 bpm, BP 118/74 mmHg. GCS 15 — alert and oriented. Mild neck stiffness. Kernig's sign mildly positive. No petechial rash. No papilloedema. No focal neurological deficits. No rash anywhere on the body. Throat slightly erythematous.
prompts.reasoning: The preserved GCS 15 is reassuring but does not exclude bacterial meningitis. Absence of purpuric rash and haemodynamic stability reduce meningococcal septicaemia likelihood. However, empirical ceftriaxone should still be administered pending LP results in cases where bacterial meningitis cannot be excluded. Pharyngeal erythema suggests concurrent viral pharyngitis — consistent with enteroviral systemic infection.

prompts.label: CT Head
prompts.text: CT head (non-contrast): normal. No haemorrhage, no mass lesion, no hydrocephalus, no cerebral oedema. Safe to proceed to lumbar puncture.
prompts.reasoning: CT is required before LP in patients with impaired consciousness, focal neurological signs, papilloedema, or immunocompromised state to exclude mass lesion and raised ICP. This patient's normal CT permits LP. In viral meningitis, CT is typically normal — unlike bacterial meningitis where oedema or complications may be seen.

prompts.label: CSF Analysis & PCR
prompts.text: CSF: clear, colourless. Opening pressure 180 mmH₂O (mildly elevated). WBC 145 cells/µL (predominantly lymphocytes — 92%). Protein: 0.62 g/L (mildly elevated). Glucose: 3.4 mmol/L; blood glucose 5.2 mmol/L (CSF:blood ratio 0.65 — normal). Gram stain: no organisms. Culture: no growth. CSF PCR: Enterovirus positive (Echovirus 9). Bacterial cultures negative. HSV PCR negative.
prompts.reasoning: The CSF profile is diagnostic of viral (aseptic) meningitis: lymphocytic pleocytosis, mildly elevated protein, NORMAL glucose (key distinction from bacterial and TB meningitis where glucose is low). Gram stain negative and cultures negative — 'aseptic'. Enterovirus PCR confirms the aetiology. Histologically, viral meningitis shows a lymphocytic infiltrate in the subarachnoid space (not PMN exudate). Management is supportive — viral meningitis is self-limiting (7-10 days). Aciclovir is not indicated as HSV PCR is negative.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 38-year-old male presents with a 4-week history of progressive headache, which he describes as constant, dull, and generalised. He has had intermittent low-grade fever and has been feeling generally unwell.
prompts.reasoning: Subacute meningitis over several weeks with constitutional symptoms demands exclusion of opportunistic CNS infections. In a patient of this age, HIV status is critical — cryptococcal meningitis is the most common cause of meningitis in HIV-positive patients with low CD4 counts worldwide. TBM and CNS lymphoma are also important differentials in the immunocompromised.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The headache is worst when lying down and improves slightly when sitting upright. He has been increasingly confused over the past week. He reports no neck stiffness. He mentions he has lost 12 kg over the past 3 months. He has been having oral thrush treated at a local clinic. No cough, no rash.
prompts.reasoning: Subacute meningitis with raised ICP symptoms (headache worse lying down) plus oral candidiasis (an AIDS-defining illness) points strongly to an opportunistic CNS infection in an HIV-positive patient. Weight loss and candidiasis indicate advanced immunosuppression. Notably, cryptococcal meningitis often presents WITHOUT classic meningism (neck stiffness) — headache and altered consciousness dominate due to raised ICP.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: HIV-positive — diagnosed incidentally 5 years ago, declined antiretroviral therapy. Lives in Singapore. Works as a landscape gardener — regular exposure to soil and bird droppings. No prior CNS infections. No TB contact. Not on any medications. No significant family history.
prompts.reasoning: HIV with poor virological control (declined ART) is the single greatest risk factor for cryptococcal meningitis. Cryptococcus neoformans is acquired through inhalation of fungal spores from soil contaminated with pigeon/bird droppings (key occupation link). CNS involvement occurs via haematogenous spread in immunocompromised patients — primary pulmonary infection is usually mild and subclinical. CD4 count < 100 cells/µL strongly predisposes.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Temperature 37.8°C, HR 108 bpm, BP 110/70 mmHg. GCS 13 (E3V4M6). Bilateral papilloedema — Grade III. Minimal neck stiffness. Oral thrush visible. No focal neurological deficits. No meningococcal rash. CD4 count reported: 48 cells/µL. Fundoscopy: dilated retinal veins bilaterally.
prompts.reasoning: Severe papilloedema (Grade III) reflects markedly raised ICP — cryptococcal meningitis causes profound raised ICP through obstruction of CSF drainage by the mucoid polysaccharide capsule of Cryptococcus. CD4 count 48 confirms advanced AIDS. The paucity of classic meningism (neck stiffness is minimal) despite significant encephalopathy is characteristic of cryptococcal meningitis — the thick polysaccharide capsule partially blunts the inflammatory response.

prompts.label: CT Head
prompts.text: CT head (non-contrast): generalised cerebral oedema and mildly dilated ventricles (communicating hydrocephalus). Multiple small hypodense foci in the basal ganglia bilaterally — 'soap bubble' lesions or 'gelatinous pseudocysts'. No ring-enhancing lesions. Chest X-ray: bilateral interstitial infiltrates.
prompts.reasoning: The 'soap bubble' or 'gelatinous pseudocysts' in the basal ganglia on CT are characteristic of cryptococcal meningitis — they represent Cryptococcus organisms expanding the Virchow-Robin (perivascular) spaces with their mucoid capsular material (not true cysts). Communicating hydrocephalus from CSF outflow obstruction by polysaccharide capsule. No ring-enhancing lesions distinguishes this from cerebral toxoplasmosis, which also commonly presents in AIDS patients.

prompts.label: CSF Analysis & India Ink Stain
prompts.text: LP performed (with therapeutic drainage of 20 mL CSF for ICP reduction). Opening pressure: 420 mmH₂O (severely elevated). CSF: mildly turbid. WBC 20 cells/µL (lymphocytes). Protein: 0.85 g/L. Glucose: 2.8 mmol/L; blood glucose 5.6 mmol/L (ratio 0.5). India ink stain: encapsulated yeast cells with a large mucoid halo ('halo appearance') — positive. CSF Cryptococcal Antigen (CrAg): positive, titre 1:512 (very high). Culture: Cryptococcus neoformans (positive). Histology of the meninges: mucoid material with encapsulated yeast visible within perivascular spaces — minimal inflammatory response (due to immunosuppression).
prompts.reasoning: India ink stain demonstrates the characteristic capsulated yeast with a clear halo (capsule excludes ink). CSF CrAg (cryptococcal antigen) is the most sensitive and specific diagnostic test. Minimal CSF inflammation (low WBC) despite heavy fungal burden reflects the profound immunosuppression — the thick polysaccharide capsule impairs phagocytosis and blunts host immune response. Pathologically, the meninges show minimal inflammation ('gelatinous meningitis') due to the immunosuppressed state. Treatment: induction with amphotericin B + flucytosine, then fluconazole maintenance. Serial LP for ICP management.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 45-year-old male presents with a 2-week history of progressively worsening headache, left-sided arm weakness, and low-grade fever. Over the last 2 days he has developed confusion.
prompts.reasoning: Subacute focal neurological deficit with headache, fever, and confusion represents a space-occupying lesion that may be inflammatory or neoplastic. Cerebral abscess must be considered alongside brain tumour and subdural empyema. The presence of fever tilts the differential towards infectious aetiology. The timeline (weeks) is more consistent with abscess maturation than acute bacterial meningitis.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: He had a dental extraction 3 weeks ago. Prior to this, he had been having toothache in a lower molar for several months — and had noticed his gum was swollen. He reports no trauma. The weakness came on gradually over 5 days. He has had fevers measured at home (38.2°C). No neck stiffness, no rash.
prompts.reasoning: Dental infection (odontogenic) is a classic source of haematogenous cerebral abscess — oral streptococci (Streptococcus milleri group/Streptococcus anginosus) are the most common pathogens in dental-origin abscesses. Bacteria seed the brain haematogenously, typically lodging at the grey-white matter junction where the vessels narrow. Abscess formation follows: cerebritis → necrosis → pyogenic capsule formation. The 3-week timeline corresponds to the cerebritis-to-capsule maturation phase.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: History of congenital ventricular septal defect (small, not surgically corrected). No previous neurological problems. Non-smoker, drinks 2-3 units/week. Works as an accountant. No recent travel. No immunosuppression. Takes no regular medications. No family history of malignancy.
prompts.reasoning: A congenital cardiac defect (VSD) is a significant risk factor for cerebral abscess — right-to-left shunts allow venous bacteria to bypass pulmonary filtration and directly enter systemic/cerebral circulation. However, even a small VSD without haemodynamic significance can facilitate paradoxical embolism with bacteraemia. Other sources: otitis media, mastoiditis, sinusitis, lung abscess, septic emboli from infective endocarditis — all must be actively excluded.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Temperature 38.4°C, HR 96 bpm, BP 136/84 mmHg. GCS 13. Left arm weakness (power 3/5, UMN pattern). Left plantar extensor. Right-sided dental swelling noted. No neck stiffness. Fundoscopy: bilateral papilloedema (Grade I). Cardiac examination: grade 2/6 pansystolic murmur at lower left sternal edge.
prompts.reasoning: Focal UMN signs (left arm weakness, extensor plantar) localise to the right hemisphere. Papilloedema confirms raised ICP from a space-occupying lesion. The pansystolic murmur is consistent with the known VSD. Dental infection (right-sided swelling) confirms the odontogenic source. The triad of raised ICP (papilloedema, headache), focal deficits, and fever is classic for cerebral abscess — though only ~50% of patients have all three components.

prompts.label: CT Head with Contrast
prompts.text: CT head (with contrast): a 3.5 cm ring-enhancing lesion in the right frontal lobe at the grey-white junction, with a hypodense centre (necrosis/pus) and marked surrounding vasogenic oedema and mass effect. No satellite lesions. No haemorrhage. Midline shifted 5 mm to the left.
prompts.reasoning: Ring-enhancing lesion on CT is the hallmark of cerebral abscess — the ring represents the vascularised pyogenic capsule surrounding the central purulent core. However, glioblastoma and metastases can also ring-enhance. Abscess ring is typically smooth and thin-walled; GBM ring is thicker and more irregular. Vasogenic oedema and mass effect are common in all. MRI with diffusion-weighted imaging (DWI) is the key differentiator — abscess restricts diffusion (bright on DWI) due to viscous pus, while tumour necrosis does not.

prompts.label: MRI DWI & Surgical Drainage
prompts.text: MRI brain DWI: the ring-enhancing lesion shows restricted diffusion centrally (high signal on DWI, low ADC) — confirming pus within the abscess cavity. CT-guided stereotactic aspiration performed: 8 mL of creamy yellow-green pus aspirated. Culture: Streptococcus anginosus (Streptococcus milleri group). Histology of the wall: inner layer of necrotic debris and PMN neutrophils; middle layer of granulation tissue/fibroblasts (capsule); outer reactive gliosis. WBC: 18.4 × 10⁹/L. CRP: 148 mg/L. Echocardiogram: no vegetations.
prompts.reasoning: Restricted diffusion on DWI (bright DWI, dark ADC) is the key MRI feature distinguishing abscess from tumour — viscous pus with high cell density restricts water molecule diffusion. Streptococcus anginosus (milleri group) is the classic pathogen in dental/abdominal-origin cerebral abscesses. Histological stages of abscess: early cerebritis (oedema, necrosis) → late cerebritis → early capsule (fibroblast ring forms) → late capsule (thick collagen wall). Treatment: surgical drainage + 6-8 weeks IV antibiotics (penicillin + metronidazole for dental origin).

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 52-year-old female is brought to ED by her husband with a 3-day history of worsening headache, fever, and increasingly erratic behaviour. This morning she had a tonic-clonic seizure witnessed by her husband.
prompts.reasoning: Encephalitis (altered consciousness, behavioural change, seizure) with fever must be treated as HSV encephalitis until proven otherwise — it is the most common cause of fatal sporadic encephalitis in adults and is treatable. Delay in aciclovir initiation significantly worsens mortality. Autoimmune encephalitis (anti-NMDAR) is an important mimic, particularly in young women, but fever and rapid progression favour infectious cause.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The behavioural change preceded the headache — her husband noticed she seemed confused, repetitive in conversation, and had an unusual 'strange smell' sensation she described before each episode of confusion. Today's seizure involved lip-smacking and right arm automatisms before generalising. She has also had olfactory and gustatory hallucinations.
prompts.reasoning: Focal temporal lobe features — olfactory/gustatory hallucinations, complex partial seizures with lip-smacking automatisms, memory disturbance and personality change — precisely localise the pathology to the temporal lobe. HSV-1 has a predilection for the temporal lobes and limbic system, producing a necrotising haemorrhagic temporal lobe encephalitis. Olfactory auras and complex partial seizures are hallmarks of temporal lobe involvement.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: No significant past medical history. No immunosuppression. No history of herpes labialis (cold sores). No recent travel. No sick contacts. Not on any regular medications. Never had a prior seizure. No family history of epilepsy or autoimmune disease.
prompts.reasoning: HSV-1 encephalitis in adults typically arises from reactivation of latent HSV-1 in the trigeminal ganglion with retrograde spread along branches of CN V to the temporal and frontal lobes — NOT necessarily from active herpes labialis. Primary HSV-1 infection can also cause encephalitis. HSV-2 typically causes encephalitis in neonates (transmitted perinatally) and aseptic meningitis in adults during primary genital herpes infection.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Temperature 39.1°C, HR 116 bpm, BP 132/78 mmHg. GCS 11 (E3V3M5). Confused and disoriented. Neck stiffness present (mild). No focal limb weakness. Bilateral plantar responses equivocal. Post-ictal drowsiness noted. No skin rash, no oral herpetic ulcers. MMSE not assessable due to confusion.
prompts.reasoning: Encephalitis (altered consciousness + behavioural change) with fever and meningism. The absence of focal limb weakness reflects predominantly limbic/temporal involvement rather than motor cortex. Post-ictal drowsiness following the seizure explains the reduced GCS. IV aciclovir (10 mg/kg TDS) must be started IMMEDIATELY without waiting for LP or MRI results, as the mortality of untreated HSV encephalitis exceeds 70%.

prompts.label: MRI Brain
prompts.text: MRI brain (FLAIR and DWI): hyperintense signal change involving the left medial temporal lobe, hippocampus, insula, and inferior frontal lobe — with haemorrhagic foci seen on T2*. The changes spare the basal ganglia. EEG: periodic lateralised epileptiform discharges (PLEDs) over the left temporal region.
prompts.reasoning: Unilateral (or asymmetric bilateral) temporal lobe FLAIR hyperintensity — particularly involving the hippocampus, insula, and inferior frontal lobe — is the MRI signature of HSV encephalitis. Haemorrhagic foci on T2* reflect the necrotising haemorrhagic nature of HSV-1 encephalitis. Pathologically: haemorrhagic necrosis with Cowdry type A intranuclear inclusion bodies in neurons and astrocytes. EEG PLEDs over the temporal lobe strongly support the diagnosis.

prompts.label: CSF PCR & Histopathology
prompts.text: CSF: opening pressure 220 mmH₂O, 120 cells/µL (predominantly lymphocytes), protein 1.1 g/L, glucose 3.4 mmol/L (ratio 0.6). Red blood cells in CSF (xanthochromia absent — RBCs from haemorrhagic necrosis, not SAH). CSF HSV PCR: positive for HSV-1. CSF culture: negative for bacteria. If brain biopsy were performed (rarely needed): Cowdry type A intranuclear eosinophilic inclusion bodies in neurons, haemorrhagic necrosis, perivascular lymphocytic cuffing in the temporal lobe. Treatment: IV aciclovir 14-21 days; outcome dramatically improved with early treatment.
prompts.reasoning: CSF HSV PCR is the gold standard — sensitivity 96%, specificity 99%. RBCs in CSF (without xanthochromia) reflect haemorrhagic temporal lobe necrosis. Lymphocytic pleocytosis with elevated protein and normal glucose is typical of viral encephalitis. Pathological hallmark: Cowdry A intranuclear eosinophilic inclusions (representing viral replication) in neurons and astrocytes of the temporal lobe, with surrounding haemorrhagic necrosis and lymphocytic infiltration. Temporal lobe predilection is unique to HSV-1.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 44-year-old male with known HIV infection presents with a 3-month history of progressive cognitive decline, slowed thinking, difficulty with memory, and personality change. His partner reports he is no longer able to manage his finances or drive safely.
prompts.reasoning: Cognitive decline in an HIV-positive patient must prompt a systematic exclusion of opportunistic CNS infections and neoplasms before diagnosing HIV-associated neurocognitive disorder (HAND). Cryptococcal meningitis, cerebral toxoplasmosis, CMV encephalitis, primary CNS lymphoma, and neurosyphilis are all important treatable causes of cognitive decline in HIV.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The decline has been gradual with no fluctuation. No headache, no fever, no focal limb weakness. He has noticed slowed movement and difficulty with his handwriting over the past 2 months. He is taking no antiretroviral therapy (ART) — was started on therapy but stopped 18 months ago due to side effects. He reports decreased libido and social withdrawal.
prompts.reasoning: Insidious progressive subcortical dementia — cognitive slowing, psychomotor retardation, personality change, and motor slowing (bradykinesia) — is the clinical hallmark of HIV-associated dementia (HAD / HIV encephalopathy). This is predominantly a subcortical syndrome (affecting basal ganglia, deep white matter), unlike cortical dementias (Alzheimer's, FTD). Absence of ART for 18 months allows viral replication and cerebral injury. Concurrent neurosyphilis must be excluded by syphilis serology.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: HIV diagnosed 6 years ago. Nadir CD4 count 56 cells/µL. Currently not on ART (stopped 18 months ago). Sexually active with multiple partners — no condom use. Last CD4 and viral load not tested in 18 months. History of oral candidiasis and one previous episode of PCP pneumonia (AIDS-defining). No hepatitis B or C co-infection.
prompts.reasoning: Low nadir CD4 (56 cells/µL) confirms advanced AIDS. HIV encephalopathy (HIV-associated dementia) is classified as an AIDS-defining illness. The primary mechanism is direct HIV neurotoxicity via infected macrophages/microglia releasing neurotoxic cytokines (TNF-α, IL-1β) and viral proteins (gp120, Tat). HIV infects brain via infected monocytes crossing the blood-brain barrier ('Trojan horse' mechanism). ART cessation directly increases risk — ART reduces HIV-associated dementia by controlling viral replication in the CNS.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Temperature 37.2°C, BP 118/74 mmHg. GCS 15 but cognitively slow. MMSE 19/30 (attention and recall impaired). Psychomotor slowing evident. Mild bilateral hyperreflexia. Bilateral extensor plantars. Mild ataxic gait. No focal weakness. No meningism. No papilloedema. HIV viral load (today): 142,000 copies/mL. CD4 count: 38 cells/µL.
prompts.reasoning: Bilateral pyramidal signs (hyperreflexia, extensor plantars) with cognitive impairment and psychomotor retardation — without focal deficit or meningism — is the classic presentation of HIV encephalopathy. Bilateral UMN signs reflect diffuse white matter and corticospinal tract involvement. CD4 38 cells/µL confirms profound immunosuppression. HIV viral load 142,000 copies/mL confirms active replication. The absence of fever, headache, or focal signs makes acute opportunistic CNS infections less likely here.

prompts.label: MRI Brain
prompts.text: MRI brain (FLAIR): diffuse, bilateral, confluent white matter hyperintensity — predominantly in the deep and periventricular white matter, symmetric, without mass effect or contrast enhancement. Diffuse cortical and subcortical atrophy disproportionate to age. No ring-enhancing lesions. No restricted diffusion. MR spectroscopy: elevated choline and myo-inositol; reduced NAA (reflecting neuronal loss).
prompts.reasoning: Diffuse, symmetric, non-enhancing white matter FLAIR hyperintensity (HIV leukoencephalopathy) without mass effect is characteristic of HIV encephalopathy — distinguishing it from toxoplasmosis (ring-enhancing) and CNS lymphoma (periventricular enhancing lesions). MRS shows elevated choline (membrane turnover/inflammation), elevated myo-inositol (microglial activation), and reduced NAA (irreversible neuronal loss). Diffuse atrophy reflects widespread neuronal injury.

prompts.label: CSF Analysis & Histopathology
prompts.text: CSF analysis: mildly elevated protein (0.78 g/L), lymphocytic pleocytosis (25 cells/µL), normal glucose, negative bacterial culture. CSF HIV RNA: elevated (neurovirulent). Toxoplasma serology CSF: negative. CSF VDRL negative. EBV PCR: negative (excludes CNS lymphoma). Syphilis TPPA: negative. Histopathology (post-mortem findings in late-stage): microglial nodules, multinucleated giant cells (fused HIV-infected macrophages), diffuse white matter pallor (vacuolar myelopathy), and astrogliosis — without direct neuronal infection (HIV does not infect neurons directly).
prompts.reasoning: HIV encephalopathy is diagnosed by exclusion of treatable causes combined with positive CSF HIV RNA and imaging findings. The pathological hallmark is the multinucleated giant cell (fused HIV-infected macrophages/microglia). Vacuolar myelopathy affects the posterior and lateral spinal cord columns. HIV infects macrophages and microglia (CD4 expressed on these cells) — NOT neurons directly. Neuronal death occurs through indirect mechanisms: viral proteins (gp120, Tat), excitotoxicity, cytokines. Treatment: ART re-initiation is the definitive and most effective treatment.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 55-year-old male presents with a 6-month history of progressive gait difficulty — he is unsteady particularly in the dark. He has also noticed loss of sensation in his feet. His wife reports personality changes and memory lapses over the same period.
accepted: Subacute Combined Degeneration of Spinal Cord
prompts.reasoning: Progressive sensory ataxia (worse in dark — loss of proprioceptive input) with peripheral neuropathy and cognitive change is the classic presentation of tabes dorsalis and general paresis of the insane — two manifestations of tertiary neurosyphilis. Vitamin B12 deficiency (subacute combined degeneration) causes a similar picture and must be excluded. HIV co-infection and neurosyphilis frequently co-occur.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: He reports shooting (lancinating) pains in his legs occurring unpredictably, described as electric shock-like. He has had difficulty knowing where his feet are, and tends to walk looking at the ground. He has had no bowel or bladder problems. He recalls a painless penile ulcer approximately 20 years ago that resolved without treatment. No visual symptoms.
prompts.reasoning: The 'lightning pains' (tabetic crises) — sudden severe shooting pains in the legs — are pathognomonic of tabes dorsalis (posterior column disease from tertiary syphilis). The positive Romberg sign (worse in dark), sensory ataxia, and lancinating pains all localise to the posterior spinal columns and dorsal root ganglia. The history of a painless ulcer (likely a primary syphilitic chancre) 20 years ago, untreated, is the key epidemiological clue — neurosyphilis manifests 10-20 years after untreated primary infection.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: Never tested for syphilis or HIV. Multiple sexual partners throughout adulthood. No prior treatment for any sexually transmitted infection. Former commercial sex worker contact. No IV drug use. Drinks 2-3 units/week. Non-smoker. No known family history of neurological disease. Not on any medications.
prompts.reasoning: Neurosyphilis is caused by Treponema pallidum CNS infection occurring in tertiary syphilis (untreated). Risk factors: male sex, HIV co-infection (accelerates progression), multiple sexual partners, untreated primary infection. General paresis (frontal lobe dementia from meningoencephalitis) and tabes dorsalis (posterior column degeneration) are the classical late manifestations. HIV testing is mandatory as co-infection dramatically accelerates neurosyphilis and requires modified treatment protocols.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: GCS 15. MMSE 23/30 (frontal lobe tasks impaired). Argyll Robertson pupils — small, irregular pupils that accommodate but do not react to light ('near-far dissociation'). Loss of proprioception and vibration sense in the feet bilaterally. Romberg positive. High-stepping ('stamping') gait. Knee and ankle jerks absent. Power and tone normal. No UMN signs.
prompts.reasoning: Argyll Robertson pupils (prostitute's pupils — accommodate but do not react to light) are the iconic sign of neurosyphilis, caused by damage to the pretectal area (light reflex pathway) with preservation of the Edinger-Westphal nucleus (accommodation pathway). Loss of proprioception and vibration sense with areflexia and a positive Romberg sign localise to the dorsal root ganglia and posterior columns (tabes dorsalis). The stamping gait results from loss of proprioceptive feedback. UMN signs are absent in tabes (pure LMN/sensory).

prompts.label: MRI Spine & Brain
prompts.text: MRI spine (T2): posterior column atrophy with T2 hyperintensity in the dorsal columns of the thoracic and cervical cord. MRI brain: mild frontotemporal atrophy; leptomeningeal enhancement visible on gadolinium-enhanced images. No focal lesions, no plaques. Chest X-ray: normal.
prompts.reasoning: Posterior column T2 signal change on spinal MRI in tabes dorsalis reflects demyelination and axonal loss in the gracile and cuneate fasciculi. Leptomeningeal enhancement reflects active syphilitic meningitis underlying the parenchymal degeneration. Frontotemporal atrophy reflects general paresis (meningoencephalitis of tertiary syphilis). MRI findings must be combined with serology and CSF analysis for definitive diagnosis.

prompts.label: Serology & CSF Analysis
prompts.text: Serum VDRL: reactive (titre 1:32). Serum TPPA: positive. HIV test: negative. CSF analysis: lymphocytic pleocytosis (42 cells/µL), elevated protein (0.9 g/L), positive CSF VDRL (diagnostic of neurosyphilis). Serum vitamin B12: normal (excludes SCD). Glucose normal. Histopathology of tabes dorsalis (classical findings): posterior column demyelination with axonal loss, fibrosis of dorsal roots, and degeneration of dorsal root ganglia neurons.
prompts.reasoning: CSF VDRL positivity is the diagnostic gold standard for neurosyphilis — it is highly specific (though insensitive; a positive result confirms, but a negative does not exclude). TPPA confirms syphilis exposure (remains positive for life). Pathologically: tabes dorsalis — demyelination and atrophy of the posterior columns and dorsal root ganglia (Treponema invades and destroys the dorsal root ganglia neurons); general paresis — diffuse meningoencephalitis with cortical atrophy. Treatment: high-dose IV aqueous penicillin G (18-24 MU/day × 10-14 days). Early treatment can halt progression.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 36-year-old male with known HIV infection and a CD4 count of 62 cells/µL presents with a 10-day history of progressive right arm weakness and difficulty speaking. He has been increasingly drowsy.
prompts.reasoning: Focal neurological deficits (hemiparesis, aphasia) with progressive drowsiness in an HIV-positive patient with profound immunosuppression (CD4 62) demands urgent evaluation. In AIDS patients, focal brain lesions are caused by toxoplasmosis in approximately 50%, primary CNS lymphoma in 20-30%, and other opportunistic infections. CT/MRI ring-enhancing lesions in this context are toxoplasmosis until proven otherwise.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The arm weakness came on subacutely over 5-7 days. He has had mild headache throughout. He has been non-compliant with trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole (co-trimoxazole) prophylaxis for 4 months — he stopped it due to rash. He keeps two cats at home. He had a fever last week (38.8°C). No seizures.
prompts.reasoning: Non-compliance with co-trimoxazole prophylaxis (which covers both PCP and Toxoplasma) in a patient with CD4 < 100 directly predisposes to cerebral toxoplasmosis. Cat ownership is a key epidemiological risk factor — cats are the definitive host of Toxoplasma gondii, and humans acquire infection through ingestion of oocysts in cat faeces or undercooked meat. Cerebral toxoplasmosis results from reactivation of latent Toxoplasma cysts in the immunocompromised brain, not new infection.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: HIV diagnosed 5 years ago. Current ART: suboptimal — missed doses frequently. CD4 nadir: 22 cells/µL. Previous episode of PCP pneumonia 2 years ago. Toxoplasma IgG serology (from 2 years ago): positive (prior exposure/latent infection). Cat owner (2 cats). No recent foreign travel. No prior CNS disease. No IV drug use.
prompts.reasoning: Positive Toxoplasma IgG confirms latent infection — reactivation occurs when immunity fails (CD4 < 100). Cerebral toxoplasmosis almost exclusively represents reactivation of latent tissue cysts (bradyzoites in brain) in AIDS patients, not new infection. ART non-compliance allows CD4 decline. Primary prophylaxis (co-trimoxazole) must be continued until CD4 > 200 on ART. The key clinical dilemma is distinguishing toxoplasmosis from primary CNS lymphoma — both present with ring-enhancing brain lesions.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Temperature 38.6°C, HR 104 bpm, GCS 12. Right hemiparesis (arm power 2/5, leg 3/5, UMN pattern). Right plantar extensor. Mild aphasia (expressive). Bilateral papilloedema Grade I. No meningism. No rash. CD4 today: 62 cells/µL. HIV viral load: 85,000 copies/mL.
prompts.reasoning: Focal UMN deficits (right hemiparesis, expressive aphasia) localise to the left hemisphere. Papilloedema indicates raised ICP from cerebral oedema around lesions. Fever is more consistent with toxoplasmosis than CNS lymphoma (lymphoma is usually afebrile). The papilloedema and focal signs mandate urgent CT head. HIV viral load confirms poor viral control, consistent with CD4 suppression.

prompts.label: CT Head with Contrast & MRI
prompts.text: CT head (with contrast): multiple bilateral ring-enhancing lesions — largest 2.8 cm in left frontal lobe, additional lesions in basal ganglia and thalamus bilaterally. Surrounding vasogenic oedema and mass effect. MRI with gadolinium confirms multiple ring-enhancing lesions with the characteristic 'target sign' (concentric rings: central enhancement, surrounding necrosis, outer rim enhancement). Located at grey-white junction and basal ganglia.
prompts.reasoning: Multiple bilateral ring-enhancing lesions at the grey-white junction and basal ganglia in an AIDS patient with CD4 < 100 is the classic CT/MRI appearance of cerebral toxoplasmosis. The 'target sign' (concentric ring pattern) on MRI is characteristic. Basal ganglia predilection distinguishes toxoplasmosis from CNS lymphoma (which is typically periventricular). Multiple lesions favour toxoplasmosis; solitary lesion favours CNS lymphoma. In practice, empirical anti-toxoplasma treatment is started first — failure to respond within 2 weeks warrants brain biopsy.

prompts.label: Serology, CSF & Empirical Treatment Response
prompts.text: Toxoplasma IgG serum: positive (titre 1:1024). CSF (LP deferred due to raised ICP — given empirical therapy first): reserved.
