With normal imaging, confirmed recurrent aura fitting ICHD-3 criteria, and a normal examination, the diagnosis of migraine with aura is secure. COCP must be discontinued. Triptans are appropriate for acute treatment; prophylaxis if ≥2 attacks/month.

prompts.label: Management & Contraindications
prompts.text: COCP immediately discontinued — progesterone-only pill prescribed. Smoking cessation advice provided. Acute treatment: sumatriptan 50 mg (triptan) — effective. Avoid taking triptans during the aura phase (takes during headache phase only). Prophylaxis considered (topiramate). Patient counselled: migraine with aura is a contraindication to COCP, aetiology is cortical spreading depression (CSD), and aura symptoms are positive and 'marching' — distinguishing from TIA (sudden, negative symptoms). Recurrent attacks at 2/month — prophylaxis initiated.
prompts.reasoning: Key management points: COCP is absolutely contraindicated in migraine with aura — must be stopped. Triptans should not be used during the aura phase (theoretical risk of vasoconstriction during a potentially susceptible phase — though evidence is limited); they are effective during the headache phase. Cortical spreading depression (CSD) is the neurophysiological correlate of aura — a slow (3 mm/min) wave of depolarisation followed by suppression, moving across the occipital cortex. CSD activates trigeminal nociceptors in meningeal vessels, initiating headache. This distinguishes the pathophysiology from tension-type (no CSD) and cluster headache.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 35-year-old male office worker presents with a 3-month history of near-daily headaches. He describes them as a 'tight band' around his head, present most afternoons and evenings after prolonged computer work.
prompts.reasoning: Near-daily band-like headache in an office worker is a classic presentation of tension-type headache (TTH). The character ('tight band', 'pressure') and temporal pattern (afternoon onset, associated with computer work and stress) are characteristic. Migraine may co-exist (migrainous features must be specifically excluded). The frequency (near-daily) raises concern for medication overuse headache (MOH) if analgesics are being frequently used. Blood pressure must be measured to exclude hypertensive headache.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: Headaches are bilateral, non-pulsating, pressing/tightening quality, mild-to-moderate intensity. They do not worsen with routine physical activity. No nausea or vomiting. Mild light sensitivity during severe episodes but no phonophobia. Each episode lasts 4–8 hours. He can continue working through the headaches (not disabling). No aura. Paracetamol 1g used daily, providing partial relief.
prompts.reasoning: ICHD-3 criteria for episodic TTH: bilateral, pressing/tightening, mild-moderate, not aggravated by activity, with at most one of photophobia or phonophobia (NOT both), no nausea. This patient satisfies all criteria — bilateral pressing headache, mild-moderate, activity does not worsen it, no nausea. The distinction from migraine: TTH is bilateral (migraine usually unilateral), non-pulsating (migraine is pulsating), does not worsen with activity, no nausea. High-frequency TTH (≥10 days/month for >3 months) is this patient's pattern. Daily paracetamol use (>15 days/month) may be superimposing MOH.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: Works 10-hour days at a computer; poor ergonomic setup. Significant work-related stress. Poor sleep (5–6 hours/night). Regular coffee drinker — 5 cups/day. No personal or family history of migraine. Not on regular medications prior to starting paracetamol. Non-smoker. Normal BMI. No significant prior medical history.
prompts.reasoning: TTH is strongly associated with stress, poor sleep, muscle tension (pericranial myofascial tenderness from prolonged poor posture), and caffeine overuse/withdrawal. Pathophysiology involves sensitisation of pericranial myofascial nociceptors and central sensitisation of trigeminal pathways. Unlike migraine, TTH does not have a strong genetic basis. Caffeine withdrawal (when morning cups are delayed) can trigger morning headaches. Poor ergonomic posture (neck/shoulder muscle tension) → pericranial tenderness → TTH. Addressing these modifiable factors is central to management.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: BP 126/82 mmHg. Normal neurological examination. No papilloedema. Normal visual fields. No focal deficits. Bilateral pericranial muscle tenderness on palpation: temporal, masseter, upper trapezius, sternocleidomastoid, suboccipital muscles — generalised tenderness bilaterally. Forward head posture noted. Cervical range of motion mildly restricted. No neck stiffness (meningism). No carotid bruits.
prompts.reasoning: Manual palpation of pericranial muscles (frontal, temporal, masseter, suboccipital, SCM, trapezius) reveals increased tenderness — a characteristic finding in TTH that distinguishes it from migraine (where palpation may be normal). This tenderness reflects sensitised pericranial myofascial nociceptors. Forward head posture from prolonged computer use places increased mechanical load on posterior cervical muscles, perpetuating headache. Normal neurological examination and normal BP exclude secondary causes. Cervical restriction points to musculoskeletal contribution.

prompts.label: Investigations
prompts.text: TTH is a clinical diagnosis — neuroimaging not routinely required if headache is typical and examination is normal. Blood tests: FBC, ESR, TFTs, CRP — all normal. BP: normal on multiple measurements. Given his pattern, MOH is suspected — analgesia use confirmed at 20 days/month. Headache diary: confirms 22 headache days/month — meets criteria for chronic TTH and superimposed MOH. No imaging abnormality on MRI performed to provide reassurance (patient's request).
prompts.reasoning: Investigations are primarily to exclude secondary causes. MRI brain is indicated for new-onset, progressive, or atypical headache. Normal blood tests and MRI exclude inflammatory, infectious, neoplastic, and metabolic causes. Chronic TTH (≥15 headache days/month for >3 months) is now present, complicated by MOH (analgesic use ≥15 days/month for >3 months). MOH must be addressed first — abrupt or gradual withdrawal of overused analgesics is required before prophylactic treatment can be assessed.

prompts.label: Management
prompts.text: Non-pharmacological: sleep hygiene (target 7–8 hours), ergonomic workstation review, physiotherapy (neck/postural exercises, trigger point therapy), stress management, caffeine reduction (gradual, to avoid withdrawal). Analgesic withdrawal for MOH: paracetamol gradually withdrawn over 4 weeks — headache temporarily worsened then improved. Acute treatment: ibuprofen (used judiciously, not daily). Prophylaxis for chronic TTH: low-dose amitriptyline 10–25 mg nocte commenced — headache frequency reduced to 8 days/month at 3-month review.
prompts.reasoning: Management of TTH is multimodal. Non-pharmacological measures (sleep, exercise, stress reduction, physiotherapy for pericranial muscle tenderness) are equally as effective as pharmacology. Amitriptyline is the evidence-based prophylactic for chronic TTH — it reduces central pain sensitisation. MOH must be addressed before prophylaxis can be assessed. Simple analgesics (ibuprofen, aspirin, paracetamol) are appropriate for acute episodic TTH — but must be limited to <10–15 days/month to avoid MOH. Triptans are not effective in TTH (unlike migraine) — confirming the TTH diagnosis when triptans fail.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 34-year-old male smoker presents with a 3-week history of excruciating, strictly right-sided periorbital headaches. The pain comes on suddenly, peaks within minutes, and he cannot lie still during attacks — he paces the room.
prompts.reasoning: Cluster headache is the primary diagnosis in a male patient with severe, strictly unilateral periorbital pain, rapid onset, and restlessness (the patient cannot remain still — in contrast to migraine, where patients prefer to lie still in a dark room). The clustering pattern (attacks occurring at similar times each day, often nocturnal) should be specifically asked about. Trigeminal autonomic cephalalgias (TACs) share unilateral periorbital pain — distinguishing by attack duration and autonomic features is essential.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: Attacks occur every day, typically at 1 AM (waking from sleep), lasting 45–90 minutes each. He describes the pain as a 'red-hot poker through my right eye — the worst pain imaginable'. During attacks: right eye becomes red and watering (conjunctival injection, lacrimation), right nostril runs, and his right eyelid droops slightly (partial ptosis). He has been having this pattern for 3 weeks, then was pain-free for 8 months last year before it started again.
prompts.reasoning: This is a classic description of cluster headache: strictly unilateral orbital/periorbital pain, 15–180 minutes duration, ipsilateral autonomic features (lacrimation, conjunctival injection, rhinorrhoea, partial Horner's syndrome — ptosis ± miosis), and nocturnal preponderance (circadian pattern — attacks typically at the same time, often 1–2 AM, linked to hypothalamic activation). The episodic cluster pattern (3 weeks of daily attacks followed by 8 months of remission) is characteristic of episodic cluster headache. Cluster headache is far more common in males (3–5:1 male predominance).

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: Significant tobacco smoker (20 pack-year history). Drinks 1–2 beers/night usually; alcohol triggers an attack reliably during an active cluster period. Had a similar 3-week cluster period 18 months ago, self-resolved without medical attention. No prior diagnosis. No medications currently. Family history: father had 'terrible eye headaches'.
prompts.reasoning: Cluster headache is strongly associated with heavy cigarette smoking (>90% of cluster headache patients are or have been smokers). Alcohol is a potent and reliable trigger during an active cluster period (but not in remission) — any amount of alcohol can trigger an attack. The episodic pattern (cluster periods of 2–12 weeks, separated by remissions >1 month) defines episodic cluster headache (vs. chronic: no remission >1 month in 12 months). Family history (possible father affected) suggests a genetic component. The hypothalamus (suprachiasmatic nucleus) is the pathological 'pacemaker' of cluster headache — PET activation confirmed.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Examined during an attack (patient pacing in examination room, agitated, holding right eye). Right eye: severe conjunctival injection, profuse lacrimation. Right nasal congestion with ipsilateral rhinorrhoea. Right partial Horner's syndrome: ptosis and miosis (partial anhidrosis). Right forehead sweating. Severe right periorbital and temporal pain (pain score 10/10). Between attacks: completely normal neurological examination. No papilloedema. Normal fundoscopy.
prompts.reasoning: Ipsilateral cranial autonomic features (Horner's syndrome — ptosis, miosis; lacrimation; conjunctival injection; rhinorrhoea; forehead sweating) result from activation of the ipsilateral trigemino-parasympathetic reflex arc and sympathetic disruption at the cavernous sinus level. The unilateral Horner's in cluster headache is caused by pericarotid sympathetic fibre oedema/inflammation at the cavernous sinus. Importantly, the restlessness/agitation during attacks (patients pace, rock, or bang their head) contrasts sharply with migraine (patients prefer immobility). Normal interictal examination excludes structural disease.

prompts.label: MRI Brain
prompts.text: MRI brain (performed to exclude secondary cluster — first presentation requiring imaging): no cavernous sinus mass, no pituitary tumour, no cavernous sinus thrombosis. MRI pituitary: no adenoma. No trigeminal nerve lesion. No posterior fossa pathology. MRI hypothalamus: normal appearance (functional hypothalamic activation on PET in research studies, not evident on standard MRI). Diagnosis: primary episodic cluster headache confirmed.
prompts.reasoning: MRI is required for the first cluster period to exclude secondary causes: pituitary macroadenoma (can mimic cluster headache — important to exclude), cavernous sinus lesions, and posterior fossa pathology. Normal MRI confirms primary cluster headache. The hypothalamus (posterior-inferior) is the pathological generator of cluster headache — PET imaging shows ipsilateral posterior hypothalamic activation during cluster attacks (not visible on standard MRI). Cavernous sinus pathology should be excluded when Horner's syndrome is present.

prompts.label: Treatment
prompts.text: Acute attack treatment: 100% high-flow oxygen (15 L/min via non-rebreather mask) — complete pain relief in 15 minutes. Subcutaneous sumatriptan 6 mg — effective alternative (onset 5–10 min). Zolmitriptan nasal spray also effective. Transitional prevention: oral prednisolone 60 mg tapering course → complete cluster suppression within 5 days. Maintenance prevention: verapamil 240 mg twice daily commenced. Complete cluster remission at 6 weeks. Advised: complete smoking cessation and alcohol abstinence during active cluster periods.
prompts.reasoning: Key treatment points: acute — 100% oxygen (the most effective acute treatment for cluster headache, free of side effects, mechanism uncertain — possibly via cerebral vasoconstriction or direct trigeminal effect) and triptans (SC sumatriptan fastest acting). Prevention — verapamil (first-line) works via calcium channel blockade (dose 240–960 mg/day); ECG monitoring required. Prednisolone provides rapid bridge to verapamil onset. Newer: galcanezumab (anti-CGRP) and sphenopalatine ganglion (SPG) neurostimulation (non-invasive). Chronic cluster headache is the most disabling primary headache — lithium or occipital nerve stimulation may be needed.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 72-year-old woman presents with a 3-week history of new-onset severe right-sided temporal headache and jaw pain when chewing. She has also had a 6 kg weight loss and marked fatigue over the past 2 months.
prompts.reasoning: New-onset temporal headache in an elderly patient must be treated as giant cell arteritis (GCA) until proven otherwise — the most feared complication is irreversible visual loss from anterior ischaemic optic neuropathy (AION), occurring in up to 20% of untreated patients. Jaw claudication (pain on chewing from ischaemia of the masseter supplied by the facial artery — a branch of the external carotid) is highly specific for GCA. Constitutional symptoms (weight loss, fatigue) suggest significant systemic inflammation. GCA almost exclusively affects patients >50 years.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: She describes the temporal headache as constant, severe, and throbbing — 'my scalp hurts to touch, even brushing my hair is painful' (scalp tenderness, allodynia). She has transient visual blurring in her right eye twice this week — 'like a grey curtain' lasting seconds. She has bilateral proximal shoulder and hip girdle aching ('polymyalgia') and morning stiffness lasting >1 hour.
prompts.reasoning: Transient monocular visual loss ('grey curtain' — amaurosis fugax) in a patient with GCA is a medical emergency — it is a warning sign of impending permanent visual loss from AION. Immediate high-dose corticosteroids must be started without waiting for biopsy. Scalp tenderness and allodynia reflect temporal artery inflammation. PMR (proximal girdle myalgia + morning stiffness) coexists with GCA in 40–60% of cases, sharing the same pathological mechanism (giant cell arteritis of medium vessels). The combination of headache + PMR symptoms + visual symptoms in an elderly woman is classic.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: No prior headaches. No history of migraine. Previously healthy. Has been taking ibuprofen with minimal effect. No diabetes, no immunosuppression. Non-smoker. Lives alone — has been unable to comb her hair or raise her arms above her head due to shoulder pain. Has not seen her GP about the shoulder pain (attributed to 'old age'). No family history of vasculitis.
prompts.reasoning: GCA is a granulomatous vasculitis of medium-to-large vessels in patients over 50 years (peak 70–80 years). It predominantly affects the external carotid branches (temporal, ophthalmic, posterior ciliary arteries). The inflammation causes intimal thickening, stenosis/occlusion, and vessel wall ischaemia. Risk factors: female sex (3:1), Northern European ancestry, HLA-DRB1*04. NSAIDs are ineffective — only corticosteroids suppress the granulomatous inflammation. The inability to raise arms (proximal shoulder weakness from PMR — inflammatory myalgia, not true weakness) is classic.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Right temporal artery: palpably tender, thickened, nodular, and non-pulsatile — a 'cord-like' feel. Left temporal artery: mildly tender, reduced pulsation. Scalp exquisitely tender to palpation. Jaw claudication reproduced by bilateral mouth opening for 30 seconds. Right eye visual acuity 6/18 (left 6/6). Right afferent pupillary defect (RAPD). Fundoscopy right eye: swollen pale optic disc with flame haemorrhages (anterior ischaemic optic neuropathy, AION). ESR and CRP urgently requested.
prompts.reasoning: Findings of a non-pulsatile, tender, cord-like temporal artery are essentially diagnostic of GCA. The right RAPD and pale swollen disc confirm AION — ischaemic infarction of the optic nerve head from occlusion of the posterior ciliary arteries. This requires IMMEDIATE high-dose IV methylprednisolone 500 mg–1 g to prevent progression to complete blindness in the contralateral eye. Once AION occurs, visual recovery is poor. This is a same-day ophthalmology and rheumatology emergency. Jaw claudication (ischaemia of masseter) has very high specificity for GCA (>90%).

prompts.label: Blood Tests & Biopsy
prompts.text: ESR: 108 mm/hr (markedly elevated; normal <20 mm/hr). CRP: 112 mg/L (markedly elevated). Hb: 98 g/L (normocytic anaemia of chronic inflammation). Platelets: 480 × 10⁹/L (reactive thrombocytosis). ALP mildly elevated. Temporal artery biopsy (performed after steroids started — steroids do not affect biopsy yield for 2–4 weeks): transmural granulomatous arteritis — fragmented internal elastic lamina, infiltrate of CD4+ T lymphocytes, macrophages, and multinucleated giant cells in the media and intima.
prompts.reasoning: Markedly elevated ESR (usually >50, often >100) and CRP are characteristic — 'a patient >50 years with a new headache and ESR >50 is GCA until proven otherwise'. Biopsy of the temporal artery is the gold standard — skip lesions mean a long segment (≥2 cm) must be taken. Histology shows transmural granulomatous inflammation: giant cells, lymphocytes, macrophages, fragmentation of the internal elastic lamina (pathognomonic). Steroids do not affect biopsy for 2–4 weeks — biopsy should not delay treatment. Normal biopsy does not exclude GCA (~30% false negative). Temporal artery USS (hypoechoic 'halo' sign) is a non-invasive alternative.

prompts.label: Treatment & Monitoring
prompts.text: Treatment: IV methylprednisolone 1 g/day × 3 days (given ocular involvement), then oral prednisolone 60 mg/day. Right eye visual acuity: no improvement (established AION). Left eye: preserved. Aspirin 75 mg added (reduces risk of stroke and visual loss in GCA). CRP normalises within 2 weeks. ESR normalising. Gradual prednisolone taper over 18–24 months. PPI (omeprazole) and bisphosphonate (alendronate) prescribed for steroid osteoporosis prophylaxis. Tocilizumab (IL-6 receptor antagonist) commenced as steroid-sparing agent — allows faster taper, reduces relapse rate.
prompts.reasoning: High-dose steroids are the cornerstone of treatment — must be started IMMEDIATELY when GCA is suspected, especially with visual symptoms (do not wait for biopsy). Aspirin reduces the risk of ischaemic events. AION: once established, visual loss is largely irreversible — the optic disc infarcts. The other eye must be protected urgently with steroids. Tocilizumab (anti-IL-6R) is approved for relapsing/refractory GCA — it allows faster steroid tapering. Steroid complications (osteoporosis, diabetes, adrenal suppression) require prophylaxis. Median treatment duration: 2 years. GCA relapse rate ~50% during steroid taper.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 28-year-old obese woman (BMI 38 kg/m²) presents with a 6-week history of daily generalised headaches, worse on waking and with Valsalva. She reports transient visual obscurations lasting seconds that occur several times per day.
prompts.reasoning: The triad of headache + transient visual obscurations + obesity in a young woman is the classic presentation of idiopathic intracranial hypertension (IIH), also called pseudotumour cerebri. Transient visual obscurations (TVOs) — momentary greying/blurring of vision lasting 1–5 seconds — are caused by transient ischaemia of the optic nerve head from papilloedema. IIH occurs predominantly in obese women of childbearing age. Cerebral venous thrombosis must be urgently excluded as a secondary cause of raised ICP.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The headache is diffuse, pressure-like, worse in the morning. She reports a new bilateral horizontal diplopia when looking to the side. She has a pulsatile tinnitus — a whooshing sound in both ears synchronous with her pulse. She has gained 12 kg over the past 18 months. She recently started tetracycline (doxycycline) for acne 3 months ago.
prompts.reasoning: Bilateral CN VI palsy (diplopia on lateral gaze) is a false localising sign of raised ICP — CN VI has the longest intracranial course and is most susceptible to stretch from raised ICP. Pulsatile tinnitus (bruit synchronous with pulse) is caused by transmission of arterial pulsations through the compressed jugular veins (transverse sinus stenosis is a hallmark of IIH). Doxycycline (tetracycline class) is a well-recognised cause of drug-induced IIH — other causes: vitamin A/retinoids, steroids (withdrawal), growth hormone. Weight gain is the strongest risk factor.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: BMI 38 kg/m². No prior neurological conditions. Started oral minocycline (tetracycline) for acne 3 months ago — symptoms coincide with initiation. Not pregnant. No oral contraceptive use currently. No vitamin A supplements. No history of venous thrombosis. No thyroid disease. Irregular menstrual periods. No prior headaches. Takes no other medications.
prompts.reasoning: IIH risk factors: female sex, obesity (particularly with recent rapid weight gain), childbearing age, tetracyclines (doxycycline, minocycline), vitamin A excess, COCP, steroids (withdrawal). The tetracycline should be stopped immediately (may contribute to IIH — mechanism unclear, possibly impaired CSF reabsorption). Irregular periods (polycystic ovary syndrome — common in obese young women) are associated with IIH. IIH diagnosis is by exclusion: secondary causes (CVT, dural AV fistula, venous obstruction, mass lesion) must be excluded first.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: BMI 38 kg/m². BP 128/80 mmHg. Bilateral papilloedema (Frisén grade 3 — obscuration of major vessels at disc margin, grade 3 haemorrhages). Bilateral CN VI palsy — limited right and left lateral gaze. Visual fields (confrontation): enlarged blind spot bilaterally, early peripheral constriction in right eye. Visual acuity: 6/9 right, 6/6 left. No other focal neurological deficits. No neck stiffness.
prompts.reasoning: Bilateral papilloedema is the cardinal sign of IIH — disc swelling from axoplasmic flow stasis in the optic nerve head caused by raised CSF pressure. Grading by Frisén scale (1–5). Bilateral CN VI palsy is a false localising sign of raised ICP (not indicating pontine pathology). Visual field loss (enlarged blind spot, peripheral constriction) reflects optic nerve damage from chronic papilloedema — if progressive, causes permanent vision loss. Visual acuity is initially preserved but visual field loss indicates irreversible optic nerve damage requiring urgent treatment.

prompts.label: MRI Brain & MRV
prompts.text: MRI brain: no mass lesion, no hydrocephalus (key finding — raised ICP without hydrocephalus or mass lesion). Posterior globe flattening (scleral flattening from raised CSF pressure transmitted via optic nerve sheath). Empty sella (herniation of subarachnoid space into sella from raised ICP — flattens pituitary). Dilated optic nerve sheaths bilaterally. MRV (magnetic resonance venography): bilateral transverse sinus stenosis (commonly seen in IIH — may be cause or consequence of raised ICP). No cerebral venous thrombosis.
prompts.reasoning: MRI is essential to exclude secondary causes (mass lesion, hydrocephalus, CVT). The characteristic MRI findings of IIH: empty sella, posterior globe flattening, dilated optic nerve sheaths, and transverse sinus stenosis. The empty sella is a useful positive finding. Absent hydrocephalus with raised ICP and papilloedema is the key radiological 'pseudotumour' feature. MRV excludes CVT. Transverse sinus stenosis is present in ~90% of IIH — whether it is a cause (reduced venous drainage → raised ICP) or effect (raised ICP compresses sinuses) is debated.

prompts.label: Lumbar Puncture & Management
prompts.text: LP (after MRI excluded contraindications): opening pressure 38 cmH₂O (normal <25 cmH₂O). CSF: crystal clear, normal glucose, normal protein, no cells — normal CSF composition confirming IIH. Doxycycline stopped immediately. Weight loss programme initiated — 10 kg weight loss target. Acetazolamide 500 mg twice daily commenced (reduces CSF production by carbonic anhydrase inhibition). Topiramate added (also reduces CSF production + weight loss effect). Visual fields formal perimetry: monitored 4-weekly. MRI optic nerve sheath diameter: reducing at 3 months.
prompts.reasoning: LP is both diagnostic and therapeutic: opening pressure >25 cmH₂O in the lateral decubitus position + normal CSF composition + no mass lesion = IIH. Immediate LP can provide symptom relief. Acetazolamide (carbonic anhydrase inhibitor) reduces choroid plexus CSF production — first-line medical treatment. Weight loss is the most effective long-term treatment — 5–10% weight loss can cause lasting remission. If medical treatment fails or progressive visual loss occurs: optic nerve sheath fenestration (direct decompression) or CSF diversion (VP shunt, LP shunt). Venous sinus stenting is emerging for cases with significant transverse sinus stenosis.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 40-year-old woman with a 10-year history of migraine presents complaining that her headaches have become daily over the past 8 months and are no longer responding to her usual medications. She is taking sumatriptan and codeine almost every day.
prompts.reasoning: The transformation from episodic migraine to daily headache in a patient overusing analgesics/triptans is the classic presentation of medication overuse headache (MOH — also called analgesic rebound headache). MOH is defined as headache on ≥15 days/month in a patient using acute headache medication on ≥10–15 days/month for >3 months. The paradox is that the medications used to treat headache perpetuate and worsen it when overused. Chronic migraine and MOH can coexist.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: She now uses sumatriptan on 12 days/month and codeine/paracetamol on 18 days/month — total medication use 22 days/month. The headache is present every morning on waking ('alarm clock headache') and quickly relieved by taking medication. Without medication, the headache escalates to severe migraine-like pain within hours. She has tried to reduce her medication but experiences 'withdrawal headache' lasting several days.
prompts.reasoning: The pattern of daily morning headaches (rebound from overnight analgesic washout) that rapidly respond to the overused medication is pathognomonic of MOH. The physiological mechanism involves: (1) downregulation of central serotonin receptors from triptan overuse — increasing sensitisation and headache frequency; (2) upregulation of CGRP and other neuropeptides; (3) central sensitisation of the trigeminovascular system. Opioids (codeine) cause the most severe MOH — they should be avoided in headache management. Withdrawal headache (3–10 days of worsened headache on stopping) must be anticipated and managed.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: 10-year history of episodic migraine (originally 2–3 attacks/month, well-controlled). After a particularly stressful work period 2 years ago, attacks increased to 5–6/month. She started using triptans more frequently and added codeine for 'breakthrough' pain. No GP review in 18 months — self-medicating. Anxious personality. Non-smoker. Works as a nurse — night shifts. Regular caffeine consumer.
prompts.reasoning: MOH risk factors: pre-existing primary headache disorder (migraine, TTH), frequent analgesic use, psychological factors (anxiety, depression), female sex. MOH threshold: opioids and triptans (≥10 days/month for ≥3 months) carry higher MOH risk than simple analgesics (≥15 days/month). Caffeine overuse and withdrawal are contributing factors. Night shifts disrupt circadian rhythms, worsening migraine. The escalating analgesic use following a period of stress — transforming episodic to chronic daily headache — is a very common clinical trajectory.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Normal neurological examination. BP 120/78 mmHg. No papilloedema. Normal fundoscopy. No neck stiffness. Bilateral mild pericranial tenderness on manual palpation. No focal deficits. Mild anxiety evident. Headache diary reviewed: 22 headache days in last month, medication used on 22 days (sumatriptan 12, codeine 10, paracetamol additional 10).
prompts.reasoning: A normal neurological examination is expected in MOH. Normal fundoscopy excludes raised ICP. The headache diary is the essential diagnostic tool — it quantifies medication use days and headache days. The ICHD-3 diagnosis of MOH requires headache ≥15 days/month + medication overuse (triptans/opioids ≥10 days/month, or simple analgesics ≥15 days/month, for >3 months) + headache developed during or worsened by overuse. No imaging is required unless atypical features are present.

prompts.label: Investigations
prompts.text: No neuroimaging required (typical presentation, normal examination). Blood tests: FBC, CRP, ESR, TFTs — all normal (to exclude secondary causes). PHQ-9 depression screen: mild-moderate depression (score 11/27). GAD-7 anxiety screen: moderate anxiety (score 12/21). Headache diary (4 weeks): confirms ≥22 medication days/month. ICHD-3 criteria for MOH confirmed. Diagnosis: medication overuse headache superimposed on chronic migraine.
prompts.reasoning: MOH is a clinical diagnosis — no specific investigation is diagnostic. Blood tests exclude thyroid disease, anaemia, and inflammatory disease. Psychological comorbidity (depression, anxiety) is common in MOH and must be identified and treated. The headache diary provides the diagnostic evidence. Once MOH is diagnosed, the primary treatment is analgesic withdrawal — without withdrawal, prophylactic medications cannot work (MOH blunts the response to all prophylactics). Psychological support and CBT improve outcomes.

prompts.label: Management & Withdrawal
prompts.text: Medication withdrawal: abrupt discontinuation of codeine and triptans agreed (abrupt preferred for triptans; gradual for opioids). Withdrawal headache warned: peaks at days 3–7, resolves within 4 weeks. Prednisolone 60 mg tapering (5-day course) used to manage severe withdrawal headache. Migraine prophylaxis: topiramate 100 mg/day commenced after withdrawal. CBT referral for anxiety and depression. 3-month follow-up: headache frequency reduced from 22 to 6 days/month. Triptans reintroduced with strict limit of ≤10 days/month.
prompts.reasoning: Analgesic withdrawal is the cornerstone of MOH treatment — without it, prophylaxis fails. Withdrawal should be monitored and supported. A short corticosteroid bridge can ease the withdrawal period (reduces the severity of rebound headache). After successful withdrawal, MOH resolves in 50–80% of patients. Prophylaxis (topiramate, amitriptyline, propranolol) is commenced after withdrawal and is much more effective once MOH is resolved. Patient education is essential — patients must understand the counter-intuitive concept that their medications are perpetuating the headaches. Relapse rate is ~45% at 5 years.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 38-year-old man presents with a 5-day history of progressive bilateral leg weakness and difficulty walking. The weakness began in his feet and has been ascending to his thighs. He also reports tingling and numbness in both feet.
prompts.reasoning: Progressive ascending weakness with distal sensory symptoms over days is the classic presentation of Guillain-Barré syndrome (GBS) — an acute inflammatory demyelinating polyradiculoneuropathy. The ascending pattern (distal to proximal, bilateral, symmetric) is key. Spinal cord lesions (transverse myelitis, cauda equina) must be excluded — they would cause UMN signs and a sensory level. The rate of progression and symmetry help distinguish GBS from other causes of acute weakness.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: Three weeks ago he had a 5-day illness with diarrhoea — diagnosed as gastroenteritis, suspected Campylobacter jejuni. The leg weakness began 2 weeks after resolution of the diarrhoea. He now has difficulty standing from a chair (proximal weakness) and his arms are becoming weak. He has bilateral facial droop developing today and difficulty swallowing. No back pain, no bladder or bowel dysfunction.
prompts.reasoning: Post-infectious onset 2–4 weeks after Campylobacter jejuni gastroenteritis is the most common trigger for GBS — molecular mimicry between C. jejuni lipopolysaccharide ganglioside-like epitopes (GM1, GD1a) and peripheral nerve myelin antigens triggers an autoimmune attack. C. jejuni is most strongly associated with the axonal variant (AMAN). Ascending weakness progressing to facial involvement (CN VII) and bulbar involvement (dysphagia) suggests rapid progression. Absent bladder/bowel dysfunction (in contrast to cauda equina syndrome) is characteristic.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: The Campylobacter gastroenteritis was confirmed by stool culture. He had no prior neurological conditions. No vaccinations in the past 6 weeks. No HIV risk factors. Not immunosuppressed. Previously fit and healthy. Works as a plumber. No family history of neuromuscular disease. No medications. No alcohol or drug use.
prompts.reasoning: GBS is triggered by various infections: Campylobacter jejuni (most common), CMV, EBV, Zika virus, COVID-19. Vaccines rarely trigger GBS (1–2/million for influenza vaccine — risk vastly outweighed by benefit). HIV can cause GBS-like syndrome. GBS may also be triggered by surgery or pregnancy. The autoimmune mechanism (molecular mimicry) explains the 2–4 week latency between infection and neurological onset — the time required for an adaptive immune response to be generated and then attack peripheral nerve components.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Bilateral facial weakness (CN VII — unable to close eyes completely, bilateral). Dysarthria and mild dysphagia. Upper limbs: bilateral proximal and distal weakness (MRC grade 4/5 in arms). Lower limbs: bilateral flaccid weakness (MRC grade 3/5 in legs, 2/5 in feet). Absent ankle and knee reflexes bilaterally. Absent biceps reflexes. Reduced pain sensation in both feet (stocking distribution). Plantar responses: downgoing (absent). BP 148/92 mmHg lying; 122/76 mmHg standing (autonomic instability — postural hypotension).
prompts.reasoning: The key examination findings: (1) Areflexia (absent deep tendon reflexes) — loss of the afferent arc (Ia fibres in peripheral nerve demyelination) is the hallmark; (2) Ascending flaccid paralysis (LMN pattern); (3) Bilateral facial nerve palsy (peripheral CN VII involvement); (4) Autonomic instability (postural hypotension, tachycardia from autonomic fibre involvement). Autonomic dysfunction in GBS is dangerous — arrhythmias and blood pressure instability are life-threatening. Respiratory function (FVC) must be monitored urgently.

prompts.label: CSF Analysis & Nerve Conduction Studies
prompts.text: LP: opening pressure normal. CSF: clear. WBC 3 cells/µL (normal). Protein: 3.8 g/L (markedly elevated — normal <0.45 g/L). Glucose: normal. Albuminocytological dissociation: high protein with normal/low cell count — pathognomonic of GBS. Nerve conduction studies (NCS): markedly reduced conduction velocities, prolonged F-waves and distal latencies, conduction block at multiple sites — consistent with demyelinating GBS (AIDP subtype). Anti-GM1 antibodies: weakly positive. FVC: 1.6 L (62% of predicted — monitoring threshold for ventilation is <20 mL/kg or 1–1.2 L).
prompts.reasoning: Albuminocytological dissociation (CSF protein very high, cell count normal) is the pathognomonic CSF finding of GBS — in the first week it may be absent (protein rises 1–2 weeks after symptom onset). NCS is the most informative investigation: AIDP (demyelinating subtype) shows conduction slowing, conduction block, prolonged F-waves; AMAN (axonal subtype, Campylobacter-associated) shows reduced amplitude without slowing. FVC monitoring is critical — intubation required if FVC <20 mL/kg. Treatment: IVIg (0.4 g/kg/day × 5 days) or plasma exchange — equivalent efficacy. Steroids alone are NOT effective in GBS.

prompts.label: ICU Monitoring & Treatment
prompts.text: Admitted to ICU for monitoring. FVC declined to 1.2 L over 24 hours — intubated (mechanical ventilation). Continuous cardiac monitoring: paroxysmal bradycardia and brief asystole (4 seconds) from autonomic instability — temporary pacemaker standby. IVIg commenced: 0.4 g/kg/day × 5 days. At 3 weeks: FVC improving, extubated at day 22. At 3 months: able to walk with aids. At 6 months: full recovery. Anti-GQ1b antibodies: negative (not Miller Fisher variant). Subtypying: AIDP (demyelinating — best prognosis).
prompts.reasoning: GBS is a medical emergency — respiratory failure from diaphragm/intercostal muscle paralysis occurs in 25% of patients. Autonomic instability (life-threatening arrhythmias, blood pressure swings) requires ICU monitoring. IVIg and plasmapheresis are equally effective; combination is NOT more effective. Steroids alone are contraindicated. Prognosis: 85% fully recover, 10–15% have residual disability, 3–5% mortality. Recovery is slow (demyelination repairs over months). Poor prognostic factors: age >60, rapid progression, axonal subtype, preceding C. jejuni infection (AMAN variant). GBS variants: Miller Fisher (anti-GQ1b — ophthalmoplegia, ataxia, areflexia), Bickerstaff brainstem encephalitis.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 58-year-old man with a 15-year history of type 2 diabetes presents with a 2-year history of numbness, tingling, and burning pain in both feet. The symptoms are worst at night and are interfering with his sleep.
prompts.reasoning: Length-dependent distal symmetric sensory neuropathy ('glove-and-stocking') is the most common form of peripheral neuropathy. In a diabetic patient of 15 years, diabetic sensorimotor polyneuropathy (DSPN) is by far the most common cause. The bilateral symmetrical distribution, onset in the feet, nocturnal worsening, and burning/neuropathic character are classic for small fibre sensory neuropathy. Other common causes — B12 deficiency, alcohol, hypothyroidism — must be screened for.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The numbness started in his toes and has crept up to just above his ankles over 2 years. He describes a 'burning, electric shock' pain especially at night. He cannot feel the floor when he walks and has had two fall episodes. He drinks 4–5 units of alcohol daily and admits to poor glycaemic control (HbA1c 94 mmol/mol — 10.7%). He has noticed painless foot ulceration.
prompts.reasoning: The ascending glove-and-stocking distribution, neuropathic pain quality (burning, electric shock), loss of protective sensation (leading to painless ulceration and falls), nocturnal exacerbation, and poor glycaemic control (HbA1c 10.7% — well above target) in a 15-year diabetic is the classic picture of diabetic sensorimotor polyneuropathy. Alcohol (4–5 units/day — above safe limits) is an additional aetiological factor. Painless foot ulceration is a serious complication of loss of protective sensation.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: Type 2 diabetes for 15 years — poorly controlled (HbA1c persistently >90 mmol/mol). Hypertension. Dyslipidaemia. On metformin, glibenclamide, and atorvastatin (note: rare statin neuropathy). Alcohol: 35 units/week (heavy use — alcoholic neuropathy contributing). Non-smoker. No family history of neuropathy. Prior examination by diabetic foot team: absent ankle reflexes noted 2 years ago. No vitamin supplements taken.
prompts.reasoning: Multiple contributing factors for peripheral neuropathy: (1) Diabetes — DSPN pathogenesis: hyperglycaemia → advanced glycation end-products (AGEs), oxidative stress, sorbitol accumulation (polyol pathway) → axonal degeneration and Schwann cell dysfunction. (2) Alcohol — direct axonal toxicity and nutritional (B1, B12) deficiency. (3) Statins — rarely cause neuropathy (must consider but far less common than diabetic cause here). Duration and severity of hyperglycaemia are the strongest risk factors for DSPN.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Bilateral absent ankle reflexes. Reduced/absent knee reflexes. Absent vibration sense at toes, ankles, and shins bilaterally. Absent joint position sense at toes. Reduced pain and temperature sensation — 'glove and stocking' to mid-shin bilaterally. Preserved power (mild distal weakness: toe extensors MRC 4/5). Bilateral plantar ulcers (1.5 cm right heel, 0.8 cm left 1st toe head — Wagner grade 1 — painless). Skin of feet: dry, warm, loss of sweating (small fibre/autonomic involvement). Charcot neuroarthropathy: right foot deformity.
prompts.reasoning: Loss of ankle reflexes (affecting the largest, most vulnerable nerve fibres — Ia afferents, A-beta fibres) is the earliest objective sign of DSPN. The 'glove-and-stocking' sensory loss pattern reflects length-dependent axon loss — longest axons (to feet) affected first and most severely. Painless ulcers are caused by loss of protective sensation (pain fibres — small C and A-delta fibres). Charcot neuroarthropathy results from loss of proprioception and pain leading to repeated unrecognised joint trauma. Dry, anhidrotic skin reflects sudomotor fibre (small autonomic fibre) involvement.

prompts.label: Blood Tests & Nerve Conduction Studies
prompts.text: HbA1c: 94 mmol/mol (10.7%). Fasting glucose: 14.2 mmol/L. Vitamin B12: 180 pmol/L (borderline low — metformin causes B12 deficiency). Folate: normal. TFTs: normal. Renal function: eGFR 52 ml/min (CKD stage 3 — uraemic neuropathy contributing). LFTs: elevated AST/ALT (alcoholic hepatitis). Serum protein electrophoresis: no paraprotein. Nerve conduction studies: reduced sural nerve SNAP amplitude (axonal sensory loss), mildly reduced conduction velocity. H-reflexes absent bilaterally (surrogate of ankle reflexes). EMG: chronic neurogenic changes in distal leg muscles.
prompts.reasoning: NCS confirms axonal sensorimotor polyneuropathy (reduced SNAP amplitudes — axonal loss; mildly reduced CV — some demyelination from AGE damage to myelin). The pattern (axonal > demyelinating, length-dependent, sensorimotor) is typical of DSPN. B12 borderline low from metformin (metformin impairs ileal B12-intrinsic factor absorption) — B12 supplementation should be commenced. Multiple contributing factors confirmed: diabetes (primary), alcohol, B12 deficiency, CKD (uraemic neuropathy). Paraprotein excluded. Management: optimise glycaemic control (the only disease-modifying treatment); neuropathic pain management (pregabalin, duloxetine, amitriptyline); foot care; B12 supplementation.

prompts.label: Management
prompts.text: Glycaemic control intensified: basal-bolus insulin commenced, target HbA1c <53 mmol/mol. Metformin continued (dose reduced given eGFR 52). B12 supplementation commenced (oral cyanocobalamin 1 mg/day). Alcohol cessation programme referred. Neuropathic pain management: pregabalin 75 mg bd — pain score reduced 8→4. Foot care: podiatry referral, therapeutic footwear, daily foot inspection education. Diabetic foot MDT: debridement of plantar ulcers, total contact casting for Charcot. eGFR improving with BP optimisation.
prompts.reasoning: DSPN management: (1) The only disease-modifying treatment is improved glycaemic control — tight HbA1c target reduces DSPN progression by ~60% (DCCT/UKPDS data). (2) Address all contributing factors (B12, alcohol, renal function). (3) Neuropathic pain: first-line — pregabalin/gabapentin, duloxetine, amitriptyline. (4) Foot care is paramount — regular podiatry, daily inspection, appropriate footwear, and prompt management of ulcers to prevent amputation. Charcot foot requires offloading (total contact cast) and prolonged immobilisation. 15-year survival after first amputation is very poor — prevention is essential.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 32-year-old woman presents with a 4-month history of intermittent bilateral drooping of her eyelids, worse in the evenings. She also reports double vision when driving at night. Both symptoms are better in the morning.
prompts.reasoning: Fatigable ptosis (worsening with sustained upward gaze or at end of day) and diplopia that fluctuate and improve with rest are the hallmark presenting features of myasthenia gravis (MG). The diurnal variation — worse in evenings, better in mornings after rest — reflects the accumulative fatigue at the neuromuscular junction (NMJ) throughout the day. Ocular MG (isolated ocular symptoms) progresses to generalised MG in ~50% within 2 years. CN III palsy and Horner syndrome cause persistent (non-fatigable) ptosis.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: In the last month, she has developed difficulty chewing tough foods by the end of a meal (jaw fatigue) and her voice becomes nasal after prolonged speaking (fatigable dysarthria). She had one episode of difficulty swallowing a dry meal. She reports she can climb stairs normally in the morning but her legs feel heavy after walking further than 500 m. No pain. No sensory symptoms.
prompts.reasoning: The progression from ocular to bulbar involvement (fatigable jaw, nasal dysarthria, dysphagia) confirms generalised MG. Fatigable bulbar symptoms (worsening with repeated use) are characteristic. The absence of sensory symptoms distinguishes MG from neuropathy. Fatigable proximal limb weakness (heavy legs with prolonged exertion) reflects NMJ dysfunction. These symptoms are absent at rest (distinguishing from other motor disorders). Myasthenic crisis — respiratory failure from diaphragm fatigue — is a life-threatening complication and must be monitored for.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: History of autoimmune thyroiditis (Hashimoto's) diagnosed at 28 years — on levothyroxine. No other autoimmune conditions. Family history: sister has SLE. Non-smoker. No medications that worsen MG (no aminoglycosides, fluoroquinolones, statins, D-penicillamine currently). Pregnant 2 years ago — symptoms worsened significantly postpartum. Delivered a baby with transient neonatal myasthenia (resolved at 6 weeks).
prompts.reasoning: Myasthenia gravis is associated with other autoimmune conditions: thyroid disease (Hashimoto's, Graves'), SLE, rheumatoid arthritis — reflecting shared autoimmune diathesis. Neonatal myasthenia — transient weakness in a neonate born to a mother with MG — is caused by placental transfer of maternal anti-AChR IgG antibodies; it resolves spontaneously within 2–8 weeks as maternal antibodies are cleared. MG typically worsens during pregnancy (particularly postpartum) and with infections, surgery, and certain drugs (aminoglycosides, fluoroquinolones, beta-blockers — all impair NMJ transmission).

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Bilateral ptosis — left > right. Fatigable ptosis: sustained upward gaze for 60 seconds → complete left ptosis and worsening right ptosis. Ice pack test: bilateral ptosis improves immediately after 2 minutes of cold pack on eyelids (positive ice pack test). Variable diplopia on lateral gaze. Pupil reactions normal. Cogan's lid twitch sign positive (left eyelid twitches upward on return of gaze from downgaze to primary position). Mild fatigable proximal limb weakness (MRC 4/5 after 5 repetitions). Normal reflexes. No sensory deficit.
prompts.reasoning: Fatigable ptosis on sustained upgaze is the key bedside test for MG. The ice pack test is highly specific: cooling the eyelid improves ptosis (cold improves NMJ transmission by reducing acetylcholinesterase activity). Cogan's lid twitch sign (lid overshoots upward then drops — from refractory period fatigue of levator palpebrae) is pathognomonic of MG. Normal pupils exclude CN III palsy. Normal reflexes (preserved — distinguishing from GBS and LEMS, where reflexes are absent/depressed). LEMS: reflexes absent, facilitation (improved strength with repeated activity), autonomic features.

prompts.label: Serological & Neurophysiological Tests
prompts.text: Anti-acetylcholine receptor (AChR) antibodies: positive (titre 18 nmol/L — elevated; normal <0.4). Anti-MuSK antibodies: not tested (AChR positive — no need). CT chest: anterior mediastinal mass (3.2 cm) — thymoma confirmed on biopsy. Repetitive nerve stimulation (RNS) at 3 Hz: >10% decremental response in trapezius and deltoid — positive for NMJ transmission failure. Single-fibre EMG (SFEMG): increased jitter (>55 µs) in orbicularis oculi — most sensitive test (>95%) for MG.
prompts.reasoning: Anti-AChR antibodies: present in 85% of generalised MG, 50% of ocular MG. Thymoma is present in 10–15% of MG patients — all MG patients require CT chest to screen for thymoma. Thymoma-associated MG tends to be more severe. Thymectomy improves MG even in non-thymoma patients. SFEMG (increased jitter) is the most sensitive test (>95%) but non-specific. RNS decremental response (>10% decrement at 3 Hz) is the most practical neurophysiological test. Edrophonium (Tensilon) test: IV acetylcholinesterase inhibitor causes immediate transient improvement in ptosis — now rarely used due to cardiac side effects.

prompts.label: Management
prompts.text: Pyridostigmine (acetylcholinesterase inhibitor) commenced — 60 mg four times daily: significant symptom improvement (90% reduction in ptosis, improved voice and chewing). Prednisolone 60 mg/day commenced for immunosuppression. Thymectomy performed: complete resection of thymoma (pathology: type B1 thymoma). Azathioprine added as steroid-sparing agent. At 12 months: complete stable remission — pyridostigmine dose reduced. Myasthenic crisis protocol: crisis plan provided (avoid fluoroquinolones, aminoglycosides, neuromuscular blocking agents; monitor FVC). FVC measured: 2.8 L (normal).
prompts.reasoning: Management: (1) Symptomatic: pyridostigmine (inhibits AChE → increases synaptic ACh → compensates for reduced AChR); (2) Immunosuppression: prednisolone (first-line), azathioprine or mycophenolate (steroid-sparing); (3) Thymectomy: mandatory in thymoma; also beneficial in non-thymoma generalised MG (MGTX trial — improves remission rates); (4) Myasthenic crisis: IVIg or plasmapheresis for rapid improvement. Eculizumab (anti-C5 complement inhibitor) is approved for refractory anti-AChR positive generalised MG. MuSK-positive MG: responds less well to pyridostigmine — rituximab often used. Cholinergic crisis from pyridostigmine overdose: SLUDGE symptoms (salivation, lacrimation, urination, defaecation, GI cramps, emesis) + fasciculations.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 45-year-old woman presents with a 3-month history of progressive bilateral proximal muscle weakness — she struggles to rise from a chair, climb stairs, and raise her arms above her head. She has also noticed a violaceous discolouration of her upper eyelids and a rash on her hands.
prompts.reasoning: Progressive symmetrical proximal muscle weakness with characteristic skin involvement is the hallmark of dermatomyositis. The combination of proximal myopathy + violaceous heliotrope rash of the upper eyelids + Gottron's papules on the hands is specific for dermatomyositis. Polymyositis has the same muscle involvement without the skin changes. Inclusion body myositis typically affects older patients and causes distal + proximal weakness. Raised CK and inflammatory muscle biopsy are expected.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The purple discolouration of her eyelids (heliotrope rash) appeared 6 weeks ago. The rash on her hands involves the knuckles — raised, scaly, erythematous papules over the PIP and MCP joints. She has difficulty swallowing solid food (bulbar involvement — dysphagia). No diplopia, no ptosis, no exertional worsening (distinguishing from MG). She has a new dry cough and mild breathlessness on exertion.
prompts.reasoning: The combination of heliotrope rash (violaceous oedematous periorbital discolouration) and Gottron's papules (scaly erythematous papules overlying the bony prominences of the knuckles — PIP and MCP joints) is pathognomonic of dermatomyositis. Dysphagia occurs from oesophageal muscle involvement — a poor prognostic sign. The dry cough and exertional breathlessness suggest interstitial lung disease (ILD) — present in 30–40% of dermatomyositis, associated with anti-Jo-1 antibodies. ILD is a major cause of mortality in inflammatory myopathies.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: No prior inflammatory conditions. No family history of muscle disease. Recently found a right breast lump — biopsy pending. Non-smoker. No medications. No prior drug exposures (statins, colchicine — excluded as drug myopathies). Post-menopausal (age 51). BMI 24. Teacher by profession — struggling to write on the board.
prompts.reasoning: Dermatomyositis is a paraneoplastic syndrome in 15–25% of adult cases — the concurrent breast lump raises the critical concern for an underlying malignancy. All adult-onset dermatomyositis/polymyositis patients require cancer screening. The most common associated cancers: breast, ovarian, lung, colorectal, nasopharyngeal (especially in Asian populations). Age >40, male sex, rapidly progressive disease, and dysphagia increase the risk of underlying malignancy. Drug-induced myopathies (statins — raised CK + proximal weakness — most common drug cause) must be excluded.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Heliotrope rash: bilateral periorbital violaceous discolouration with periorbital oedema. Gottron's papules: erythematous scaly papules over bilateral MCP and PIP joints. V-sign (erythema on anterior chest and neck). Shawl sign (erythema on upper back, shoulders). Mechanic's hands (roughened, cracked skin on lateral fingers). Bilateral symmetric proximal weakness: shoulder abductors MRC 3/5, hip flexors MRC 3/5. Distal strength preserved. Absent myalgia. Normal reflexes. No fasiculations. Mild bilateral basal crepitations on chest auscultation.
prompts.reasoning: The multiple cutaneous features of dermatomyositis: heliotrope rash (periorbital), Gottron's papules (knuckle papules — pathognomonic), V-sign (anterior chest décolletage), shawl sign (upper back), mechanic's hands (lateral finger cracking — associated with anti-synthetase syndrome, anti-Jo-1 antibodies). Proximal symmetric weakness with preserved distal strength and normal reflexes (distinguishing from neuropathy — which affects reflexes). Preserved sensation distinguishes all myopathies from neuropathy. Basal crepitations suggest ILD.

prompts.label: Blood Tests & EMG
prompts.text: CK: 4,800 IU/L (markedly elevated — normal <200 IU/L). LDH elevated. AST/ALT elevated (from muscle, not liver). ESR: 88 mm/hr. CRP: 52 mg/L. Anti-Jo-1 antibodies: positive (myositis-specific antibody — associated with anti-synthetase syndrome: ILD + myositis + arthritis + mechanic's hands + Raynaud's). ANA: positive (1:640). EMG: myopathic changes — short duration, low amplitude polyphasic potentials, increased insertional activity, fibrillations at rest. Normal nerve conduction studies (NCS — distinguishes from neuropathy).
prompts.reasoning: Markedly elevated CK (from inflamed muscle) is the key biochemical marker — CK can reach 50× normal in active polymyositis/dermatomyositis. Anti-Jo-1 (anti-histidyl tRNA synthetase) antibodies identify the anti-synthetase syndrome — a clinically distinct subset with ILD, arthritis, Raynaud's, and mechanic's hands, carrying significant morbidity from ILD. EMG distinguishes myopathy from neuropathy: myopathic pattern (short, polyphasic, low amplitude motor unit potentials + spontaneous activity at rest). NCS is normal in myopathy (unlike GBS or peripheral neuropathy).

prompts.label: Muscle Biopsy & Malignancy Workup
prompts.text: Muscle biopsy (left vastus lateralis): perifascicular atrophy — myofibre necrosis and regeneration concentrated at the periphery of fascicles (pathognomonic of dermatomyositis). Perimysial and perivascular CD4+ T lymphocyte and B cell infiltrate. Complement (MAC) deposition on capillaries. HRCT chest: bilateral ground-glass opacities and reticulation (ILD — non-specific interstitial pneumonia pattern). Right breast core biopsy: invasive ductal carcinoma (ER+, HER2-). Diagnosis: paraneoplastic dermatomyositis with anti-synthetase syndrome and ILD.
prompts.reasoning: Perifascicular atrophy is the pathognomonic histological finding of dermatomyositis — necrosis and regeneration of peripheral fascicle fibres caused by ischaemia from complement-mediated small vessel vasculitis. CD4+ T cells and B cells (not CD8+ as in polymyositis) target intramuscular vessels. MAC (membrane attack complex) deposition on capillaries is the earliest histological change. In contrast, polymyositis shows endomysial CD8+ T cell infiltration without perifascicular pattern. ILD on HRCT (NSIP pattern) — managed with high-dose steroids and immunosuppression. Breast cancer treatment (surgery, chemotherapy) may improve the dermatomyositis.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 54-year-old man with known rheumatoid arthritis presents with a 3-week history of sudden-onset right foot drop followed 10 days later by left wrist drop. He describes these as completely separate events, each appearing acutely within hours.
prompts.reasoning: Acute, sequential, anatomically distinct peripheral nerve deficits — affecting the right peroneal nerve (foot drop) then the left radial nerve (wrist drop) — define mononeuritis multiplex (MM). Unlike symmetric polyneuropathy, MM affects named individual nerves non-contiguously. The temporal pattern (sudden onset, separate events) distinguishes it from a global peripheral neuropathy. The underlying aetiology is usually vasculitis (nerve infarction from occlusion of the vasa nervorum) — and rheumatoid arthritis is a well-recognised systemic vasculitis cause.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The right foot drop appeared acutely while walking and has not resolved. The left wrist drop appeared one morning after waking and has also persisted. Each deficit was preceded by 1–2 days of severe aching and burning pain in the affected limb. He has lost 6 kg over 3 months and reports fatigue and night sweats.
prompts.reasoning: Acute painful onset of a mononeuropathy — with pain preceding the weakness — is the hallmark of ischaemic nerve infarction (the pathological mechanism in vasculitic MM). The vasa nervorum (small nutrient vessels supplying peripheral nerves) are occluded by vasculitic inflammation, causing infarction of the nerve fascicles. Constitutional symptoms (weight loss, fatigue, night sweats) are 'B symptoms' pointing toward systemic vasculitis or an underlying malignancy driving MM.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: Rheumatoid arthritis for 12 years — currently on methotrexate and hydroxychloroquine (no anti-TNF therapy yet). No diabetes. No recent infection. Non-smoker. Alcohol: 8 units/week. No family history. No prior neuropathy.
prompts.reasoning: Rheumatoid arthritis is the most common connective tissue disease causing vasculitic mononeuritis multiplex. Other causes include: ANCA-associated vasculitis (PAN, GPA, eosinophilic GPA), SLE, cryoglobulinaemia (often hepatitis C-associated), sarcoidosis, leprosy, and diabetes mellitus. In diabetic MM (diabetic amyotrophy/Bruns-Garland syndrome), the femoral nerve or lumbosacral plexus is preferentially affected. Malignancy must be excluded as a paraneoplastic cause of MM.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Right foot: inability to dorsiflex or evert right foot (MRC 0/5); absent ankle jerk; reduced sensation dorsum of right foot (common peroneal nerve distribution). Left hand: wrist drop — wrist and finger extension power MRC 0/5; absent brachioradialis reflex; reduced sensation dorsum of 1st web space (radial nerve distribution). No fasciculations. All other limbs normal. Synovitic joints at bilateral MCPs (RA). Palpable purpura on both shins.
prompts.reasoning: Examination confirms two anatomically distinct peripheral nerve lesions: right common peroneal nerve (foot drop + dorsal foot sensory loss) and left radial nerve (wrist drop + 1st web space sensory loss). These are individual named peripheral nerves — not a radiculopathy or plexopathy. The pattern of isolated individual nerve deficits, each with motor and sensory loss in a named nerve distribution, is the hallmark of MM. Palpable purpura on the shins is a skin manifestation of small-vessel vasculitis — a key diagnostic clue.

prompts.label: Blood Tests & Nerve Conduction Studies
prompts.text: FBC: normocytic anaemia (Hb 102 g/L). CRP 84 mg/L. ESR 102 mm/hr. ANCA: positive (MPO-ANCA/p-ANCA titre 1:160). Cryoglobulins: negative. Hepatitis B/C serology: negative. RF: markedly elevated (RA-related). Nerve conduction studies: reduced CMAP amplitudes in right common peroneal and left radial nerve with normal conduction velocities — consistent with axonal mononeuropathies (nerve infarction).
prompts.reasoning: Elevated inflammatory markers with positive MPO-ANCA (p-ANCA) indicates ANCA-associated vasculitis overlapping with RA — rheumatoid vasculitis is typically ANCA-positive. NCS showing reduced CMAP amplitudes (axonal loss) with preserved conduction velocities confirms axonal mononeuropathies — the pattern of nerve infarction (ischaemic axonal death from vasa nervorum occlusion), as opposed to demyelinating neuropathy. Cryoglobulins and hepatitis serology exclude cryoglobulinaemic vasculitis from hepatitis C.

prompts.label: Nerve Biopsy & Management
prompts.text: Sural nerve biopsy: epineural vessel wall thickening, inflammatory infiltrate (predominantly CD4+ T cells and monocytes) in vessel walls, fibrinoid necrosis of arteriolar walls, axonal degeneration in nerve fascicles with wallerian degeneration — confirming vasculitic neuropathy. Treatment: IV methylprednisolone 1 g/day × 3 days, then oral prednisolone 60 mg/day. Cyclophosphamide added for ANCA-positive vasculitis. At 6 months: no new nerve lesions; partial recovery of right foot (MRC 3/5).
prompts.reasoning: Nerve biopsy is the gold standard for confirming vasculitic mononeuritis multiplex — histology shows inflammatory vessel wall destruction (fibrinoid necrosis) and resultant axonal infarction (wallerian degeneration in fascicles). The sural nerve (pure sensory, easily accessible at the ankle) is the standard biopsy site. Treatment: high-dose corticosteroids for acute vasculitic MM; cyclophosphamide for ANCA-associated or severe disease. Recovery is partial — axonal regeneration is slow (1 mm/day) and often incomplete. Ongoing management of RA with anti-TNF therapy requires careful monitoring for infections.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 35-year-old male presents with excruciating right shoulder and upper arm pain that began 5 days ago, severe enough to wake him from sleep. Today he noticed he is unable to lift his right arm and the pain is beginning to subside.
prompts.reasoning: The classic biphasic presentation of neuralgic amyotrophy (Parsonage-Turner syndrome) is intense, acute-onset, non-positional shoulder/upper arm pain followed by rapid onset of weakness as the pain subsides. This sequence — severe pain then weakness — is characteristic. Rotator cuff tear and dislocation are possible but would not explain the subsequent flaccid weakness. Cervical radiculopathy causes radicular pain that worsens with neck movement. The spontaneous onset without trauma narrows the differential.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: The pain was severe (10/10), constant, burning and aching, localised to the right shoulder and lateral arm — unaffected by arm position. It lasted 4 days then abruptly improved. He now cannot abduct his right arm at all and cannot flex the elbow fully. He had a flu-like illness 2 weeks ago. No neck pain. No sensory symptoms in the hand.
prompts.reasoning: The trigger of a preceding viral illness (immune activation) is present in 25–50% of neuralgic amyotrophy cases — supporting an immune-mediated pathogenesis (post-infectious or idiopathic autoimmune attack on brachial plexus fascicles). Inability to abduct (deltoid — axillary nerve, C5) and reduced elbow flexion (biceps — musculocutaneous nerve, C5/C6) with pain that is non-positional (unlike cervical root compression) localises to the upper brachial plexus. Absent sensory symptoms in the hand distinguishes upper plexus involvement from lower trunk lesions.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: No prior neurological conditions. No recent surgery or vaccination in the past 4 weeks. No family history of recurrent brachial plexus neuropathy (which would suggest hereditary neuralgic amyotrophy — SEPT9 mutation). No diabetes. Works as a physiotherapist — the weakness is significantly impacting his work.
prompts.reasoning: Triggers for neuralgic amyotrophy include: viral illness, vaccination (particularly influenza and COVID-19 vaccines), surgery, trauma, and childbirth — all linked to immune activation. Hereditary neuralgic amyotrophy (HNA) caused by SEPT9 (Septin-9) mutation presents with recurrent bilateral episodes and may have dysmorphic features (hypotelorism, epicanthal folds). The absence of family history and this being a first episode makes sporadic neuralgic amyotrophy more likely.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Right shoulder: deltoid wasting visible. Shoulder abduction: MRC 1/5 (right), 5/5 (left). Elbow flexion: MRC 3/5 (right). Elbow extension: MRC 5/5. Wrist and hand power: normal. Right biceps reflex: absent. Brachioradialis reflex: reduced. Sensation: mild reduced sensation over the right lateral arm (axillary nerve territory — regimental badge area) and lateral forearm. Passive shoulder range of motion: normal (excluding rotator cuff pathology).
prompts.reasoning: Examination confirms upper brachial plexus (C5–C6) involvement: deltoid wasting (axillary nerve), reduced shoulder abduction and elbow flexion (musculocutaneous nerve), absent biceps reflex (C5/C6 arc). Preserved wrist and hand function excludes lower plexus or cord involvement. Sensory loss over the lateral arm (axillary nerve cutaneous branch — regimental badge area) is the key sensory finding in axillary nerve involvement. Normal passive shoulder movement excludes structural joint pathology.

prompts.label: Nerve Conduction Studies & MRI
prompts.text: NCS/EMG: reduced CMAP amplitude of right axillary nerve (deltoid recording). EMG: acute denervation (fibrillations and positive sharp waves) in right deltoid, supraspinatus, and infraspinatus with normal cervical paraspinals (excluding cervical radiculopathy). Sensory nerve action potentials (SNAPs): reduced amplitude lateral antebrachial cutaneous nerve (musculocutaneous). MRI brachial plexus: hyperintense signal in the right upper trunk of the brachial plexus on T2 — no mass lesion, no cervical disc prolapse.
prompts.reasoning: EMG is the key investigation: denervation in multiple nerve territories (deltoid — axillary; supraspinatus/infraspinatus — suprascapular) with normal cervical paraspinals confirms brachial plexus involvement (not C5/C6 root — which would show paraspinal changes). Paraspinal muscles are supplied by the dorsal primary rami, which branch proximal to the plexus — their normality localises the lesion distal to the root. MRI plexus shows T2 hyperintensity from oedema/inflammation in the plexus fascicles — confirming neuralgic amyotrophy. Absence of mass lesion excludes malignant plexopathy.

prompts.label: Diagnosis & Management
prompts.text: Diagnosis: Parsonage-Turner syndrome (idiopathic neuralgic amyotrophy), upper brachial plexus pattern. Acute pain management: NSAIDs and short course oral prednisolone 60 mg/day × 2 weeks (reduces acute pain duration; not proven to improve motor recovery). Intensive physiotherapy commenced for shoulder stabilisation. At 3 months: deltoid power improved to MRC 3/5. At 12 months: MRC 4/5, returning to modified clinical work. Prognosis: 90% make substantial recovery by 3 years; complete recovery in 70–80%.
prompts.reasoning: Neuralgic amyotrophy is a self-limiting condition caused by immune-mediated inflammation of brachial plexus fascicles — likely a focal inflammatory mononeuritis. The triphasic course (severe pain → weakness onset as pain subsides → slow recovery over months) is characteristic. Steroids given early may reduce pain duration but do not clearly improve motor outcomes. Recovery depends on axonal regeneration (1 mm/day) — muscles close to the plexus (deltoid, biceps) recover faster than those distant (intrinsic hand muscles if lower plexus affected). Physiotherapy prevents adhesive capsulitis and maintains range of motion during recovery.

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 42-year-old woman presents with a 6-month history of numbness and tingling in both hands, predominantly affecting the thumb, index, middle, and radial half of the ring finger. The symptoms are worst at night and often wake her from sleep.
prompts.reasoning: Nocturnal paraesthesiae in the median nerve sensory distribution (thumb, index, middle, radial half of ring finger) is the classic presenting symptom of carpal tunnel syndrome (CTS) — the most common peripheral nerve entrapment neuropathy. Nocturnal worsening is characteristic because wrist flexion during sleep increases carpal tunnel pressure. Bilateral presentation is common, especially in the context of systemic predisposing conditions. Cervical radiculopathy (C6/C7) can mimic CTS but typically has neck pain and radicular radiation.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: She describes waking at 2–3 am with burning, tingling, and numbness in both hands — relieved by shaking or dangling her hands out of bed (the 'flick sign'). She has noticed difficulty doing up buttons (fine motor impairment). She works as a data entry clerk (repetitive keyboard use for 8+ hours/day). She is 30 weeks pregnant.
prompts.reasoning: The 'flick sign' (shaking the hand to relieve nocturnal paraesthesiae) is highly sensitive and specific for CTS. Fine motor impairment (difficulty with buttons) suggests early thenar weakness. CTS is common in pregnancy due to fluid retention causing increased carpal tunnel pressure — typically resolving postpartum. Repetitive wrist flexion-extension (keyboard use) is an occupational risk factor. The distribution (median nerve: thumb to radial ring finger) distinguishes CTS from ulnar neuropathy (little and ulnar ring finger).

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: Hypothyroidism (myxoedema — a cause of CTS via infiltration of the carpal tunnel with glycosaminoglycans) — on levothyroxine. Gestational diabetes this pregnancy. No rheumatoid arthritis. BMI 28. Previous pregnancy 3 years ago — had similar symptoms that resolved postpartum. No acromegaly symptoms. No amyloidosis.
prompts.reasoning: Multiple predisposing factors for CTS are present: hypothyroidism (accumulation of glycosaminoglycans in connective tissue increases tunnel volume), pregnancy (oedema and fluid retention), and repetitive hand use. Other causes: RA (synovial proliferation in the tunnel), DM, acromegaly (bony enlargement of the wrist), amyloidosis (infiltration), obesity, wrist fractures (post-traumatic). Recurrent CTS in a previous pregnancy and resolution postpartum strongly suggests the same aetiology here.

prompts.label: Physical Examination
prompts.text: Tinel's sign: percussion over the carpal tunnel at the wrist reproduces tingling in the right median nerve distribution (positive). Phalen's test: 60 seconds of maximum wrist flexion reproduces bilateral median distribution paraesthesiae (positive bilaterally). Thenar eminence: early wasting of the right abductor pollicis brevis (APB). Thumb opposition (APB): right MRC 4/5, left MRC 5/5. Sensation: reduced light touch over right thumb and index finger. Grip strength reduced (right). Cervical spine: full range of motion, no Spurling's test positive.
prompts.reasoning: Tinel's sign (tingling with percussion over the carpal tunnel — at the flexor retinaculum) and Phalen's test (wrist flexion for 60 seconds reproduces symptoms) are the key bedside tests for CTS. Thenar wasting (specifically APB — abductor pollicis brevis, the muscle most consistently supplied by the recurrent motor branch of the median nerve) indicates significant motor involvement. Normal cervical spine examination and negative Spurling's test exclude cervical radiculopathy. Right hand more severely affected — dominant hand with heavier keyboard use.

prompts.label: Nerve Conduction Studies
prompts.text: Nerve conduction studies: prolonged median nerve distal sensory latency (right 4.8 ms, left 4.2 ms — both >3.5 ms, normal). Median nerve SNAP amplitude: reduced bilaterally. Prolonged median nerve distal motor latency to APB (right 5.1 ms, left 4.4 ms — both >4.2 ms, normal). Conduction velocity of ulnar nerve: normal. Median-ulnar latency comparison (palmar stimulation): difference >0.3 ms — confirms CTS. Ultrasound of right wrist: cross-sectional area of median nerve at the tunnel inlet: 15 mm² (normal <10 mm²) — swollen nerve confirming entrapment.
prompts.reasoning: NCS is the diagnostic gold standard for CTS: prolonged median nerve distal sensory and motor latencies across the wrist, with reduced SNAP amplitudes. Normal ulnar nerve conduction excludes a generalised neuropathy. The median-ulnar sensory latency comparison (at the same palm stimulation distance) is the most sensitive NCS test for CTS. Ultrasound shows a swollen, flattened median nerve at the carpal tunnel inlet (cross-sectional area >12 mm² is diagnostic) — an increasingly used alternative or adjunct to NCS, particularly in pregnancy where NCS may not be needed.

prompts.label: Management
prompts.text: Given pregnancy: night splints (wrist in neutral position) commenced — 60% improvement in nocturnal symptoms. Corticosteroid injection into the carpal tunnel: deferred until postpartum given gestational diabetes. Levothyroxine dose reviewed — TSH mildly elevated; dose optimised. Postpartum (6 weeks after delivery): symptoms resolved bilaterally. At 3-month postpartum review: mild residual right-hand symptoms — corticosteroid injection performed; complete resolution. Surgical decompression (carpal tunnel release): planned if symptoms recur — divides the flexor retinaculum under local anaesthesia; >90% success rate.
prompts.reasoning: Management of CTS in pregnancy is conservative (splinting, steroid injection if needed) as most cases resolve postpartum. Night splints (maintain wrist in neutral position — minimises carpal tunnel pressure) are first-line for mild-moderate CTS. Corticosteroid injection into the carpal tunnel: effective short-term relief (avoid in diabetics/pregnancy where possible). Surgical carpal tunnel release (division of the flexor retinaculum): definitive treatment for moderate-severe or refractory CTS — performed under local anaesthesia as day surgery; >90% long-term success. Thenar wasting is an indication for early surgery (motor involvement).

prompts.label: Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: A 29-year-old male presents with numbness and tingling in the left little finger and ring finger for 3 weeks, along with weakness and difficulty spreading his fingers. He returned from a 10-day cycling holiday in the Alps 3 weeks ago.
prompts.reasoning: Sensory loss in the little and ring fingers with intrinsic hand weakness (difficulty spreading fingers) localises to the ulnar nerve — the 'cyclist's palsy' pattern. Prolonged handlebar pressure compresses the ulnar nerve at Guyon's canal (the wrist). This is distinct from carpal tunnel syndrome (which affects the thumb/index/middle finger — median nerve distribution). C8/T1 radiculopathy can cause similar sensory symptoms but would have associated cervical root signs and affect the medial forearm.

prompts.label: History of Presenting Complaint
prompts.text: During the 10-day cycling tour he spent 6–8 hours/day on his bicycle, gripping the handlebars. He noticed the tingling developing by day 5, affecting the left little and ulnar half of the ring finger. He now reports weakness when trying to grip objects firmly and difficulty with fine pinch (making the 'OK' sign). No neck pain, no elbow pain. No symptoms in the right hand.
prompts.reasoning: Prolonged handlebar compression of the hypothenar eminence causes ulnar nerve entrapment at Guyon's canal (wrist), producing purely distal ulnar neuropathy — affecting the deep motor branch (intrinsic hand muscles) and the superficial sensory branch (little and ulnar ring finger). The absence of elbow pain, medial forearm sensory loss, and normal flexor carpi ulnaris power (FCU is innervated proximal to Guyon's canal — above the wrist) localises the lesion to the wrist rather than the cubital tunnel at the elbow.

prompts.label: Past Medical & Social History
prompts.text: No prior neuropathy or hand problems. No diabetes, no hypothyroidism. Right-handed — the affected hand is his left (non-dominant). Works as an architect — detailed drawing is now difficult. No trauma to the elbow. No history of elbow fractures (cubitus valgus — tardy ulnar palsy). No recent weight loss or lymphadenopathy (excluding malignant infiltration of the ulnar nerve).
prompts.reasoning: Ulnar neuropathy has two common sites: (1) Cubital tunnel at the elbow (most common overall — compressed by leaning on elbow, post-fracture cubitus valgus, or tardy ulnar palsy after lateral condyle fracture in childhood); (2) Guyon's canal at the wrist (handlebar compression, occupational).
